title: DeepSlide: From Artifacts to Presentation Delivery
summary: Presentations are a primary medium for scholarly communication, yet most AI slide generators optimize the artifact (a visually plausible deck) while under-optimizing the delivery process (pacing, narrative, and presentation preparation). We present DeepSlide, a human-in-the-loop multi-agent system that supports preparing the full presentation process, from requirement elicitation and time-budgeted narrative planning, to evidence-grounded slide--script generation, attention augmentation, and rehearsal support. DeepSlide integrates (i) a controllable logical-chain planner with per-node time budgets, (ii) a lightweight content-tree retriever for grounding, (iii) Markov-style sequential rendering with style inheritance, and (iv) sandboxed execution with minimal repair to ensure renderability. We further introduce a dual-scoreboard benchmark that cleanly separates static artifact quality from dynamic delivery excellence. Across 20 domains and diverse audience profiles, DeepSlide matches strong baselines on artifact quality while consistently achieving larger gains on delivery metrics, improving narrative flow, pacing precision, and slide--script synergy with clearer attention guidance.

AI.tldr: DeepSlide is a human-in-the-loop multi-agent AI system that helps create not just slide decks but full presentations, optimizing both slides and delivery (narrative, pacing, rehearsal).
AI.motivation: Most existing AI slide generators focus on producing visually plausible slide decks but neglect the broader presentation process: understanding requirements, structuring the narrative, allocating time, grounding content in evidence, and supporting actual delivery and rehearsal. There is a need for tools that optimize how information is communicated over time, not just how slides look statically.
AI.method: The paper proposes DeepSlide, a multi-agent system that involves humans in the loop and supports end-to-end presentation preparation. Its core technical components are: (i) a controllable logical-chain planner with time budgets for each node to manage pacing; (ii) a lightweight content-tree retriever that grounds the presentation in source materials; (iii) Markov-style sequential rendering with style inheritance to produce coherent slides across the deck; and (iv) sandboxed execution with minimal repair mechanisms to ensure all generated slides render correctly. The authors also design a dual-scoreboard benchmark that separately evaluates static artifact quality (slides themselves) and dynamic delivery quality (narrative, pacing, slide–script coordination). They test DeepSlide over 20 domains and various audience profiles.
AI.result: Empirically, DeepSlide performs on par with strong baseline systems in terms of static artifact quality (how good the slides look and read). However, it significantly outperforms baselines on delivery-related metrics such as narrative flow, pacing precision against time budgets, and coherence between slides and speaker script, including clearer guidance on where to focus attention.
AI.conclusion: DeepSlide demonstrates that jointly modeling slides and delivery, with human-in-the-loop and multi-agent design, can substantially improve the overall quality of presentations. By explicitly planning narrative structure, time allocation, and grounding in evidence, and by evaluating both artifact and delivery, the system yields better-paced, more coherent, and more effective presentations without sacrificing slide quality.

title: SkillSmith: Compiling Agent Skills into Boundary-Guided Runtime Interfaces
summary: Recently, skills have been widely adopted in large language model (LLM)-based agent systems across various domains. In existing frameworks, skills are typically injected into the agent reasoning loop as contextual guidance once matched to a runtime task, enabling specialized task-solving capabilities. We find that this execution paradigm introduces two major sources of redundancy: irrelevant context injection and repeated skill-specific reasoning and planning. To this end, we propose SkillSmith, a boundary-first compiler-runtime framework that compiles skill packages offline into minimal executable interfaces. By extracting fine-grained operational boundaries from skills, SkillSmith enables agents to dynamically access and execute only the relevant components at runtime, thereby minimizing unnecessary context injection and redundant reasoning overhead. In the evaluation on SkillsBench benchmark, SkillSmith reduces solve-stage token usage by 57.44%, thinking iterations by 42.99%, solve time by 50.57% (2.02x faster), and token-proportional monetary cost by 57.44% compared with using raw-skills. Moreover, compiled artifacts produced by a stronger model can be reused by a smaller or more efficient runtime model, improving task accuracy in cases where raw skill interpretation fails. The source code and data are available at https://github.com/AetherHeart-AI/Aeloon.

AI.tldr: SkillSmith is a compiler-runtime framework that compiles LLM skills into minimal executable interfaces to cut redundant context and reasoning, significantly reducing tokens, time, and cost while improving or preserving task performance.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM agent systems use skills by injecting whole skill packages as context and re-reasoning over them each time. This leads to inefficiencies: irrelevant context is often added to the prompt, and the model repeatedly performs skill-specific reasoning and planning already implicit in the skills. The authors want to remove this redundancy to save tokens, time, and cost, while keeping or improving task accuracy and enabling reuse of stronger models’ skill processing by smaller runtime models.
AI.method: They introduce SkillSmith, a boundary-first compiler-runtime framework. Offline, it compiles raw skill packages into compact, minimal executable interfaces by extracting fine-grained operational boundaries within each skill. At runtime, the agent no longer loads entire skills as context; instead, it dynamically calls only the relevant compiled components via these interfaces, avoiding unnecessary context injection and duplicate planning. The framework supports cross-model reuse where compiled artifacts from a stronger model are executed by a smaller runtime model.
AI.result: On the SkillsBench benchmark, SkillSmith substantially reduces solve-stage resource usage versus using raw skills: 57.44% fewer tokens, 42.99% fewer thinking iterations, 50.57% shorter solve time (2.02x speedup), and 57.44% lower token-based monetary cost. It also improves task accuracy in scenarios where smaller or less capable models struggle to interpret raw skills but can instead rely on compiled artifacts from stronger models.
AI.conclusion: SkillSmith shows that treating skills via a compiler-runtime abstraction with fine-grained operational boundaries is more efficient than traditional context-based skill injection. It cuts redundant context and reasoning, lowering token, time, and cost overhead, and allows stronger models’ skill understanding to be distilled into artifacts usable by cheaper runtime models, thus enhancing both efficiency and robustness of LLM-based agent systems.

title: Always Learning, Always Mixing: Efficient and Simple Data Mixing All The Time
summary: Data mixing decides how to combine different sources or types of data and is a consequential problem throughout language model training. In pretraining, data composition is a key determinant of model quality; in continual learning and adaptation, it governs what is retained and acquired. Yet existing data mixing methods address only one phase of this lifecycle at a time: some require smaller proxy models tied to a single training phase, others assume a fixed domain set, and continual learning lacks principled guidance altogether. We argue that data mixing is fundamentally an online decision making problem -- one that recurs throughout training and demands a single, unified solution. We introduce OP-Mix (On-Policy Mix), a data mixing algorithm that operates across the entire language model training lifecycle. Our main insight is that candidate data mixtures can be cheaply simulated by interpolating between low-rank adapters trained directly on the current model, eliminating separate proxy models and ensuring the search is always grounded in the model's actual learning dynamics. Across pretraining, continual midtraining, and continual instruction tuning, OP-Mix consistently finds near-optimal mixtures while using a fraction of the compute of the baselines. In pretraining, OP-Mix improves upon training without mixing by 6.3% in average perplexity. For continual learning, OP-Mix matches the performance of both retraining and on-policy distillation while using 66% and 95% less overall compute, respectively. OP-Mix suggests a different view of language model training: not a sequence of distinct phases, but a single continuous process of learning from data.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes OP-Mix, a unified, online data mixing algorithm for language model training that works across pretraining and continual learning, achieving near-optimal mixtures with much lower compute cost than baselines.
AI.motivation: Data composition strongly influences language model quality in pretraining and what is learned or retained in continual learning, but current data mixing methods are fragmented, phase-specific, often require proxy models, fixed domains, or ad-hoc choices, with little principled guidance for continual learning.
AI.method: Formulate data mixing as an online decision-making problem across the full training lifecycle and introduce OP-Mix, which simulates candidate data mixtures cheaply by interpolating between low-rank adapters trained on the current model, thereby searching over mixtures on-policy without separate proxy models and adapting mixtures throughout pretraining, continual midtraining, and instruction tuning.
AI.result: Across pretraining, continual midtraining, and continual instruction tuning, OP-Mix consistently discovers near-optimal data mixtures while using only a fraction of the compute of baselines; specifically, in pretraining it improves average perplexity by 6.3% over no-mixing training, and in continual learning it matches the performance of retraining and on-policy distillation with 66% and 95% less compute, respectively.
AI.conclusion: Data mixing for language models should be treated as a unified, online decision problem spanning all training phases; OP-Mix operationalizes this by using adapter interpolation on the current model to efficiently identify good mixtures, yielding better or comparable performance to existing approaches at far lower compute cost and suggesting that language model training is best viewed as a single continuous data-driven process rather than disjoint stages.

title: Fair outputs, Biased Internals: Causal Potency and Asymmetry of Latent Bias in LLMs for High-Stakes Decisions
summary: Instruction-tuned language models exhibit behavioural fairness in high-stakes decisions while retaining biased associations in their internal representations. However, whether these suppressed representations can affect model outputs - and whether such causal potency is symmetric across demographic groups - remains unknown. We investigate the use of open-weight models for mortgage underwriting using matched applications that differ only in racially-associated names and reveal a critical disconnect: models show no output-level bias, yet retain and amplify demographic representations across model layers. Through activation steering and novel cross-layer interventions, we demonstrate that this suppressed information is decision-relevant: when reinjected at critical layers, it produces near-complete decision reversals. Critically, this latent bias is asymmetric - steering interventions affect decisions in one demographic direction, while producing minimal effects in reverse - and susceptible to adversarial prompt engineering and parameter-efficient fine-tuning. These findings demonstrate that behavioural audits focused on outputs are insufficient: fair outputs can mask exploitable internal biases. They also motivate dual-layer testing frameworks combining output evaluation with representational analysis for AI governance in high-stakes decisions.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that large language models can give fair-looking decisions (e.g., in mortgage approvals) while still internally encoding strong, exploitable racial information that can flip decisions when manipulated.
AI.motivation: To understand whether instruction-tuned language models that appear behaviorally fair in high-stakes tasks like mortgage underwriting still contain latent demographic biases in their internal representations, and whether these hidden biases can causally influence decisions in asymmetric ways across demographic groups.
AI.method: They use open-weight language models on paired mortgage applications that are identical except for racially-associated names. They probe internal representations across layers to measure demographic encoding and amplification. Then they apply activation steering and new cross-layer interventions to re-inject demographic information at selected layers and observe how decisions change. They further test robustness to adversarial prompts and parameter-efficient fine-tuning.
AI.result: The models’ outputs show little or no apparent racial bias in mortgage underwriting decisions, but internal activations strongly encode and even amplify racial information layer by layer. When demographic signals are steered or reinserted at specific layers, decisions nearly reverse, revealing that the suppressed information is decision-relevant. The causal influence is asymmetric: steering in one demographic direction strongly shifts outcomes, but the reverse has much weaker impact. These internal biases are also vulnerable to adversarial prompting and light fine-tuning, which can re-expose them at the output level.
AI.conclusion: Fair-looking outputs from instruction-tuned language models can conceal powerful, asymmetric internal demographic biases that remain causally relevant to high-stakes decisions and can be exploited through prompt engineering or fine-tuning. Therefore, relying solely on behavioral audits of outputs is inadequate for AI governance. The paper argues for dual-layer testing frameworks that combine traditional output-level fairness evaluations with systematic representational analyses of internal model states.

title: Fluency and Faithfulness in Human and Machine Literary Translation
summary: Literary translation requires balancing target-language fluency with faithfulness to the source. Recent large language models (LLMs) often produce fluent translations, but it remains unclear whether fluency corresponds to semantic preservation in literary text. We examine this relationship using 130,486 translated paragraphs from 106 novels in 16 source languages, including human, Google Translate, and TranslateGemma translations. Fluency is measured as original-likeness with a translationese classifier trained on paragraph part-of-speech n-grams, and faithfulness with the automatic translation evaluation metric COMET-KIWI. We control for paragraph length and find a consistent negative correlation between fluency and faithfulness. The pattern appears for both human and Google Translate, but is weaker and often non-significant for TranslateGemma. These results show that segment length matters for automatic evaluation and suggest a tradeoff between fluency and faithfulness in literary translation.

AI.tldr: The paper empirically studies whether more fluent literary translations are also more semantically faithful, finding a systematic tradeoff: higher fluency often coincides with lower faithfulness at the paragraph level.
AI.motivation: In literary translation, translators and systems must navigate a tension between producing text that reads naturally in the target language (fluency) and preserving the meaning, style, and nuances of the source (faithfulness). While large language models are praised for generating very fluent text, it is not well understood whether this fluency reflects, enhances, or harms semantic preservation in complex, stylistically rich literary texts. Existing evaluation work focuses largely on non-literary domains or shorter segments, and often assumes that better fluency aligns with better translation quality. The paper aims to rigorously test this assumption and characterize the fluency–faithfulness relationship in real literary data across many languages and translation modalities.
AI.method: The authors assemble a large corpus of 130,486 translated paragraphs drawn from 106 novels written in 16 different source languages. For each paragraph, they include translations from three sources: professional human translators, Google Translate, and TranslateGemma. They operationalize fluency as "original-likeness"—how similar a translated paragraph looks to native, non-translated text—using a translationese classifier trained on part-of-speech n-gram features at the paragraph level. Faithfulness is quantified with COMET-KIWI, a reference-free automatic evaluation metric that estimates semantic adequacy. They then analyze the correlation between the two scores while controlling for paragraph length, comparing patterns across translation sources and languages to see whether higher fluency is associated with higher or lower faithfulness.
AI.result: After controlling for paragraph length, they find a consistent negative correlation between fluency (original-likeness) and faithfulness (COMET-KIWI) across the dataset. That is, paragraphs that look more like original target-language text tend to be less semantically faithful to the source. This negative association holds for both human translations and Google Translate outputs. In contrast, for TranslateGemma the correlation is weaker and often not statistically significant, indicating a less pronounced or inconsistent tradeoff for that system. The analysis also shows that using longer segments (paragraphs) matters: evaluation behavior and correlations differ from what is commonly observed on shorter units like sentences.
AI.conclusion: The study provides empirical evidence that in literary translation there is often a tradeoff between target-language fluency and semantic faithfulness: making a translation read more like native text can come at the cost of accurately preserving the source content, at least as captured by automatic metrics. This tradeoff appears robust for human and Google Translate outputs but is less clear-cut for TranslateGemma. The findings highlight that segment length—here, paragraph-level analysis—significantly affects automatic evaluation outcomes and the observed relationships between quality dimensions. Overall, the paper cautions against assuming that greater fluency in literary translation automatically implies better faithfulness, and suggests that evaluation frameworks and system design should explicitly account for this tension.

title: CAX-Agent: A Lightweight Agent Harness for Reliable APDL Automation
summary: Large language models deployed for MAPDL finite-element simulation face practical reliability challenges: without structured execution control, tool encapsulation, and fault recovery, outputs may be inconsistent and task failures are common. The Agent Harness paradigm addresses this by inserting domain-specific orchestration middleware that manages tool lifecycles, workflow state, and recovery escalation. This paper presents the architecture of CAX-Agent, a lightweight agent harness purpose-built for MAPDL automation, and empirically evaluates one of its core components -- the recovery policy.CAX-Agent organizes execution into three layers -- LLM service, agent harness, and solver backend -- with a recovery ladder that escalates from deterministic rule patching through model-driven regeneration to context enrichment and human intervention. We evaluate three recovery strategies (no_recovery, rule_only, and model_only) on 50 standard structural benchmarks with three repeated runs per strategy (450 case-runs total). Two independent human raters score task completion under blind conditions; inter-rater agreement is strong (quadratic weighted Cohen's kappa = 0.84, 96 percent of score pairs within one point). Model_only achieves the best completion rate (0.9267), task score (3.59/4), total score (9.16/10), and zero-intervention rate (0.84), outperforming rule_only (0.7733, 3.17/4, 7.03/10, 0.00) and no_recovery (0.6933, 2.74/4, 5.60/10, 0.00) with large effect sizes (Cliff's delta = 0.81-0.87). The benchmark uses deliberately simple geometries to isolate recovery-policy effects; we discuss the scope of these findings and directions for broader validation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an agent-harness middleware (CAX-Agent) to make LLM-driven MAPDL simulations more reliable, and shows that model-based automatic recovery significantly improves task success compared with having no recovery or only rule-based fixes.
AI.motivation: When large language models are used to drive MAPDL finite-element simulations directly, they often fail or produce inconsistent results because there is no structured way to manage tools, workflows, or error recovery. Engineering users need reliable, low-intervention automation for standard structural analysis tasks, but current free-form LLM tool use is fragile in production. The authors aim to systematically improve reliability by introducing a domain-specific orchestration layer that can manage execution and recover from failures.
AI.method: They design the CAX-Agent architecture as middleware between the LLM and the MAPDL solver, organizing execution into three layers (LLM service, agent harness, solver backend). A key element is a multi-step "recovery ladder" that escalates from deterministic rule-based patches, to regenerating actions with the model, to enriching context, and finally to human intervention. They then empirically evaluate three recovery strategies (no recovery, rule-based only, and model-based only) on 50 standard structural-analysis benchmarks, each run three times under each strategy (450 total runs). Two human experts independently score task completion in a blinded fashion, and inter-rater agreement is quantified. Performance metrics and effect sizes are computed and compared across strategies.
AI.result: Inter-rater agreement on task scoring is strong (quadratic weighted Cohen's kappa of 0.84, with 96% of score pairs within one point), supporting the reliability of the evaluation. Among recovery strategies, the model-only approach performs best, with a completion rate of 0.9267, mean task score of 3.59/4, total score of 9.16/10, and zero-intervention rate of 0.84. These results substantially exceed those of rule-only recovery (0.7733 completion, 3.17/4, 7.03/10, 0.00 zero-intervention) and no-recovery (0.6933 completion, 2.74/4, 5.60/10, 0.00 zero-intervention). The performance advantages correspond to large effect sizes (Cliff's delta between 0.81 and 0.87).
AI.conclusion: CAX-Agent, a domain-specific agent harness for MAPDL, can markedly increase reliability of LLM-driven finite-element simulations, primarily through model-based recovery mechanisms that handle errors and guide execution without requiring human intervention in most cases. Rule-based fixes alone help but are clearly inferior to model-driven recovery. The presented benchmarks, while based on simple geometries, demonstrate substantial gains and motivate broader validation on more complex and realistic engineering tasks.

title: DiscoExplorer: An Open Interface for the Study of Multilingual Discourse Relations
summary: The relations connecting propositions in discourse such as cause (A because B) or concession (A although B) are a subject of intense interest in Computational Linguistics and Pragmatics, but challenging to study and compare across languages. Recent progress in standardizing discourse relation inventories across datasets offers the potential to facilitate such studies, but is hindered by the complexity of relevant data and the lack of easily accessible interfaces to analyze it. In this paper we present DiscoExplorer, a new open source web interface, capable of running on local computers, which we use to make datasets from the DISRPT Shared Task on discourse relation classification publicly available, covering 16 different languages. We present the query language, search and visualization facilities for relations and signaling devices such as connectives, as well as some example studies.

AI.tldr: Introduces DiscoExplorer, an open-source web interface to explore multilingual discourse-relation datasets from the DISRPT Shared Task.
AI.motivation: Discourse relations like cause or concession are important but hard to study and compare across languages due to complex, heterogeneous datasets and lack of user-friendly analysis tools, even though recent standardization efforts make cross-dataset research more feasible.
AI.method: Design and implementation of DiscoExplorer, a local, open-source web interface that loads DISRPT Shared Task datasets for 16 languages and offers a query language, search functions, and visualization tools focused on discourse relations and their signaling devices such as connectives; demonstrated via example analyses.
AI.result: A working publicly available tool (DiscoExplorer) plus the release of DISRPT discourse-relation datasets through this interface, with demonstrated capabilities for querying and visualizing relations and connectives across 16 languages.
AI.conclusion: A practical, open-source infrastructure is now available to easily explore standardized multilingual discourse-relation data, lowering the barrier to cross-linguistic and corpus-based research on discourse relations and their signals.

title: NOVA: Fundamental Limits of Knowledge Discovery Through AI
summary: Can AI systems discover genuinely new knowledge through iterative self improvement, and if so, at what cost? We introduce the NOVA framework, which models the common ``generate, verify, accumulate, retrain'' loop as an adaptive sampling process over a knowledge space. We identify sufficient conditions under which accumulated genuine knowledge eventually covers a finite domain, and show how their violations produce distinct failure modes: contamination, forgetting, exploration failure, and acceptance failure. We then analyze imperfect verification and identify a contamination trap: as easy-to-find knowledge is exhausted, the model mass assigned to new valid artifacts shrinks, so even small false-positive rates can cause invalid artifacts to enter the knowledge base faster than genuine discoveries. We clarify that Good--Turing estimation is a local batch-diversity diagnostic, not an estimator of the historically undiscovered valid mass that governs long-term discovery. Under a separate tail-equivalence assumption relating the model's effective discovery distribution to a Zipf law with exponent $α>1$, we prove that the cumulative generation cost required to obtain $D$ distinct genuine discoveries satisfies $R_{\mathrm{cum}}(D)=Θ(c_{\mathrm{gen}}D^α)$, where $c_{\mathrm{gen}}$ is the per-candidate generation cost. This scaling law quantifies asymptotic diminishing returns as the discovery frontier advances. Finally, we formalize human amplification through guidance, generation, and verification, explaining why expert input is most valuable near autonomous exploration barriers.

AI.tldr: The paper studies whether and how AI systems can keep discovering genuinely new knowledge via an iterative self-improvement loop, and what fundamental limits and failure modes arise.
AI.motivation: As AI systems are increasingly used to autonomously explore, hypothesize, and expand knowledge bases, we lack a rigorous understanding of when such self-improving discovery loops truly yield new, valid knowledge versus getting stuck, polluted, or inefficient. The authors want to formalize this process and characterize the conditions, risks, and costs of long-term autonomous discovery.
AI.method: The authors define the NOVA framework, modeling the typical “generate, verify, accumulate, retrain” loop as an adaptive sampling process over a formal knowledge space. They derive sufficient conditions that guarantee eventual coverage of a finite domain and then analytically study what happens when each condition is broken, yielding distinct failure modes (contamination, forgetting, exploration failure, acceptance failure). They extend the analysis to imperfect verification, formally identify a “contamination trap,” critique the use of Good–Turing estimation as a progress metric, and then assume a tail-equivalence relation between the model’s discovery distribution and a Zipf law to derive asymptotic cost scaling for obtaining many distinct genuine discoveries.
AI.result: They (1) provide conditions under which an iterative discovery loop will eventually cover all genuine knowledge in a finite domain; (2) classify failure modes when these conditions fail; (3) show that with imperfect verification, systems can enter a contamination trap where false positives accumulate faster than real discoveries as easy discoveries are exhausted; (4) formally clarify that Good–Turing estimates only local batch diversity and do not measure the remaining undiscovered valid mass; and (5) under a Zipf-like tail assumption, prove that the total cost of obtaining D distinct genuine discoveries scales as Θ(c_gen D^α), revealing strong diminishing returns.
AI.conclusion: Iterative self-improving AI systems can, in principle, discover all genuine knowledge in a finite domain, but only under stringent conditions on exploration, verification, and retention. Violations lead to systematic failure modes, especially contamination under imperfect verification. Discovery also faces fundamental diminishing returns: the marginal cost of new genuine findings grows polynomially with the number already obtained. Human experts are therefore most impactful when they help systems overcome exploration and verification barriers, particularly near the frontier where autonomous progress becomes inefficient or unreliable.

title: Automatic Construction of a Legal Citation Graph from 100 Million Ukrainian Court Decisions: Large-Scale Extraction, Topological Analysis, and Ontology-Driven Clustering
summary:
Half a billion citation edges extracted from 100.7 million Ukrainian court decisions reveal that judicial citation structure encodes legal domain boundaries without supervision and predicts future legislative importance with near-perfect accuracy. We construct the first large-scale citation graph from the complete EDRSR registry (99.5 million full texts, 1.1 TB), extracting 502 million citation links across six types via regex on commodity hardware in approximately 5 hours, with precision of 1.00 on a 200-decision validation sample (95% Wilson CI: [0.982, 1.000]).
  Three principal findings emerge. (1) The degree distribution follows a power law (alpha = 1.57 +/- 0.008), placing the Ukrainian court network near the EU Court of Justice and below the US Supreme Court, with hub articles cited by millions of decisions. (2) Louvain community detection on the co-citation projection recovers legal domain boundaries (civil, criminal, administrative, commercial) with modularity Q = 0.44-0.55 and temporal stability (NMI = 0.83-0.86 across periods), constituting an automatically constructed legal ontology grounded in judicial practice. (3) Citation features predict top-1000 articles with AUC = 0.9984, substantially outperforming a naive frequency baseline (P@1000 = 0.655); temporal dynamics detect legislative regime changes as phase transitions and the 2022 invasion as a citation entropy spike (H: 11.02 -> 13.49) with emergent wartime legislation nodes.
  The citation-derived ontology is operationalized as the domain layer of a workflow memory system for LLM-assisted legal analysis, connecting to the ontology-controlled paradigm. The extraction pipeline, analysis code, and aggregated statistics are released as open data.

AI.tldr: The paper builds and analyzes a massive citation graph from nearly all Ukrainian court decisions, showing that judicial citation patterns reveal legal domains, predict which laws become important, and react sharply to major events, and then uses this structure as an ontology layer for LLM-based legal tools.
AI.motivation: There is no large-scale, machine-readable map of how Ukrainian courts use legislation over time, making it hard to (1) understand the structure and evolution of the legal system, (2) automatically discover legal domains and ontologies from practice, and (3) build effective AI tools that respect real-world legal boundaries. The authors aim to fill this gap by constructing a full national citation graph and showing its analytical and practical value.
AI.method: The authors parse 99.5M full-text Ukrainian court decisions from the EDRSR registry using regular expressions to extract 502M citation edges across six citation types on commodity hardware. They validate extraction precision on a hand-checked sample. They then: (1) analyze the degree distribution of the resulting legislation–decision graph; (2) build a co-citation projection of legal articles and apply Louvain community detection over time to see whether communities align with known legal domains and remain stable; (3) construct citation-based features to predict which legal articles will become highly cited (top-1000) and evaluate against a frequency baseline; (4) analyze temporal dynamics of citation entropy to identify regime changes and wartime effects. Finally, they integrate the induced ontology as a domain layer in a workflow memory system for LLM-supported legal analysis.
AI.result: (1) The citation degree distribution follows a power law with exponent ~1.57, similar to other high-level courts, with some legal articles serving as massive hubs. (2) Louvain communities on the co-citation graph closely match standard legal domains (civil, criminal, administrative, commercial) with high modularity (0.44–0.55) and strong temporal stability (NMI 0.83–0.86), effectively yielding a data-driven legal ontology. (3) Citation-based features almost perfectly predict which legal articles will become top-1000 by future importance (AUC 0.9984), far outperforming a naive frequency-based baseline (P@1000 0.655). (4) Temporal analysis of the network detects legislative regime changes as phase transitions and shows that the 2022 invasion manifests as a sudden spike in citation entropy (from 11.02 to 13.49) and the appearance of new wartime legislation hubs. They publish their pipeline, code, and aggregate stats as open data.
AI.conclusion: Judicial citation behavior in Ukraine encodes a rich, stable, and highly predictive structure that (a) naturally recovers functional legal domains, (b) forecasts which legislative articles will become central, and (c) surfaces systemic shocks such as wars and regime changes. This emergent ontology can be operationalized as a domain layer in LLM-based legal workflow systems, supporting more structured and practice-grounded AI assistance. The released open data and tools provide a reusable infrastructure for empirical legal studies and legal-tech applications.

title: ICRL: Learning to Internalize Self-Critique with Reinforcement Learning
summary: Large language model-based agents make mistakes, yet critique can often guide the same model toward correct behavior. However, when critique is removed, the model may fail again on the same query, indicating that it has not internalized the critique's guidance into its underlying capability. Meanwhile, a frozen critic cannot improve its feedback quality over time, limiting the potential for iterative self-improvement. To address this, we propose learning to internalize self-critique with reinforcement learning(ICRL), a novel framework that jointly trains a solver and a critic from a shared backbone to convert critique-induced success into unassisted solver ability. The critic is rewarded based on the solver's subsequent performance gain, incentivizing actionable feedback. To address the distribution shift between critique-conditioned and critique-free behavior, ICRL introduces a distribution-calibration re-weighting ratio that selectively transfers critique-guided improvements compatible with the solver's own prompt distribution. Additionally, a role-wise group advantage estimation stabilizes joint optimization across the two roles. Together, these mechanisms ensure that the solver learns to improve itself without external critique, rather than becoming dependent on critique-conditioned behavior. We evaluate ICRL on diverse benchmarks spanning agentic and mathematical reasoning tasks, using Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-8B as backbones. Results show consistent improvements, with average gains of 6.4 points over GRPO on agentic tasks, and 7.0 points on mathematical reasoning. Notably, the learned 8B critic is comparable to 32B critics while using substantially fewer tokens. The code is available at https://github.com/brick-pid/ICRL.

AI.tldr: They propose ICRL, a reinforcement learning framework that turns self-critique successes into lasting, critique-free improvements for language-model agents.
AI.motivation: LLM agents often improve when given critiques on their own outputs, but this improvement does not persist once the critique is removed, meaning the model has not internalized the feedback. Also, using a frozen critic prevents the feedback from getting better over time, limiting self-improvement. The authors want a way for models to learn from self-critique so that (1) the solver gets better even without critique at test time and (2) the critic itself can improve, enabling scalable, iterative self-improvement.
AI.method: They introduce ICRL, a reinforcement learning framework that jointly trains a solver and a critic from one shared backbone. The critic produces feedback on the solver’s behavior and is rewarded according to the solver’s subsequent performance gain, which encourages feedback that is concretely helpful. To bridge the gap between critique-conditioned and critique-free behavior distributions, they use a distribution-calibration re-weighting ratio that selectively transfers only those improvements that are compatible with the solver’s own prompt distribution without critique. They further propose a role-wise group advantage estimation method to stabilize joint optimization of solver and critic roles during training.
AI.result: On a variety of benchmarks covering agentic tasks and mathematical reasoning, using Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-8B as base models, ICRL yields consistent improvements over the GRPO baseline: on average +6.4 points on agentic tasks and +7.0 points on math reasoning tasks. The 8B critic trained with ICRL performs comparably to much larger 32B critics while using significantly fewer tokens.
AI.conclusion: ICRL effectively converts critique-guided success into lasting, critique-free capabilities in LLM agents. By jointly training a solver and critic with performance-gain-based rewards, distribution calibration, and role-wise advantage estimation, the approach reduces dependence on explicit critique while enabling an efficient critic whose quality rivals much larger models.

title: Greedy or not, here I come: Language production under vocabulary constraints in humans and resource-rational models
summary: Communicating using only a limited vocabulary is a common but challenging cognitive phenomenon, requiring an ideal communicator to plan carefully to optimize for intelligibility while circumventing a constrained lexicon. In this work, we investigate how humans respond to a broad array of questions under variable vocabulary limitations, consisting of only 250 highly frequent words at the most restrictive. We provide theoretically motivated comparisons to greedy and globally optimal sampling algorithms using Sequential Monte Carlo inference with large language models. Humans generally resemble greedy sampling more than globally optimal sampling, though more skilled humans are more likely to backtrack and revise -- a non-greedy behavior. An observed human pattern of leaning on semantically light words in high-constraint settings falls out of both greedy and globally optimal sampling. We discuss the results and their broader implications for resource-rational cognition, psycholinguistics, L2 communication, and language impairments.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how humans communicate when forced to use a very small vocabulary and compares their behavior to different language-model-based sampling algorithms.
AI.motivation: In many real-world situations—such as second-language use, language impairments, or constrained communication games—speakers must express complex ideas using only a limited set of words. Understanding how people plan and produce language under these constraints can illuminate general principles of cognition and communication efficiency, and can be compared to formal algorithmic strategies used in language models.
AI.method: Participants answered a wide variety of questions while restricted to vocabularies of varying size, with the smallest containing only 250 very frequent words. Their responses were then compared to outputs from large language models configured to generate under similar lexicon constraints. The authors implemented theoretically motivated baselines using greedy sampling and globally optimal sampling via Sequential Monte Carlo inference, and analyzed how human behavior aligns with or diverges from these algorithms.
AI.result: Human responses were more similar overall to greedy sampling than to globally optimal Sequential Monte Carlo sampling. However, higher-performing participants showed more backtracking and revision behavior, which is characteristic of non-greedy search. Additionally, in highly constrained vocabulary settings, both humans and algorithms tended to rely more heavily on semantically light, high-frequency words.
AI.conclusion: Humans under lexical constraints tend to use locally myopic, greedy-like strategies, but skilled communicators incorporate limited non-greedy revisions, suggesting a resource-rational compromise between optimal planning and cognitive costs. The convergence of humans and algorithms on the use of semantically light words under constraints supports theories in psycholinguistics about how people adapt their language use. The findings have implications for understanding resource-rational cognition, second-language communication strategies, and language production in individuals with lexical access limitations.

title: NIMO Controller: a self-driving laboratory orchestrator based on the Model Context Protocol
summary: Self-driving laboratories (SDLs) have attracted increasing attention as a means of accelerating scientific discovery; however, developing SDL software remains technically demanding. To improve accessibility, orchestration software frameworks have been proposed to coordinate SDL components. Nevertheless, existing frameworks are primarily designed for human interaction and do not provide standardized interfaces suitable for AI agents. In this work, we propose an SDL software architecture based on the Model Context Protocol (MCP), in which all SDL functionalities are exposed through MCP servers. Following this design principle, we introduce an MCP-based SDL orchestrator, named NIMO Controller. It provides a visual programming interface automatically generated through MCP-based tool discovery, allowing human users to design experimental workflows without writing code. The same MCP backend can also be accessed by AI agents, providing a unified interface for both human users and AI agents. We demonstrate the proposed system through a case study on a color-matching SDL. The results validate the usability of the proposed MCP-based SDL architecture.

AI.tldr: They design an AI- and human-friendly software architecture for self-driving labs using the Model Context Protocol, and demonstrate it on a color-matching experiment.
AI.motivation: Self-driving labs promise to accelerate scientific discovery, but existing orchestration software is hard to develop and mainly tailored to human users, lacking standardized interfaces that AI agents can use directly.
AI.method: They architect an SDL where all functionalities are exposed as MCP servers, then implement an MCP-based orchestrator (NIMO Controller) that auto-discovers MCP tools to build a visual programming interface for experimental workflows, accessible both to humans and AI agents. They validate it on a color-matching SDL case study.
AI.result: They successfully built NIMO Controller with an MCP backend that supports both a no-code visual workflow builder for humans and a unified interface for AI agents, and they show it working in a color-matching self-driving lab scenario.
AI.conclusion: An MCP-centric architecture can standardize how SDL components are exposed to both humans and AI agents, improving usability and accessibility; the case study indicates this design is practical and effective for real SDL applications.

title: Adesua: Development and Feasibility Study of an AI WhatsApp Bot for Science Learning in West Africa
comment: 11 pages. Accepted at the 27th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Education (AIED 2026)
summary: Sub-Saharan Africa faces persistently high student-teacher ratios and shortages of qualified teachers, limiting students' access to personalized learning support and formative assessment. To address this challenge, we present Adesua, a WhatsApp-based AI Teaching Assistant for science education that extends the Kwame for Science platform. Adesua leverages WhatsApp's widespread adoption in Africa to provide accessible, curriculum-aligned learning support for Junior High School (JHS) and Senior High School (SHS) students across West Africa. The system integrates curated textbooks and 33 years of national examination questions with generative AI to enable conversational question answering and automated assessment with feedback via a WhatsApp bot. Students can ask science questions, take timed or untimed multiple-choice tests by topic or exam year, and receive instant grading and detailed explanations of correct and incorrect responses. A 6-month feasibility deployment in 2025 had 56 active users in Ghana, including students and parents. Quantitative evaluation showed a high perceived usefulness, with a helpfulness score of 93.75\% for AI-generated answers, albeit with a small number of ratings (n=16). These preliminary results provide a basis for more extensive future evaluation of a WhatsApp-based AI assistant to assess its potential to offer scalable, low-cost personalized learning support and formative assessment in resource-constrained educational contexts.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Adesua, a WhatsApp-based AI teaching assistant designed to provide accessible, personalized science education and formative assessment for secondary students in West Africa, showing initial feasibility and perceived usefulness in a small Ghanaian pilot.
AI.motivation: Sub-Saharan Africa suffers from high student-teacher ratios and a shortage of qualified teachers, which constrains personalized learning and timely formative assessment. Many students lack access to quality, curriculum-aligned support outside the classroom, yet WhatsApp is widely used and could serve as a low-cost, familiar delivery channel. The paper aims to explore whether an AI-driven assistant on WhatsApp can help close this support gap at scale.
AI.method: The authors extended the existing Kwame for Science platform to create Adesua, a WhatsApp bot that integrates curated textbooks and 33 years of national exam questions with generative AI. The system supports conversational Q&A and automated multiple-choice testing (timed/untimed, by topic or exam year) with instant grading and explanatory feedback. They conducted a 6‑month feasibility deployment in Ghana in 2025 with 56 active users (students and parents), collecting basic usage data and user ratings of answer helpfulness.
AI.result: During the 6‑month deployment, 56 users actively engaged with Adesua. Quantitative feedback on AI-generated responses indicated high perceived usefulness, with a reported helpfulness score of 93.75%, although based on a small sample of 16 ratings. The paper primarily reports feasibility and user perception rather than learning gains or long-term outcomes.
AI.conclusion: Adesua appears technically feasible and acceptable to users as a WhatsApp-based AI teaching assistant for science education in a resource-constrained context, with early evidence of high perceived helpfulness. However, the current findings are preliminary and based on a small-scale pilot. The authors conclude that more extensive future evaluations are needed to rigorously assess its effectiveness, scalability, and impact on learning outcomes across broader populations in Sub-Saharan Africa.

title: Verifiable Agentic Infrastructure: Proof-Derived Authorization for Sovereign AI Systems
summary:
Modern cloud and enterprise systems rely on identity-centric authorization, assuming that callers possessing valid credentials are safe to execute commands. The emergence of autonomous AI agents invalidates this assumption: agents can generate syntactically valid but semantically unsafe actions, making standing privileges a significant operational risk. This risk becomes especially acute in sovereign AI systems, where autonomous agents may interact with cloud infrastructure, regulated data, financial workflows, and national-scale digital services. Governed mutation substrates reduce this risk by interposing on agent actions: agents submit intents, infrastructure evaluates context and policy, and execution is mediated. However, this shifts the trust boundary: how can the decision to authorize an intent be made verifiable, distributed, and replayable?
  We introduce a Distributed Trust Framework (DTF), a verification framework for governed mutation systems that computes execution authority from structured, verifiable artifacts. DTF introduces a Justification Proof to encode the admissibility basis of an action, a consensus model for independent evaluation, an ephemeral Execution Identity derived from the approved proof, and an append-only Evidence Chain that preserves the authorization lifecycle. Under stated substrate assumptions, this architecture enforces a compact authorization invariant: no high-stakes execution without a proof object, no derived authority without consensus, and no valid mutation detached from evidence.
  We define the model, instantiate it over an OpenKedge-based governed mutation substrate, and show how it maps onto cloud-native environments. By shifting authorization from standing identity to proof-derived authority, DTF provides an infrastructure foundation for making agentic execution governable, auditable, and bounded in sovereign AI deployments.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Distributed Trust Framework (DTF) that replaces standing identity-based privileges with proof-based, consensus-validated authority for autonomous AI agents acting on critical infrastructure.
AI.motivation: Identity-centric authorization assumes that any actor with valid credentials is safe, which breaks down with autonomous AI agents that can issue semantically unsafe but syntactically valid commands. This is especially dangerous in "sovereign AI" settings involving critical infrastructure, finance, regulated data, or national services. Governed mutation systems can mediate agent actions, but they shift the trust problem to the decision process itself: how to ensure that authorization decisions are verifiable, distributed, and auditable.
AI.method: The authors design the Distributed Trust Framework (DTF) as a verification layer for governed mutation substrates. DTF formalizes four key elements: (1) Justification Proofs that capture the admissibility rationale for each action; (2) a consensus model where independent evaluators validate these proofs; (3) ephemeral Execution Identities derived from approved proofs rather than long-lived credentials; and (4) an append-only Evidence Chain that records the full authorization lifecycle. They instantiate this model on an OpenKedge-based governed mutation substrate and map it onto cloud-native environments.
AI.result: The result is an architecture that enforces a strong authorization invariant: no high-stakes execution can occur without an associated proof object; no authority can be derived without reaching consensus on the proof; and no valid system mutation can exist without a corresponding evidence record. The authors demonstrate that DTF can be realized on existing governed mutation infrastructure (OpenKedge) and integrated with contemporary cloud-native environments.
AI.conclusion: By shifting from identity-based standing privileges to proof-derived, consensus-validated, and fully recorded authority, DTF offers a foundation for making autonomous agent execution governable, auditable, and constrained in sovereign AI deployments. This improves safety and accountability for high-stakes AI-driven operations over critical and cloud-native infrastructures.

title: Eskwai for Students: Generative AI Assistant for Legal Education in Ghana
comment: 10 pages. Accepted at the 27th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Education (AIED 2026)
summary: Recent advances in generative AI have shown their potential to be leveraged for legal education. Yet, work on the development and deployment of such systems for legal education in the Global South is limited. In this work, we developed Eskwai for Students, a generative AI assistant to help law students with their legal education. Eskwai for Students is a retrieval augmented generation (RAG) system that provides answers to a wide range of legal questions for law students grounded in a curated database of over 12K case laws and 1.4K legislation in Ghana. We deployed Eskwai for Students in a longitudinal study of 30 months (2.5 years) used by 3.1K law students in Ghana who made 32K queries. We evaluated the helpfulness of our AI, and provided insight into the kinds of queries law students submit to this generative AI tool, which raises some ethical concerns. This work contributes to an understanding of how law students in the Global South are using generative AI for their studies and the ways it could be leveraged responsibly to advance legal education.

AI.tldr: The paper presents and evaluates Eskwai for Students, a RAG-based generative AI legal assistant deployed for Ghanaian law students over 2.5 years.
AI.motivation: There is growing interest in using generative AI for legal education, but existing systems and research are largely centered on the Global North. There is a gap in tools tailored to the legal context, curricula, and resources of the Global South, particularly with locally relevant case law and legislation. The authors aim to fill this gap and understand how law students in Ghana actually use such tools and what opportunities and risks arise.
AI.method: The authors design and implement Eskwai for Students, a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system that answers legal questions using a curated corpus of over 12,000 case law documents and 1,400 pieces of legislation from Ghana. They then deploy the system in the wild for 30 months, during which 3,100 law students submit 32,000 queries. They evaluate the system’s helpfulness and analyze the query logs qualitatively and/or quantitatively to identify usage patterns and ethical concerns.
AI.result: Eskwai for Students attracted substantial usage: 3.1K students submitted 32K queries over 2.5 years. The system was able to provide grounded answers to a wide range of legal questions based on Ghanaian legal sources. The evaluation shows that students found the AI assistant helpful, and the query analysis reveals how students integrate generative AI into their legal studies. It also uncovers problematic or ethically concerning uses reflected in some of the queries.
AI.conclusion: The study shows that a domain-specific, locally grounded RAG-based generative AI system can support legal education in the Global South at scale. It highlights both the educational benefits and emerging ethical issues in how students use such tools. The authors argue that responsible design, deployment, and policy guidance are needed to leverage generative AI to advance legal education while mitigating risks, particularly in under-studied Global South contexts.

title: Solvita: Enhancing Large Language Models for Competitive Programming via Agentic Evolution
summary: Large language models (LLMs) still struggle with the rigorous reasoning demands of hard competitive programming. While recent multi-agent frameworks attempt to bridge this reliability gap, they remain fundamentally stateless: they rely on static retrieval and discard the valuable problem-solving and debugging experience gained from previous tasks. To address this, we present Solvita, an agentic evolution framework that enables continuous learning without requiring weight updates to the underlying LLM. Solvita reorganizes problem-solving into a closed-loop system of strategy selection, program synthesis, certified supervision, and targeted hacking, executed by four specialized agents: Planner, Solver, Oracle, and Hacker. Crucially, each agent is paired with a trainable, graph-structured knowledge network. As the system operates, outcome signals, such as pass/fail verdicts, test certification quality, and adversarial vulnerabilities discovered by the Hacker, are recast as reinforcement learning updates to these network weights. This allows the agents to dynamically route future queries based on past successes and failures, effectively accumulating transferable reasoning experience over time. Evaluated across CodeContests, APPS, AetherCode, and live Codeforces rounds, Solvita establishes a new state-of-the-art among code-generation agents, outperforming existing multi-agent pipelines and nearly doubling the accuracy of single-pass baselines.

AI.tldr: Solvita is a multi-agent framework that lets LLM coding agents continually improve at competitive programming without updating model weights, by learning in a graph-structured memory using reinforcement signals from past problem-solving attempts.
AI.motivation: Existing large language models and multi-agent systems perform poorly on hard competitive programming because they lack robust, rigorous reasoning and are essentially stateless: they do not effectively reuse problem-solving experience across tasks. The authors want a way for code-generation agents to accumulate and exploit past successes, failures, and debugging insights, without the cost and complexity of fine-tuning model weights.
AI.method: They design Solvita, an agentic evolution framework with four specialized LLM-based agents—Planner, Solver, Oracle, and Hacker—organized in a closed-loop process: planning strategies, synthesizing code, certifying solutions with tests, and performing adversarial hacking. Each agent is connected to a trainable, graph-structured knowledge network. During operation, outcome signals (e.g., pass/fail, test quality, vulnerabilities found) are converted into reinforcement learning updates on the graph network, which controls how future queries are routed and what prior experience is retrieved. This lets the system adapt strategy and retrieval based on historical performance while keeping the base LLM frozen.
AI.result: On multiple competitive programming benchmarks—CodeContests, APPS, AetherCode, and live Codeforces rounds—Solvita achieves state-of-the-art performance among code-generation agents. It clearly outperforms existing multi-agent pipelines and nearly doubles the accuracy of strong single-pass LLM baselines on these tasks.
AI.conclusion: Continuous, reinforcement-driven adaptation in a graph-structured knowledge network can substantially improve LLM performance on difficult programming tasks, even without updating model weights. By closing the loop between planning, solving, verifying, and attacking solutions, and by retaining and routing through accumulated experience, Solvita delivers a new state-of-the-art for agentic code generation in competitive programming settings.

title: Capability Conditioned Scaffolding for Professional Human LLM Collaboration
summary: Large language model personalization typically adapts outputs to user preferences and style but does not account for differences in user evaluation capacity across domains of expertise. This limitation can encourage Professional Domain Drift, where users rely on AI generated reasoning in domains they cannot reliably evaluate. We introduce Capability Conditioned Scaffolding, a typed framework that partitions expertise into strong, mixed, and weak domains and conditions intervention behavior on structured capability profiles. A pilot evaluation across multiple MMLU subsets and four LLM substrates shows consistent profile conditioned intervention behavior, including categorical inversion under profile swapping and selective activation in mixed domain risk zones. These findings suggest that capability aware scaffolding can support more reliable professional human AI collaboration beyond stylistic personalization.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a framework that tailors LLM assistance not just to user preferences but to their domain-specific expertise, to avoid users over‑relying on AI in areas they can’t properly judge.
AI.motivation: Current personalization of large language models focuses on style and preferences, ignoring that users differ in their ability to evaluate AI outputs across domains. This can cause professionals to unknowingly depend on AI reasoning in domains where they are weak, a phenomenon the authors term Professional Domain Drift. The motivation is to design interaction patterns that are sensitive to a user’s domain expertise so that AI support becomes safer and more reliable in professional contexts.
AI.method: The authors introduce Capability Conditioned Scaffolding, a typed interaction framework that segments user expertise into strong, mixed, and weak domains via a structured capability profile. The framework conditions how the AI intervenes—how much reasoning it shows, what kind of guidance it gives, when it defers or warns—on these domain labels. They then run a pilot evaluation using several MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding) subsets and four different underlying LLMs to test whether the system produces consistent, profile‑dependent behavior such as changing its stance when expertise profiles are swapped and activating more cautious behavior in medium‑risk domains.
AI.result: The experiments demonstrate that the scaffolding mechanism reliably changes AI behavior according to the specified capability profile. When user profiles are swapped, the model’s behavior in corresponding domains inverts as expected (e.g., more guidance for weak domains, more deference in strong ones), and in mixed‑expertise domains the system selectively activates more cautious or supportive behaviors. These effects are observed consistently across multiple MMLU subsets and four different LLMs, indicating the approach is not tied to a single model.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that capability‑aware scaffolding—conditioning AI interaction patterns on structured user expertise profiles—can more effectively manage risk and support trustworthy human‑AI collaboration than traditional stylistic personalization alone. By explicitly accounting for where users are strong or weak, such frameworks can help mitigate Professional Domain Drift and lead to safer deployment of LLMs in professional settings.

title: Neural Activation Patterns Across Language Model Architectures: A Comprehensive Analysis of Cognitive Task Performance
summary: This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of neural activation patterns across six distinct large language model (LLM) architectures, examining their performance on twelve cognitive task categories. Through systematic measurement of final activation values, attention entropy, and sparsity patterns, we reveal fundamental differences in how encoder and decoder architectures process diverse cognitive tasks. Our analysis of 144 task-model combinations demonstrates that mathematical reasoning consistently produces the highest attention entropy across all architectures, while decoder models exhibit significantly higher sparsity patterns compared to encoder models. The findings provide critical insights into the computational characteristics of modern language models and their task-specific neural behaviors, with implications for model selection and optimization in big data applications.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes neural activation patterns in six different large language model architectures over twelve cognitive task types, revealing systematic differences between encoder and decoder models that affect attention entropy, sparsity, and task processing.
AI.motivation: To better understand how different LLM architectures internally process various kinds of cognitive tasks, and to identify systematic architectural differences that can guide model selection and optimization for real-world, data-intensive applications.
AI.method: The authors evaluate six distinct LLM architectures on twelve cognitive task categories, measuring three key internal metrics for each of 144 task–model combinations: final activation values, attention entropy, and sparsity patterns. They then compare these metrics across encoder versus decoder architectures and across task types, with a focus on how specific tasks influence attention distribution and neuron activation sparsity.
AI.result: They find that tasks involving mathematical reasoning consistently lead to the highest attention entropy across all six architectures, suggesting more diffuse or complex attention patterns for math tasks. Additionally, decoder-style models show significantly higher sparsity in their activation patterns than encoder-style models, indicating more selective neuron usage in decoders.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that encoder and decoder LLMs differ in fundamental ways in how they allocate attention and activate neurons across cognitive tasks, with mathematical reasoning standing out as especially attention-demanding. These insights can inform more informed model selection, configuration, and optimization strategies for big data and task-specific applications.

title: Why are language models less surprised than humans? Testing the Parse Multiplicity Mismatch Hypothesis
summary: Surprisal theory posits that the processing difficulty of a word is determined by its predictability in context, offering a potential link between human sentence processing and next-word predictions from language models. While language model (LM) surprisals successfully predict reading times in naturalistic text, they systematically underpredict the magnitude of difficulty observed in controlled studies of syntactic ambiguity, particularly in garden path sentences. This mismatch might arise from differences in the computational constraints between humans and LMs. Here we test one such hypothesis, specifically, that LMs may be able to simultaneously consider a greater number of distinct sentence interpretations at once, compared to humans. Using Recurrent Neural Network Grammars (RNNGs) with word-synchronous beam search, we systematically vary the number of simultaneous parses used to compute word surprisal, and then use these surprisals to predict human reading times. Reducing the number of simultaneous active parses indeed increases the magnitude of predicted garden path effects, but not nearly enough to capture the full magnitude of the effects in humans. This suggests that differences in the number of simultaneous parses available to LMs and humans cannot reconcile LM-based surprisal with human sentence processing.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether limiting the number of parses considered by language models can reconcile surprisal-based predictions with human reading difficulty, especially in garden-path sentences, and finds that it cannot.
AI.motivation: Surprisal from language models predicts human reading times in naturalistic text but underestimates difficulty in garden-path and syntactic ambiguity studies. The authors are motivated to understand whether this discrepancy is due to computational differences between humans and language models, specifically in how many sentence interpretations can be maintained simultaneously during processing.
AI.method: The authors use Recurrent Neural Network Grammars (RNNGs) with word-synchronous beam search to compute word surprisal. They systematically manipulate the beam size, which controls the number of simultaneous parses (sentence interpretations) the model maintains. They then correlate these surprisal values with human reading times for sentences with syntactic ambiguity, especially garden-path constructions, to see how beam size affects predicted processing difficulty.
AI.result: As the number of simultaneous active parses is reduced (smaller beam size), the model’s predicted garden-path effects increase in magnitude, moving closer to human data. However, even with strong limitations, the model still underpredicts the size of human garden-path effects. The change in beam size does not bridge the gap between LM-based surprisal and observed human reading difficulty.
AI.conclusion: Limiting the number of parses that a language model can maintain concurrently does not fully account for the discrepancy between surprisal-based predictions and human reading times in garden-path sentences. Therefore, differences in the number of simultaneous parses alone cannot explain why language models underpredict human processing difficulty; other cognitive or architectural factors must be involved.

title: Zero-Shot Goal Recognition with Large Language Models
comment: 9 pages, 1 figure, 1 table; appendix with 8 figures and 2 code listings (29 pages total); submitted to NeurIPS 2026
summary: Large language models have recently reached near-parity with classical planners on well-known planning domains, yet this competence relies on world-knowledge exploitation rather than genuine symbolic reasoning. Goal recognition is a complementary abductive task structurally better suited to LLM strengths: it consists of evaluating consistency with world knowledge rather than generating novel action sequences. This paper provides the first systematic zero-shot evaluation of frontier LLMs as goal recognisers on key classical PDDL benchmarks. Our results show that LLM competence on goal recognition is uneven: some models scale with evidence and approach landmark-based accuracy at full observations, while others remain anchored to world-knowledge priors regardless of how much evidence accumulates. Qualitative analysis of model reasoning traces reveals that this divergence reflects a fundamental difference in evidence integration rather than domain familiarity. These findings position goal recognition as a principled benchmark for the foundational planning knowledge of LLMs.

AI.tldr: LLMs are tested as zero-shot goal recognizers on classical planning benchmarks, revealing uneven performance and highlighting goal recognition as a good test of their planning knowledge.
AI.motivation: While LLMs match classical planners on some planning tasks, this may stem from exploiting world knowledge rather than true symbolic reasoning. Goal recognition, which focuses on evaluating consistency with known goals instead of generating plans, may better fit LLM strengths and help clarify what kind of planning competence they really have.
AI.method: The authors perform the first systematic zero-shot evaluation of state-of-the-art LLMs on classical PDDL goal-recognition benchmarks. They compare multiple models, varying the amount of observed action evidence, and contrast LLM performance with landmark-based goal recognition methods. They also qualitatively analyze the models’ reasoning traces to understand how they integrate evidence.
AI.result: Some LLMs improve and approach landmark-based accuracy as more observations are provided, indicating effective evidence integration, while others stay close to their initial world-knowledge priors and barely change their predictions despite additional evidence. The divergence is not explained by simple domain familiarity but by different behaviors in integrating observational evidence.
AI.conclusion: LLM performance on goal recognition is heterogeneous: certain models can integrate evidence and achieve competitive accuracy, whereas others cannot move beyond prior knowledge. This makes goal recognition a principled benchmark to probe and compare the foundational planning and evidence-integration capabilities of LLMs.

title: Reasoning Models Don't Just Think Longer, They Move Differently
summary: Reasoning-trained language models often spend more tokens on harder problems, but longer chains of thought do not show whether a model is merely computing for more steps or following a different internal trajectory. We study this distinction through hidden-state trajectories during chain-of-thought generation across competitive programming, mathematics, and Boolean satisfiability. Raw trajectory geometry is strongly shaped by generation length: longer generations mechanically alter path statistics, so difficulty-dependent comparisons are misleading without adjustment. After residualizing trajectory statistics on length, difficulty remains systematically coupled to corrected trajectory geometry across all domains studied. The clearest reasoning-specific separation appears in the code domain, where harder problems show more direct corrected trajectories and less heterogeneous local curvature in reasoning-trained models than in matched instruction-tuned baselines. Corrected difficulty-geometry coupling is weaker, but still present, in mathematics and Boolean satisfiability. Prompt-stage linear probes do not mirror the code-domain separation, and behavioral annotations show that stronger corrected coupling co-occurs with strategy shifts and uncertainty monitoring. Together, these findings establish length correction as a prerequisite for generation-time trajectory analysis and show that reasoning training can be associated with distinct corrected trajectory geometry, with the strength of the effect depending on the domain.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how the internal hidden-state trajectories of reasoning-trained language models relate to problem difficulty, after carefully removing the confounding effect of output length.
AI.motivation: Longer chains of thought on harder problems could simply reflect more computation steps rather than genuinely different internal reasoning processes. Existing trajectory analyses are confounded by sequence length, making it unclear whether models change how they reason as problems become harder or just reason for longer.
AI.method: The authors analyze hidden-state trajectories during chain-of-thought generation for tasks in competitive programming, mathematics, and Boolean satisfiability. They characterize the geometry of these trajectories (e.g., path statistics, curvature, directness), then "residualize" or correct these statistics with respect to generation length to remove mechanical length effects. They compare reasoning-trained models to instruction-tuned baselines, examine difficulty-geometry coupling after length correction, use prompt-stage linear probes, and relate trajectory patterns to behavioral annotations such as strategy shifts and uncertainty monitoring.
AI.result: Raw trajectory geometry is dominated by generation length, making difficulty-based comparisons misleading. After correcting for length, problem difficulty still systematically correlates with trajectory geometry across all domains. The strongest, clearest effect appears in code: harder problems induce more direct, less locally curved corrected trajectories in reasoning-trained models than in instruction-tuned baselines. In math and SAT, the coupling between difficulty and corrected geometry is weaker but still evident. Prompt-stage linear probes do not reproduce the code-domain separation, and patterns of stronger corrected coupling are aligned with observed strategy changes and uncertainty tracking in behavior.
AI.conclusion: Length correction is essential for meaningful analysis of generation-time hidden-state trajectories in language models. After controlling for length, reasoning training is linked to distinct internal trajectory geometry, especially in code tasks, suggesting that such training changes not only how long models think but also how they organize their internal reasoning. The magnitude and clarity of this effect depend on the task domain.

title: Belief Engine: Configurable and Inspectable Stance Dynamics in Multi-Agent LLM Deliberation
summary: LLM-based agents are increasingly used to simulate deliberative interactions such as negotiation, conflict resolution, and multi-turn opinion exchange. Yet generated transcripts often do not reveal why an agent's stance changes: movement may reflect evidence uptake, anchoring, role drift, echoing, or changed prompt and retrieval context. We introduce the Belief Engine (BE), an auditable belief-update layer that treats "belief" as an evidential state over a proposition and exposes it as scalar stance. BE extracts arguments into structured memory and updates stance with a log-odds rule controlled by evidence uptake u and prior anchoring a. Across multiple base LLMs, parameter sweeps show that these controls reliably shape stance dynamics while preserving an evidence-level update trail. On DEBATE, a human deliberation dataset with pre/post opinions, BE best reconstructs participants whose final stance follows extracted evidence; stable and evidence-opposed cases instead point to anchoring or factors outside the extracted evidence stream. BE provides configurable infrastructure for studying evidence-grounded deliberation, where openness, commitment, convergence, and disagreement can be tied to explicit update assumptions rather than hidden prompt effects.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Belief Engine (BE), a transparent belief-update layer for LLM agents that models stance as evidence-based beliefs and makes updates auditable and controllable.
AI.motivation: LLM-based agents are used for tasks like negotiation and multi-turn opinion exchange, but current systems often fail to show why an agent’s stance changes. Stance shifts may come from multiple hidden causes—such as evidence uptake, role drift, or prompt changes—making it difficult to study or control deliberation behavior. The authors want a principled, inspectable way to connect stance changes to explicit evidence and update assumptions.
AI.method: They define a Belief Engine (BE) that treats belief as an evidential state over propositions and exposes it as a scalar stance. BE: (1) extracts arguments from dialogue into a structured memory; (2) updates the stance using a log-odds rule, parameterized by two controls: evidence uptake (u) and prior anchoring (a); and (3) records an evidence-level trail of updates.
