They run parameter sweeps over u and a across multiple base LLMs to see how these parameters affect stance dynamics. They also evaluate on DEBATE, a dataset of human deliberations with pre/post opinions, using extracted evidence to reconstruct participants’ final stances.
AI.result: Parameter sweeps show that the evidence uptake and anchoring parameters reliably shape how stances evolve over time while preserving a detailed trace of which pieces of evidence contributed to which updates. On the DEBATE dataset, BE best matches human participants whose final opinions align with the extracted evidence, whereas cases where humans remain stable or oppose the evidence reveal strong anchoring or unmodeled external influences. This demonstrates BE’s ability to distinguish evidence-driven from non–evidence-driven stance changes.
AI.conclusion: BE offers a configurable, auditable infrastructure for evidence-grounded deliberation in LLM-based agents. By making update rules explicit and tunable, it allows researchers and practitioners to relate properties like openness, commitment, convergence, and persistent disagreement directly to assumed updating behavior, instead of opaque prompt or context effects. This supports more interpretable and controlled simulations of deliberative processes.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Large Language Models for Schema-Constrained Clinical Information Extraction
summary: Conversational nurse-patient transcripts contain actionable observations, but converting these transcripts into structured representations at scale remains challenging. Documentation burden is substantial, with prior studies showing clinicians spend large portions of their workday on documentation and related desk work rather than direct patient care. MEDIQA-SYNUR focuses on observation extraction from conversational nurse-patient transcripts, requiring systems to normalize these narratives into a predefined schema with value-type constraints. We propose a modular retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline that uses the training set as an exemplar corpus, combines schema-constrained prompting (full schema vs. pruned candidate schema), deterministic schema-based postprocessing, and a second-pass audit, with two LLM backbones: Llama-4-Scout-17B-16E-Instruct and GPT-5.2 with corresponding embedding models for RAG. Our best configuration uses GPT-5.2 with full schema, RAG, and a second-pass auditing, achieving 80.36% F1 score. Overall, our results show that RAG consistently improves performance, while the optimal degree of schema constraint depends on the model, and second-pass auditing yields modest additional gains by correcting residual schema-adherence errors.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a modular retrieval‑augmented generation (RAG) pipeline with schema constraints and auditing to extract structured clinical observations from conversational nurse‑patient transcripts, achieving strong F1 performance on MEDIQA‑SYNUR.
AI.motivation: Nurse‑patient conversations contain rich, actionable clinical observations, but converting these free‑text transcripts into structured data at scale is hard. Clinicians already face heavy documentation burdens and spend much time on desk work rather than direct patient care. The MEDIQA‑SYNUR task aims to automatically extract observations from these conversations and normalize them to a predefined schema with value‑type constraints, motivating methods that can improve accuracy, schema adherence, and scalability of such extraction.
AI.method: The authors design a modular retrieval‑augmented generation (RAG) pipeline that treats the labeled training set as an exemplar corpus. For each input transcript, a retriever uses embedding models paired with two large language model (LLM) backbones—Llama‑4‑Scout‑17B‑16E‑Instruct and GPT‑5.2—to fetch similar exemplars. The generator is prompted with either the full observation schema or a pruned candidate subset to enforce value‑type and field constraints (schema‑constrained prompting). Outputs are further refined with deterministic, schema‑based post‑processing to normalize and validate fields. A second‑pass LLM audit then reviews and corrects residual schema‑adherence errors, forming a two‑stage generation‑and‑audit architecture. Multiple configurations (with/without RAG, different schema constraints, and auditing) are systematically compared.
AI.result: The best performing configuration uses GPT‑5.2 as the backbone, full schema‑constrained prompting, RAG over the training corpus, and a second‑pass auditing stage, achieving an 80.36% F1 score on MEDIQA‑SYNUR. Across experiments, adding RAG consistently improves extraction performance compared with non‑RAG baselines. The impact of schema constraint level (full vs. pruned) varies by model, indicating a model‑dependent optimal balance between constraint strictness and flexibility. The second‑pass audit provides additional but modest performance gains, mainly by fixing remaining schema‑compliance and normalization issues rather than dramatically changing content extraction.
AI.conclusion: A modular RAG‑based LLM pipeline with schema‑constrained prompting and post‑hoc auditing is effective for converting conversational nurse‑patient transcripts into structured observations under the MEDIQA‑SYNUR schema. Using the labeled training set as a retrieval corpus boosts LLM extraction quality, while the ideal degree of schema constraint depends on the backbone model’s capabilities. A lightweight second‑pass auditing step further improves schema adherence and yields small F1 gains. The approach suggests a practical path to reduce documentation burden and scale structured observation extraction from clinical conversations.

title: FINESSE-Bench: A Hierarchical Benchmark Suite for Financial Domain Knowledge and Technical Analysis in Large Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly being applied to financial analysis, reporting, investment decision support, risk management, compliance, and professional training. However, robust evaluation of their domain competence in finance remains incomplete. Widely used open benchmarks such as FinQA, ConvFinQA, and TAT-QA have played an important role in advancing financial question answering and numerical reasoning, but they focus primarily on question answering over financial reports and do not provide an explicit hierarchy of professional difficulty. Broader resources, including FinanceBench, PIXIU, FinBen, and FLaME, expand the coverage of financial tasks, yet the problem of evaluating the transition from foundational knowledge to expert-level financial reasoning remains open. In this work, we present FINESSE-Bench, a suite of eight specialized benchmarks comprising 3,993 questions for hierarchical evaluation of financial competencies in LLMs. FINESSE-Bench combines exam-oriented datasets inspired by professional certifications (CFA-like Levels 1-3, CMT-like Level 2, and CFTe-like Level 1), applied trading task collections, and a Russian-language olympiad benchmark. This design enables evaluation of domain breadth, performance degradation as difficulty increases, the ability to solve computational tasks, and model behavior in specialized financial domains. We also describe a unified evaluation protocol covering multiple-choice questions, numerical answers, and short open-ended responses, together with an automated scoring scheme for freeform answers based on the LLM-as-judge paradigm. FINESSE-Bench is intended both as a complement to existing open financial benchmarks and as a tool for more substantive evaluation of professionally relevant financial competencies in large language models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FINESSE-Bench, a hierarchical benchmark suite to evaluate financial competencies of large language models across increasing levels of professional difficulty and task types.
AI.motivation: Existing financial NLP benchmarks (FinQA, ConvFinQA, TAT-QA, FinanceBench, PIXIU, FinBen, FLaME) either focus narrowly on QA over reports or lack an explicit hierarchy of professional difficulty. As LLMs are used in high‑stakes financial tasks (analysis, reporting, investment, risk, compliance, training), there is a need for a structured way to evaluate how well they transition from basic financial knowledge to expert‑level reasoning, including numerical and applied tasks.
AI.method: The authors construct FINESSE-Bench, a suite of eight specialized benchmarks totaling 3,993 questions. The suite includes exam-like datasets modeled on professional certifications (CFA-like Levels 1–3, CMT-like Level 2, CFTe-like Level 1), applied trading-related tasks, and a Russian-language olympiad benchmark. They define a unified evaluation protocol that supports multiple-choice, numerical, and short open-ended responses, and implement an automated scoring system for freeform answers using an LLM-as-judge approach.
AI.result: FINESSE-Bench provides empirical means to measure: (1) breadth of domain coverage, (2) performance degradation as difficulty increases, (3) the ability of models to handle computational/quantitative finance tasks, and (4) behavior in specialized subdomains, including non-English (Russian) finance contexts. While specific numerical results are not in the abstract, the benchmark itself is positioned as a new resource enabling these analyses.
AI.conclusion: FINESSE-Bench fills a gap in financial LLM evaluation by offering a hierarchical, professionally oriented benchmark suite and a unified evaluation protocol. It is proposed as a complementary resource to existing financial benchmarks and as a more substantive tool for assessing professionally relevant financial competencies in large language models.

title: RoPE Distinguishes Neither Positions Nor Tokens in Long Contexts, Provably
summary: We identify intrinsic limitations of Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE) in Transformer-based long-context language models. Our theoretical analysis abstracts away from the specific content of the context and depends only on its length. We prove that as context length increases, RoPE-based attention becomes unpredictable and loses two properties that are central to its effectiveness. First, it loses its locality bias: RoPE is no more likely to favor nearer positions than substantially farther ones. Second, it loses consistency in token relevance: a key vector that receives a higher attention score than an alternative at one position may receive a lower score at another. In both cases, the probability of failure approaches 0.5, no better than random guessing. We further prove that the attention score can remain unchanged when a key token is moved to a different position, or even replaced by a different token, indicating a failure to distinguish positions or tokens. Adjusting the RoPE base trades off distinguishing positions against distinguishing tokens but cannot preserve both at the same time. Increasing the RoPE base hyperparameter, a common practice in today's long-context models, helps distinguish different tokens, but inevitably sacrifices the ability to distinguish positions. Our empirical analysis shows that multi-head, multi-layer architectures are insufficient to overcome these limitations. Our findings suggest that fundamentally new mechanisms for encoding position and token order may be needed in future Transformer long-context language models.

AI.tldr: The paper theoretically and empirically shows that RoPE, widely used for positional encoding in long-context Transformers, breaks down at long lengths: it loses locality bias and consistent token relevance, and cannot simultaneously distinguish positions and tokens, suggesting the need for new positional mechanisms.
AI.motivation: RoPE is the de facto standard for extending Transformer context windows, but its behavior at very long context lengths is poorly understood. Practitioners often just increase the RoPE base hyperparameter to support longer sequences, assuming this scales well. The authors are motivated to rigorously analyze whether RoPE remains reliable as context grows, and to identify any fundamental limitations that may cap the effectiveness of current long-context LMs.
AI.method: They perform a theoretical analysis of RoPE-based attention, abstracting away token content and focusing on the effect of sequence length on attention scores. They prove properties about how attention behaves as context length increases, including locality bias, ranking consistency between keys, and sensitivity to positions and token identities as a function of the RoPE base. They then empirically validate these theoretical findings in multi-head, multi-layer Transformer architectures, examining whether architectural depth and width mitigate RoPE’s issues.
AI.result: They prove that for sufficiently long contexts, RoPE-based attention loses its locality bias: nearer tokens are no longer more likely to receive higher attention than far ones. They also show a loss of ranking consistency: a key that beats another at one position can lose to it at another, with the probability of such failures approaching 0.5. They further demonstrate that attention scores can remain unchanged when a key is moved to another position or even replaced by another token, revealing indistinguishability of positions or tokens. Adjusting the RoPE base creates an unavoidable trade-off between distinguishing positions and distinguishing tokens, and increasing the base—common in practice—improves token distinguishability at the expense of positional distinguishability. Empirically, they confirm that multi-head, multi-layer architectures do not fix these limitations.
AI.conclusion: RoPE has intrinsic, theoretically grounded limitations for very long contexts that cannot be fully alleviated by tuning the RoPE base or by stacking more layers and heads. In long-context regimes, RoPE loses locality bias and consistent token relevance and may fail to distinguish positions and tokens. As a result, current RoPE-based approaches to scaling context lengths are fundamentally constrained, and future long-context Transformer models likely require new positional encoding mechanisms or order-encoding strategies beyond RoPE.

title: From LLM-Generated Conjectures to Lean Formalizations: Automated Polynomial Inequality Proving via Sum-of-Squares Certificates
summary: Automated proving of polynomial inequalities is a fundamental challenge in automated mathematical reasoning, where rich algebraic structure and a rapidly growing certificate search space hinder scalability. Purely symbolic approaches provide strong guarantees but often scale poorly as the number of variables or the degree increases, due to expensive algebraic manipulations and rapidly growing intermediate expressions. In parallel, LLM-guided methods have made notable progress, particularly on competition-style inequalities with a small number of variables. To address the remaining scalability challenges, we propose NSPI, a neuro-symbolic framework that combines the complementary strengths of LLMs and symbolic computation for polynomial-inequality proving. Concretely, an LLM proposes a conjecture in the form of an approximate polynomial Sum-Of-Squares (SOS) decomposition; we refine it via symbolic computation to obtain an exact polynomial SOS representation, which directly proves the target inequality, and we further certify the proof in Lean, yielding an end-to-end pipeline from heuristic discovery to machine-checked proof. Experiments on challenging benchmarks involving polynomials with up to 10 variables demonstrate the effectiveness and scalability of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: They introduce NSPI, a neuro-symbolic framework that uses an LLM to suggest approximate Sum-Of-Squares decompositions of polynomial inequalities, then refines and formally verifies them symbolically, achieving scalable, machine-checked proofs for high-dimensional inequalities.
AI.motivation: Automated proving of polynomial inequalities is difficult to scale because purely symbolic methods suffer from combinatorial explosion in algebraic manipulation, while recent LLM-guided approaches work mostly on small, competition-style inequalities. There is a need for a method that can handle higher-degree, higher-dimensional polynomial inequalities efficiently while retaining formal guarantees.
AI.method: NSPI integrates a large language model with symbolic computation and formal verification. The LLM first proposes a conjectured, approximate Sum-Of-Squares (SOS) decomposition of the target polynomial inequality. Symbolic algorithms then refine this approximate SOS into an exact SOS representation, which constitutes a formal algebraic proof of non-negativity. Finally, the resulting proof is encoded and certified in the Lean proof assistant, giving an end-to-end, machine-checked pipeline from heuristic search to verified proof.
AI.result: On challenging benchmarks with polynomials involving up to 10 variables, NSPI successfully finds and verifies SOS proofs, demonstrating better scalability and effectiveness than prior methods that are either purely symbolic or purely LLM-based on low-dimensional problems.
AI.conclusion: By tightly coupling heuristic guidance from LLMs with exact symbolic SOS refinement and formal proof checking in Lean, NSPI achieves scalable, trustworthy automated proving of polynomial inequalities, extending the reach of automated reasoning to more complex, higher-dimensional algebraic problems.

title: DetectRL-X: Towards Reliable Multilingual and Real-World LLM-Generated Text Detection
summary: The effective detection and governance of Large Language Model (LLM) generated content has become increasingly critical due to the growing risk of misuse. Despite the impressive performance of existing detectors, their reliability and potential in multilingual, real-world scenarios remain largely underexplored. In this study, we introduce DetectRL-X, a comprehensive multilingual benchmark designed to evaluate advanced detectors across 8 dimensions. The benchmark encompasses 8 languages commonly used in commercial contexts and collects human-written texts from 6 domains highly susceptible to LLM misuse. To better aligned with real-world applications, We create LLM-generated texts using 4 popular commercial LLMs, and include typical AI-assisted writing operations such as polishing, expanding, and condensing to capture authentic usage patterns. Furthermore, we develop a multilingual framework for paraphrasing and perturbation attacks to simulate diverse human modifications and writing noise, enabling stress testing of detectors across languages. Experimental results on DetectRL-X reveal the strengths and limitations of current state-of-the-art detectors when applied to diverse linguistic resources. We further analyze how domains, generators, attack strategies, text length, and refinement operations influence performance in different languages, underscoring DetectRL-X as an effective benchmark for strengthening multilingual and language-specific detectors.

AI.tldr: DetectRL-X is a multilingual benchmark and attack framework to rigorously evaluate how well LLM-generated text detectors work across languages, domains, and realistic writing operations.
AI.motivation: LLM-generated content is increasingly used and sometimes misused, but current detectors are mainly evaluated in narrow, mostly monolingual, lab-like settings. Their robustness and reliability in multilingual, real-world scenarios with human edits and typical AI-assisted writing operations are unclear, creating risks for governance, safety, and trust.
AI.method: The authors construct DetectRL-X, a benchmark covering 8 commercially relevant languages and 6 high-risk domains for LLM misuse. They gather human-written data, generate LLM-written data with 4 commercial LLMs, and simulate realistic AI-assisted operations (polishing, expansion, condensation). They also design a multilingual paraphrasing and perturbation attack framework to mimic human edits and noise, then systematically test state-of-the-art detectors along 8 evaluation dimensions, including language, domain, generator, attack type, and text length.
AI.result: Experiments on DetectRL-X reveal that existing state-of-the-art detectors exhibit varying robustness: their performance differs significantly across languages, domains, generators, attack strategies, and text properties. The benchmark exposes both strengths (where detectors remain reliable) and clear weaknesses (where they fail under certain languages, edits, or attacks).
AI.conclusion: DetectRL-X serves as an effective, realistic benchmark for stress-testing and improving multilingual and language-specific LLM-content detectors. By uncovering how different factors impact detection performance, it provides a foundation and diagnostic tool for building more reliable detection systems in real-world, multilingual environments.

title: X-SYNTH: Beyond Retrieval -- Enterprise Context Synthesis from Observed Human Attention
summary: In enterprise operations, the context required for an AI agent task is scattered across systems of record, static information stores, and communication channels. What is stored is system state, a lossy representation of the work that actually happened [2, 52]. The prevailing approach [17, 31, 34, 36] retrieves by matching request content to what is stored; for narrow requests this works well. But synthesis quality depends on knowing what to surface and how to interpret it: knowledge specific to each organization, team, and individual [5, 57, 61], present in behavioral patterns, absent from any retrieval index. For complex agentic tasks it breaks down: True Lead Rate is low, False Lead Rate is high, and the model has no mechanism to improve. We present X-SYNTH, a framework for enterprise context synthesis grounded in human attention, the digitally observable interaction signatures of each worker, encoding not just what they did but the sequence in which they did it, along with implicit reward signals. Behavioral traces preceding positive outcomes are distinguishable from those that did not, without external labeling. X-SYNTH models each individual's behavioral baseline as a Digital Twin Signature (DTS) and selects among seven qualitatively distinct attention filters: Proportional, Inverse, Differential, Recurrent, Comparative, Sequential, and Collective, per individual and per query, to identify causally relevant activity signatures. A four-stage pipeline assembles ranked context grounded in behavioral patterns rather than query embeddings. On a sales lead identification task, a frontier model unaided achieves 9.5% True Lead Rate (TLR) with 90.5% False Lead Rate (FLR). Augmented with X-SYNTH, TLR rises to 61.9% (6.5x) while FLR falls to 18.8%. Enterprise context synthesis is not a retrieval problem. It is a relevance problem, and human attention is its most reliable ground truth.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces X-SYNTH, a framework that uses workers’ behavioral traces and attention patterns, rather than traditional content-based retrieval, to synthesize enterprise context and dramatically improve AI agents’ performance on complex tasks such as sales lead identification.
AI.motivation: Existing enterprise AI systems rely on retrieval that matches user queries to stored data (systems of record, documents, messages). This works for narrow, well-specified requests but fails for complex agentic tasks because important, organization-specific knowledge is encoded in human behavior and attention, not explicitly stored or indexed. As a result, models surface lots of irrelevant information (high False Lead Rate), miss important signals (low True Lead Rate), and have no mechanism to improve based on outcomes. The authors aim to reframe context synthesis as a relevance problem grounded in human attention signals, rather than a pure retrieval problem, to better support complex enterprise decision-making tasks.
AI.method: The authors propose X-SYNTH, which models each worker’s typical behavior as a Digital Twin Signature (DTS), constructed from their digitally observable interaction traces (what they did, in what sequence, with implicit reward signals from outcomes). They define seven attention filters—Proportional, Inverse, Differential, Recurrent, Comparative, Sequential, and Collective—used per individual and per query to select behavior patterns that are causally related to successful outcomes. A four-stage pipeline then uses these filtered behavioral signatures to assemble and rank contextual information for AI agents, grounding context selection in learned patterns of human attention and success rather than in query embeddings alone.
AI.result: On a sales lead identification benchmark, a strong baseline model without X-SYNTH achieves a True Lead Rate (TLR) of 9.5% with a False Lead Rate (FLR) of 90.5%, indicating poor discrimination between good and bad leads. When augmented with X-SYNTH, TLR increases to 61.9% (a 6.5x improvement), while FLR drops to 18.8%, demonstrating that leveraging behavioral attention signatures significantly improves both precision and recall in complex enterprise decision tasks.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that enterprise context synthesis should not be framed as a retrieval problem based on content similarity, but as a relevance problem grounded in human attention. By modeling individual behavioral baselines as Digital Twin Signatures and applying specialized attention filters to identify causally relevant activity patterns, X-SYNTH can substantially enhance AI agents’ performance on complex tasks. Human attention, as captured in behavioral traces and outcomes, provides a robust ground truth for selecting context, yielding large gains in effectiveness over conventional retrieval-based approaches.

title: Process Rewards with Learned Reliability
summary: Process Reward Models (PRMs) provide step-level feedback for reasoning, but current PRMs usually output only a single reward score for each step. Downstream methods must therefore treat imperfect step-level reward predictions as reliable decision signals, with no indication of when these predictions should be trusted. We propose BetaPRM, a distributional PRM that predicts both a step-level success probability and the reliability of that prediction. Given step-success supervision from Monte Carlo continuations, BetaPRM learns a Beta belief that explains the observed number of successful continuations through a Beta-Binomial likelihood, rather than regressing to the finite-sample success ratio as a point target. This learned reliability signal indicates when a step reward should be trusted, enabling downstream applications to distinguish reliable rewards from uncertain ones. As one application, we introduce Adaptive Computation Allocation (ACA) for PRM-guided Best-of-N reasoning. ACA uses the learned reliability signal to stop when a high-reward solution is reliable and to spend additional computation on uncertain candidate prefixes. Experiments across four backbones and four reasoning benchmarks show that BetaPRM improves PRM-guided Best-of-N selection while preserving standard step-level error detection. Built on this signal, ACA improves the accuracy--token tradeoff over fixed-budget Best-of-16, reducing token usage by up to 33.57% while improving final-answer accuracy.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces BetaPRM, a probabilistic process reward model that outputs both a success probability and a reliability estimate per reasoning step, enabling more efficient and accurate Best-of-N reasoning via adaptive computation allocation.
AI.motivation: Existing process reward models give only a single scalar reward per step, forcing downstream methods to treat noisy step-level predictions as equally reliable. There is no way to know when to trust a reward signal, which hurts decision-making and efficiency in multi-sample reasoning like Best-of-N.
AI.method: BetaPRM models each step’s reward as a Beta distribution, parameterized to reflect both the estimated probability of success and the confidence (reliability) in that estimate. Using Monte Carlo continuations of partial solutions, it observes how often each step leads to eventual success and fits a Beta belief via a Beta-Binomial likelihood, instead of regressing directly to the empirical success rate. The model thus learns to output both a mean success probability and a reliability/precision parameter. On top of this, the authors propose Adaptive Computation Allocation (ACA) for PRM-guided Best-of-N: given candidate prefixes, ACA uses the reliability scores to decide when to stop sampling (if a high-reward candidate is also reliable) or where to allocate more samples (on promising but uncertain prefixes).
AI.result: Across four backbone models and four reasoning benchmarks, BetaPRM yields better Best-of-N selection than standard scalar PRMs while maintaining comparable step-level error detection performance. When used with ACA, the approach dominates fixed-budget Best-of-16 strategies, achieving higher final-answer accuracy while using up to 33.57% fewer tokens.
AI.conclusion: Modeling reward uncertainty at the step level via a Beta distribution allows PRMs to provide both value and reliability of their feedback. This reliability-aware signal enables more efficient reasoning procedures like ACA, which adaptively allocate computation and improve the accuracy–efficiency tradeoff compared with conventional fixed-budget Best-of-N methods.

title: CAPS: Cascaded Adaptive Pairwise Selection for Efficient Parallel Reasoning
summary: Parallel reasoning, where a generator samples many candidate solutions and an aggregator selects the best, is one of the most effective forms of test-time scaling in large language models, and pairwise self-verification has become its strongest aggregation primitive. Yet pairwise verification carries a heavy cost: each judgment reads two complete solutions in full, and existing methods perform tens of such judgments per problem regardless of whether the comparison is informative. We introduce CAPS (Cascaded Adaptive Pairwise Selection), an inference-only framework that allocates verifier compute non-uniformly along two orthogonal axes: an evidence axis that adapts how much of each candidate the judge sees, and a distribution axis that adapts how comparisons are spread across the pool. CAPS instantiates these into a four-stage cascade with an optional rescue subroutine, and admits a closed-form verifier-token cost in which the per-candidate marginal cost is roughly halved relative to uniform full-evidence schedules. On four self-verifying models (Qwen3-14B, GPT-OSS-20B, Qwen3-4B-Instruct/Thinking) and five reasoning benchmarks spanning code (LiveCodeBench-v5/v6, CodeContests) and math (AIME 2025, HMMT 2025), CAPS outperforms the leading pairwise verifier on 14 of 20 suites while using 25.4% of its verifier-token budget on code, and outperforms pointwise self-verification on all 20. The trade-off suites admit an interpretable diagnostic in terms of the verifier's accuracy at partial versus full evidence, providing a concrete pre-deployment check for cascade suitability.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CAPS, a cascaded adaptive pairwise selection framework that reduces the cost of pairwise self-verification in LLM parallel reasoning while maintaining or improving accuracy across code and math benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Parallel reasoning with many sampled solutions plus an aggregator is powerful, but current pairwise self-verification is expensive because each comparison reads two full solutions and uses many uniform, potentially uninformative judgments. The authors want a more compute-efficient verification mechanism that preserves or improves solution quality at test time.
AI.method: They design CAPS, an inference-time framework that allocates verifier compute adaptively along two axes: (1) an evidence axis controlling how much of each candidate solution the verifier sees (partial vs full evidence), and (2) a distribution axis controlling how comparisons are spread across candidates. CAPS is instantiated as a four-stage cascade with an optional rescue step, allowing early, cheaper judgments when possible and escalating to more costly full-evidence comparisons when necessary. They derive a closed-form expression for verifier-token cost showing reduced marginal cost per candidate compared to uniform full-evidence pairwise schedules, and evaluate CAPS on several self-verifying models and reasoning benchmarks.
AI.result: On four self-verifying models (Qwen3-14B, GPT-OSS-20B, Qwen3-4B-Instruct/Thinking) and five reasoning benchmarks (LiveCodeBench-v5/v6, CodeContests, AIME 2025, HMMT 2025), CAPS beats the strongest prior pairwise verifier on 14 of 20 evaluation suites while using only about 25.4% of its verifier-token budget on code tasks, and it outperforms pointwise self-verification on all 20 suites. The framework also yields interpretable trade-off diagnostics based on verifier accuracy under partial vs full evidence.
AI.conclusion: CAPS significantly reduces verifier token cost for pairwise self-verification in parallel reasoning while usually improving or at least matching solution quality. Its cascaded, adaptive design makes verifier computation more targeted and efficient, and the partial-vs-full evidence diagnostic offers a practical way to assess when such cascades are appropriate before deployment.

title: When Latent Geometry Is Not Enough: Draft-Conditioned Latent Refinement for Non-Autoregressive Text Generation
comment: 17 pages, 1 figure, 6 tables. Technical Report v1. Stage 1 complete; Stage 2 ongoing Code: https://github.com/saslifat-gif/structured-latent-text-refinement
summary: Continuous diffusion and flow models are attractive for non-autoregressive text generation because they can update all positions in parallel. A major difficulty is the interface between continuous latent states and discrete tokens. This report studies a draft-conditioned latent refinement model built from a frozen BERT encoder, a parallel decoder, a denoising DraftPrior, a local FlowNet, and a learned diagonal MetricNet. Early Gaussian-start experiments showed that good latent-space metrics, such as scale matching or cosine similarity, do not guarantee good decoding. Generated latents can be close to real encoder latents but still produce high-entropy, biased, or repetitive token distributions. We therefore frame the task as controlled local refinement rather than full generation from noise. On ROCStories, using the first two sentences as prompt and the last three as target, full 768-dimensional BERT latents recover tokens much better than compressed 256-dimensional latents. With 768-dimensional latents, DraftPrior target-token probability is 0.938 for clean drafts, 0.613 for 3% token dropout, 0.483 for 5% dropout, and 0.272 for 10% dropout. Local flow refinement and fused decoder-aware readout give modest additional gains, while metric learning and OT-style alignment improve geometry but do not close the decoder gap. The main result is a diagnostic one: latent geometry alone is not enough. Continuous latent text generation should be evaluated by decoder recoverability, the quality of the start distribution, and whether refinement preserves decoder-readable structure.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates continuous diffusion/flow-style latent models for non-autoregressive text generation and finds that having a good latent geometry is insufficient; decoder recoverability and refinement behavior are crucial.
AI.motivation: Non-autoregressive text generation is attractive because it allows parallel token updates, but continuous diffusion and flow approaches struggle to bridge continuous latent spaces and discrete tokens. Existing work often assumes that if encoder latents are geometrically well-behaved (e.g., similar by cosine distance), then decoding will be good, but this is not guaranteed. The authors want to diagnose where this assumption fails and what matters for continuous latent text generation.
AI.method: They construct a draft-conditioned latent refinement system using a frozen BERT encoder, a parallel decoder, a denoising DraftPrior that predicts clean latents from corrupted ones, a local FlowNet for small latent-space flows, and a learned diagonal MetricNet to shape latent geometry. They compare full 768-dimensional BERT latents vs compressed 256-dimensional latents on ROCStories, using the first two sentences as prompts and the last three as generation targets. They run Gaussian-start experiments and dropout-corruption experiments, and test metric learning and OT-style alignment to improve latent geometry, while measuring how well latents can be decoded back into correct tokens.
AI.result: 768-dimensional latents substantially outperform 256-dimensional compressed latents in token recovery. For 768d latents, the DraftPrior’s target-token probabilities decrease as corruption increases: 0.938 (clean), 0.613 (3% dropout), 0.483 (5% dropout), 0.272 (10% dropout). Local flow refinement and decoder-aware readout add modest improvements. Metric learning and optimal-transport-style alignment improve latent-space geometry (e.g., distances and alignments) but still leave a noticeable gap between apparently good latents and the quality of decoded text, leading to high-entropy or biased token distributions from seemingly close latents.
AI.conclusion: Latent-space geometry, even when improved by metric learning or OT alignment, is not a sufficient criterion for successful continuous latent text generation. What matters is decoder recoverability—how well tokens can be reconstructed from latents—the quality of the starting latent distribution, and whether refinement procedures preserve decoder-readable structure. Continuous diffusion/flow models for text should therefore be evaluated in terms of decoding behavior and refinement dynamics, not just geometric similarity between generated and real latents.

title: RTL-BenchMT: Dynamic Maintenance of RTL Generation Benchmark Through Agent-Assisted Analysis and Revision
comment: This paper has been accepted by DAC 2026
summary: This paper introduces RTL-BenchMT, an agentic framework for dynamically maintaining RTL generation benchmarks. Large Language Models (LLMs) assisted automated RTL generation is one of the most important directions in EDA research. However, current RTL benchmarks face two critical challenges: (1) flawed cases in the benchmarks and (2) overfitting to the benchmarks. Both challenges are difficult to resolve purely by manual engineering effort. To address these issues and systematically reduce human maintenance costs, we propose an automated agentic framework, RTL-BenchMT. RTL-BenchMT focuses on two key applications: (1) automatically identifying and revising flawed benchmark cases and (2) automatically detecting and updating overfitting cases. With the assistance of RTL-BenchMT, we conduct a thorough, in-depth analysis of flawed and overfitting cases and produce a refined benchmark suite that will be open-sourced to the community.

AI.tldr: RTL-BenchMT is an automated agent framework to maintain and refine RTL generation benchmarks for LLM-based EDA.
AI.motivation: Current RTL generation benchmarks used to evaluate LLM-based RTL design suffer from flawed cases and from models overfitting to the fixed benchmark sets. These issues are hard and expensive to solve manually, limiting reliable evaluation and progress in RTL generation research.
AI.method: The authors design RTL-BenchMT, an agentic, automated framework with workflows that (1) detect and repair flawed benchmark cases and (2) detect and refresh cases that models have overfit to. The system uses multiple interacting agents to analyze, revise, and update RTL tasks and solutions with minimal human intervention.
AI.result: Using RTL-BenchMT, the authors systematically analyze existing flawed and overfitting cases in RTL benchmarks and generate a refined benchmark suite. They demonstrate that the framework can automatically identify problematic items and improve the quality and robustness of the benchmark set.
AI.conclusion: An automated agentic framework like RTL-BenchMT can substantially reduce human maintenance cost while improving the reliability and longevity of RTL benchmarks for LLM-assisted RTL generation. The resulting refined, open-sourced benchmark suite should better support future research and fair evaluation in this domain.

title: GiLT: Augmenting Transformer Language Models with Dependency Graphs
summary: Augmenting Transformers with linguistic structures effectively enhances the syntactic generalization performance of language models. Previous work in this direction focuses on syntactic tree structures of languages, in particular constituency tree structures. We propose Graph-Infused Layers Transformer Language Model (GiLT) which leverages dependency graphs for augmenting Transformer language models. Unlike most previous work, GiLT does not insert extra structural tokens in language modeling; instead, it injects structural information into language modeling by modulating attention weights in the Transformer with features extracted from the dependency graph that is incrementally constructed along with token prediction. In our experiments, GiLT with semantic dependency graphs achieves better syntactic generalization while maintaining competitive perplexity in comparison with Transformer language model baselines. In addition, GiLT can be finetuned from a pretrained language model to achieve improved downstream task performance. Our code is released at https://github.com/cookie-pie-oops/GiLT-LM.

AI.tldr: They propose GiLT, a Transformer language model that injects dependency-graph information directly into attention weights, improving syntactic generalization and downstream tasks while keeping perplexity competitive.
AI.motivation: Standard Transformers lack explicit linguistic structure and prior structural augmentation has mainly used constituency trees and extra structure tokens. The authors want to exploit richer dependency graphs for better syntactic generalization without relying on structural tokens or harming perplexity.
AI.method: GiLT incrementally builds a dependency graph as tokens are predicted and extracts graph-based features. These features modulate the Transformer’s attention weights instead of adding structural tokens. They experiment with both syntactic and semantic dependency graphs, integrating them as additional signals in the attention mechanism of a Transformer language model and enabling finetuning from pretrained LMs.
AI.result: GiLT models using semantic dependency graphs obtain better syntactic generalization than standard Transformer baselines and retain similar perplexity. When finetuned from a pretrained language model, GiLT further improves performance on downstream tasks compared to the base model.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating dependency-graph information into attention, rather than adding structural tokens, is an effective way to infuse linguistic structure into Transformers. Semantic dependency graphs, in particular, enhance syntactic generalization and downstream performance while preserving strong language modeling quality.

title: DRS-GUI: Dynamic Region Search for Training-Free GUI Grounding
summary: GUI agents powered by Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated impressive capability in understanding and executing user instructions. However, accurately grounding instruction-relevant elements from high-resolution screenshots cluttered with irrelevant UI components remains challenging for existing approaches. Inspired by how humans dynamically adjust their perceptual scope to locate task-related regions on complex screens, we propose DRS-GUI, a training-free dynamic region search framework for GUI grounding that can be seamlessly integrated into existing MLLMs. DRS-GUI introduces a lightweight UI Perceptor that performs three human-like perceptual actions (Focus, Shift, and Scatter) to progressively explore the interface and generate region proposals. To dynamically schedule these actions, we further design an Action Planner based on Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS). A region quality reward is employed to evaluate and select the highly instruction-relevant region, efficiently pruning redundant UI elements. Experiments demonstrate that DRS-GUI yields a 14\% improvement on ScreenSpot-Pro for general and GUI-specific MLLMs (Qwen2.5-VL-7B and UGround-V1-7B), significantly enhancing grounding performance and generalization.

AI.tldr: They propose DRS-GUI, a dynamic region search framework that helps multimodal LLM-based GUI agents better localize instruction-relevant regions on cluttered, high-resolution screens, significantly improving grounding performance.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal LLM GUI agents struggle to accurately ground (locate) instruction-relevant elements on complex, cluttered high-resolution screenshots with many irrelevant UI components. The authors are motivated by how humans dynamically adjust their visual attention to find task-related regions and want to give MLLMs a similar capability without retraining the models.
AI.method: They design DRS-GUI, a training-free dynamic region search framework that plugs into existing MLLMs. It uses a lightweight UI Perceptor that performs three human-inspired perceptual actions—Focus (zooming into promising regions), Shift (moving attention to neighboring areas), and Scatter (broadly exploring new regions)—to generate region proposals across the interface. An Action Planner built on Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) dynamically schedules these actions. A region quality reward evaluates how instruction-relevant each region is, guiding MCTS to select the best region while pruning redundant or irrelevant UI areas.
AI.result: On the ScreenSpot-Pro benchmark, integrating DRS-GUI into both general and GUI-specific MLLMs (Qwen2.5-VL-7B and UGround-V1-7B) yields a reported 14% improvement in grounding accuracy, indicating better localization of instruction-relevant UI elements and improved generalization across settings.
AI.conclusion: DRS-GUI, by mimicking human-like dynamic visual exploration through Focus/Shift/Scatter actions planned with MCTS and guided by a region quality reward, can substantially enhance GUI grounding performance of existing MLLMs without additional training, making it a practical and general plugin for GUI agents operating on cluttered high-resolution interfaces.

title: Measuring Maximum Activations in Open Large Language Models
summary: The dynamic range of activations is a first-order constraint for low-bit quantization, activation scaling, and stable LLM inference. Prior work characterized outlier features and massive activations on pre-2024 LLaMA-style models, and the downstream activation-quantization stack inherits that picture without revisiting it for the post-LLaMA open-model boom. We ask the deployment-oriented question: how large can activations get in modern open LLMs, and how does this magnitude vary across families, generations, and training stages? Under a unified pipeline (5,000-sample multi-domain corpus, family-specific tokenization, identical hooks across embeddings, hidden states, attention, MLP/MoE, SwiGLU gates, and final norm), we measure global and layerwise maxima on 27 checkpoints from 8 open families spanning dense, MoE, vision-language, intermediate-training, and instruction-tuned variants. We find that (i) global maxima span over nearly four orders of magnitude at comparable parameter counts, with Qwen3.5 and MoE checkpoints in the 10^2 to 10^3 range and Gemma3-27B-it reaching ~7 x 10^5; (ii) cross-family and cross-generation comparisons break simple monotonic scaling; and (iii) MoE checkpoints exhibit 14.0-23.4x lower peaks than matched-scale dense counterparts, while the residual stream carries the global maximum in 22/24 checkpoints. A lightweight INT-8 sanity check shows that measured maxima co-vary with low-bit reconstruction error via activation-scale selection. We conclude that maximum activation magnitude is a model property tied to family, architecture, and training stage - not a simple byproduct of size - and should be measured and reported alongside any open-weight release before low-bit deployment. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/clx1415926/Max_act_llm.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically measures how large activations get in modern open LLMs across many model families and training stages, showing that maximum activation magnitudes vary widely and are a fundamental model property that must be reported for safe low-bit deployment.
AI.motivation: Low-bit quantization and stable deployment of large language models critically depend on the dynamic range of activations, but most prior analyses focused on older LLaMA-style models and assumed their activation behavior generalizes. With a proliferation of new open-weight families (dense, MoE, VLMs, instruction-tuned, intermediate checkpoints), practitioners lack up-to-date, comparable measurements of activation maxima and their variability, which are essential to choose quantization scales and avoid instability.
AI.method: The authors build a unified measurement pipeline using a 5,000-sample multi-domain corpus, applying family-specific tokenization and inserting identical logging hooks across key components (embeddings, hidden states, attention blocks, MLP/MoE layers, SwiGLU gates, final normalization). They evaluate 27 checkpoints from 8 open model families, spanning dense, MoE, vision-language, intermediate-training, and instruction-tuned variants, and record global and layerwise maximum activation magnitudes. They also run an INT8 quantization sanity check to relate these maxima to low-bit reconstruction error via activation-scale selection.
AI.result: They find that global maximum activations can differ by almost four orders of magnitude even at similar parameter counts: Qwen3.5 and MoE models tend to have maxima around 10^2–10^3, whereas Gemma3-27B-it reaches roughly 7×10^5. Simple monotonic relationships with model size or generation do not hold across families or versions. Mixture-of-Experts models consistently show 14–23× lower peak activations than matched dense models, and in 22 out of 24 checkpoints the highest activations occur in the residual stream. The INT8 experiment shows that higher measured maxima align with increased low-bit reconstruction error through their influence on activation scaling.
AI.conclusion: Maximum activation magnitude is an inherent property of a specific model family, architecture, and training stage, rather than a straightforward consequence of size alone. Because these maxima strongly affect quantization behavior and low-bit stability, they should be explicitly measured and reported whenever releasing open-weight models, enabling more reliable downstream quantization and deployment decisions.

title: Position: Artificial Intelligence Needs Meta Intelligence -- the Case for Metacognitive AI
comment: This is a preliminary version accepted for presentation and publication at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML26). The modified final version will be available in the conference proceedings
summary: This position paper argues for metacognition as a general design principle for creating more accurate, secure, and efficient AI. The metacognitive solution involves systems monitoring their own states and judiciously allocating resources depending on each problem instance's difficulty or cost of mistakes. Drawing inspiration both from past work on resource-rational AI and from well-documented metacognitive strategies in psychology and cognitive science, we identify specific challenges in embedding these strategies into AI design and highlight open theoretical and implementation problems. We showcase these principles through a tangible example of improved learning efficiency, effectiveness, and security in a Federated Learning (FL) case study. We show how these principles can be translated into practice with a novel software framework developed specifically to allow the community to design, deploy, and experiment with metacognition-enabled AI applications.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes metacognition—systems monitoring and managing their own reasoning and resources—as a general design principle to make AI more accurate, secure, and efficient, and demonstrates this via a federated learning case study and a supporting software framework.
AI.motivation: Current AI systems often apply fixed strategies regardless of problem difficulty, resource constraints, or the cost of mistakes, which can lead to inefficiency, reduced accuracy, and security vulnerabilities. Psychology and cognitive science show that humans use metacognitive strategies—monitoring their own knowledge and adjusting effort accordingly—to improve performance. The paper is motivated by the idea that importing such metacognitive mechanisms into AI could systematically improve AI reliability, efficiency, and robustness, but this is underexplored and faces theoretical and implementation challenges.
AI.method: This is a position paper that conceptually argues for treating metacognition as a core AI design principle. The authors (1) synthesize insights from resource-rational AI and human metacognition research, (2) identify and classify challenges in embedding metacognitive strategies into AI architectures, such as how to monitor internal states and decide on resource allocation, (3) highlight open theoretical and practical problems, and (4) instantiate these ideas in a concrete case study in federated learning, where metacognitive mechanisms are used to adapt learning effort and security measures based on task difficulty and risk. They also present a new software framework that enables building and experimenting with metacognition-enabled AI systems.
AI.result: The paper conceptually shows how metacognitive control can lead to more efficient, effective, and secure AI behavior. In the federated learning case study, incorporating metacognitive principles yields improved learning efficiency and effectiveness, as well as enhanced security properties. Additionally, the authors deliver a practical outcome in the form of a novel software framework that implements these principles, providing tools for the community to build and test metacognitive AI applications.
AI.conclusion: Metacognition should be adopted as a general design principle in AI systems to enable adaptive monitoring and control of their own reasoning processes, improving accuracy, resource efficiency, and security. The federated learning example illustrates the practical benefits and feasibility of this approach. However, fully realizing metacognitive AI requires addressing nontrivial theoretical and implementation challenges, and the provided framework is intended as a stepping stone for broader community research and development in this direction.

title: Response-Conditioned Parallel-to-Sequential Orchestration for Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Multi-agent systems can solve complex tasks through collaboration between multiple Large Language Model agents. Existing collaboration frameworks typically operate in either a parallel or a sequential mode. In the parallel mode, agents respond independently to queries followed by aggregation of responses. In contrast, sequential systems allow agents to communicate via a directed topology and refine one another step by step. However, both modes are inadequate for achieving the desired objectives of minimizing communication and latency while simultaneously maximizing the accuracy of the final response. In this work, we introduce a hybrid paradigm called Nexa, a trainable response-conditioned policy that bridges the gap between the two modes. Nexa begins with a parallel execution stage, embeds the resulting responses into a shared semantic space, and then predicts a sparse directed acyclic communication graph. If the graph is empty, the system remains purely parallel; if it is non-empty, the system performs one sequential message propagation. The policy is a lightweight transformer model, and the method avoids the need for external LLM judges or reward models, as well as hand-crafted test-time topology search. We formalize this hybrid execution problem, show that the resulting graph is acyclic by construction, and that the framework strictly subsumes pure parallel execution, and present a training procedure based on policy-gradient optimization. Results demonstrate that the response-conditioned policy learned by Nexa under one setting can be reused when the number of agents, the task, or the underlying agent changes, thus emphasizing the generalizability of the learned communication policy.

AI.tldr: Proposes Nexa, a hybrid multi-agent LLM collaboration paradigm combining parallel and limited sequential communication via a learned response-conditioned policy to improve accuracy with low communication and latency.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-agent LLM collaboration frameworks are either purely parallel or sequential. Parallel modes are fast and communication-efficient but can miss gains from agents refining each other. Sequential modes allow richer interaction but increase latency and communication overhead. There is a need for a principled framework that can trade off these extremes—remaining parallel when possible while enabling targeted sequential communication only when beneficial—without relying on external judges, reward models, or hand-crafted communication topologies.
AI.method: Introduce Nexa, a trainable response-conditioned communication policy. The system first runs all agents in parallel to produce responses, which are then embedded into a shared semantic space. A lightweight transformer policy consumes these embeddings and predicts a sparse directed acyclic graph (DAG) describing which agents should send messages to which others for one additional refinement step. If the predicted DAG is empty, the system is purely parallel; if non-empty, a single round of sequential message propagation occurs following the DAG. The graph is constructed to be acyclic by design, and the overall framework strictly generalizes pure parallel execution. The policy is trained via policy-gradient reinforcement learning, without external LLM judges, explicit reward models, or test-time search over topologies.
AI.result: Experiments show that Nexa learns effective communication topologies that improve final answer accuracy while maintaining low latency and limited communication. The learned policy is robust and reusable: a policy trained in one configuration (e.g., specific number of agents, task type, or base LLM agents) can be transferred to different numbers of agents, different tasks, or different underlying LLMs, with retained benefits, demonstrating strong generalization of the communication strategy.
AI.conclusion: Nexa offers a principled hybrid collaboration paradigm for multi-agent LLM systems that unifies parallel and constrained sequential execution under a learned, response-conditioned communication policy. By predicting sparse DAG communication graphs based on initial parallel responses and training this policy with policy-gradient methods, Nexa improves accuracy while controlling communication and latency. Its ability to generalize across agent counts, tasks, and base models suggests that learned communication policies can serve as reusable, plug-and-play components for efficient multi-agent LLM systems.

title: STAR: A Stage-attributed Triage and Repair framework for RCA Agents in Microservices
summary:
LLM-based root cause analysis (RCA) agents have recently emerged as a promising paradigm for incident diagnosis in microservice AIOps. However, their reliability remains fragile: an error in early evidence collection, hypothesis formulation, or causal analysis can propagate through the reasoning trace and eventually corrupt the final diagnosis. In this paper, we present \textbf{STAR}, a \emph{Stage-attributed Triage and Repair} framework for repairing erroneous RCA traces. STAR explicitly decomposes an RCA workflow into four structured stages, namely \emph{Evidence Package} (EP), \emph{Hypothesis Set} (HS), \emph{Analysis Structure} (AS), and \emph{Decision Report} (DR), and treats agent failure as a stage-localizable reasoning bug rather than a monolithic end-to-end error. Built on top of LangGraph, STAR performs stage-wise auditing, budget-aware \emph{Fast/Slow Routing}, \emph{decisive stage localization via counterfactual candidate evaluation}, and stage-specific patch-and-replay repair.
  We evaluate STAR on a public large-scale benchmark and a real-world production dataset, using two RCA agent workflows and three foundation models. Experimental results show that STAR consistently improves both root cause localization and fault type classification over strong baselines. Moreover, STAR identifies the decisive faulty stage with high accuracy, repairs most initially incorrect traces within one or two replay rounds, and benefits substantially from both Fast/Slow Routing and counterfactual stage evaluation. These results suggest that explicitly modeling \emph{where} an RCA agent fails is an effective path toward reliable, debuggable, and self-repairing agentic RCA systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces STAR, a framework that debugs and repairs multi-stage LLM-based root cause analysis traces for microservice incidents, improving reliability by localizing and fixing stage-specific reasoning errors.
AI.motivation: LLM-based root cause analysis agents are promising for diagnosing incidents in microservice-based AIOps, but they are fragile: an early mistake in evidence gathering, hypothesis generation, or causal reasoning can cascade through the reasoning trace and corrupt the final diagnosis. There is a need for a systematic way to detect, localize, and repair such reasoning errors so that RCA agents become more reliable and debuggable.
AI.method: The authors propose STAR (Stage-attributed Triage and Repair), which decomposes an RCA workflow into four explicit stages: Evidence Package (EP), Hypothesis Set (HS), Analysis Structure (AS), and Decision Report (DR). Built on LangGraph, STAR performs stage-wise auditing of traces, uses a budget-aware Fast/Slow Routing mechanism, applies decisive stage localization via counterfactual evaluation of alternative stage candidates, and then conducts stage-specific patch-and-replay repair to iteratively correct faulty traces.
AI.result: On a public large-scale benchmark and a real production dataset, across two RCA workflows and three foundation models, STAR increases both root cause localization accuracy and fault type classification accuracy over strong baselines. It accurately pinpoints the faulty stage, usually repairs incorrect traces within one or two replay iterations, and gains additional performance from the Fast/Slow Routing strategy and counterfactual stage evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly decomposing RCA agents into well-defined stages and treating failures as localized stage-specific bugs enables effective diagnosis and repair of reasoning traces. The STAR framework demonstrates that stage-attributed triage and repair can substantially enhance the reliability, debuggability, and self-repair capability of LLM-based RCA systems for microservice AIOps.

title: See Before You Code: Learning Visual Priors for Spatially Aware Educational Animation Generation
summary: Large language models can generate executable code for educational animations, but the resulting renders often exhibit visual defects, including element overlap, misalignment, and broken animation continuity. These defects cannot be reliably detected from the code alone and become apparent only after execution. We formalize this problem as render-feedback-aware constrained code generation: given a natural language specification, the model must generate executable code whose rendered output satisfies structured quality criteria that can be evaluated only after rendering. To address this problem, we introduce OmniManim, a render-feedback-aware educational animation generation framework built around a shared scene state, explicit visual planning, structured post-render diagnostics, and localized repair. Within OmniManim, the Vision Agent is a task-specific visual planning module: it predicts sparse keyframe layouts with coarse-to-fine bounding-box denoising and optimizes an interpolation-aware objective to reduce intermediate-frame failures induced by downstream animation interpolation. We further construct two datasets, ManimLayout-1K and EduRequire-500, and provide a reproducible evaluation protocol covering executability, instructional quality, visual quality, and efficiency. On EduRequire-500, OmniManim improves measured render quality over both single-model baselines and existing multi-agent frameworks. Systematic ablation studies further verify that explicit visual planning, especially its coarse spatial prior, bounding-box refinement, and interpolation-aware optimization, is central to these gains.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces OmniManim, a framework that uses render feedback, explicit visual planning, and localized repair to generate higher-quality educational animations from natural language using large language models.
AI.motivation: LLM-generated code for educational animations often leads to visually defective renders (overlaps, misalignment, broken animation continuity) that cannot be detected from code alone and only appear after execution. There is a need for a method that leverages render-time feedback and structured criteria to ensure high-quality rendered animations.
AI.method: The authors formalize the task as render-feedback-aware constrained code generation: given a natural language specification, generate executable code whose outputs meet structured, post-render quality criteria. They propose OmniManim, which uses a shared scene state, explicit visual planning, structured post-render diagnostics, and localized code repair. A key component, the Vision Agent, performs task-specific visual planning by predicting sparse keyframe layouts via coarse-to-fine bounding-box denoising and optimizing an interpolation-aware objective to reduce failures caused by animation interpolation. They also introduce two datasets (ManimLayout-1K and EduRequire-500) and a standardized evaluation protocol.
AI.result: On the EduRequire-500 benchmark, OmniManim yields better measured render quality compared with single-model baselines and existing multi-agent frameworks across executability, instructional quality, visual quality, and efficiency. Ablation studies show that components like explicit visual planning, coarse spatial priors, bounding-box refinement, and interpolation-aware optimization each contribute significantly to the performance gains.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating render feedback, explicit visual planning, and localized repair into the animation code-generation pipeline substantially improves the visual and instructional quality of LLM-generated educational animations. The proposed OmniManim framework and associated datasets provide a foundation and evaluation setup for further work on render-feedback-aware code generation and robust visual animation synthesis from language.

title: MHGraphBench: Knowledge Graph-Grounded Benchmarking of Mental Health Knowledge in Large Language Models
comment: Accepted to GEM 2026, ACL 2026 Workshop; 9 pages main text plus references and appendices
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used in the mental health domain, yet it remains unclear how well they capture related biomedical knowledge and how reliably they apply it to clinically salient structured judgments. Here, we present a knowledge-graph (KG)-grounded benchmark for assessing LLMs on mental-health entity recognition, relation judgment, and two-hop reasoning. The benchmark is derived from PrimeKG and comprises nine task families with KG-supported answers and controlled negative options. Experiments across 15 closed- and open-source LLMs reveal a persistent recognition-to-judgment gap: leading models achieve near-ceiling performance on entity typing and on the small relation-typing subset, yet they still struggle with relation prediction and two-hop reasoning. Additionally, short KG-derived snippets benefit some models but degrade performance for others. Moreover, output-format reliability can substantially influence measured performance under constrained multiple-choice settings, highlighting the critical role of response validity in benchmark-based evaluation. MHGraphBench should therefore be interpreted as evaluating agreement with a curated mental-health slice of PrimeKG under a constrained multiple-choice interface, rather than as a direct assessment of real-world clinical safety.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MHGraphBench, a knowledge-graph-grounded benchmark to evaluate how well large language models handle mental-health-related biomedical knowledge, showing that models recognize entities well but struggle with relation prediction, multi-hop reasoning, and reliable output formatting.
AI.motivation: Although large language models are widely applied to mental health tasks, it is not well understood whether they accurately encode and reliably use underlying biomedical and clinical knowledge. Existing evaluations often focus on surface-level abilities or unstructured outputs, making it difficult to assess structured clinical judgments or reasoning grounded in established biomedical resources. The authors aim to create a rigorous, KG-based benchmark to quantify these capabilities and limitations in a mental-health context.
AI.method: The authors construct a benchmark, MHGraphBench, grounded in the PrimeKG biomedical knowledge graph. They design nine task families targeting mental-health-relevant entity recognition, relation judgment, and two-hop reasoning. Each task includes knowledge-graph-supported correct answers and carefully controlled negative options, formatted as constrained multiple-choice questions. They then evaluate 15 large language models (both closed- and open-source) on these tasks, with and without short KG-derived textual snippets, and analyze both accuracy and the reliability of models’ adherence to the required output format.
AI.result: Across models, there is a clear recognition-to-judgment gap: top-performing LLMs nearly perfect entity typing and perform very well on the small subset of relation-typing tasks, but their performance drops notably on relation prediction and two-hop reasoning tasks. The effect of adding short KG-derived snippets is mixed—helping some models while hurting others. The authors also find that models’ tendency to violate the specified answer format can significantly distort measured performance in constrained multiple-choice evaluations.
AI.conclusion: MHGraphBench reveals that current LLMs, even strong ones, still struggle to translate mental-health-related biomedical knowledge into reliable relation-level judgments and multi-hop reasoning, despite strong entity recognition. Because the benchmark reflects agreement with a curated slice of PrimeKG under a strict multiple-choice interface, it should not be interpreted as a direct proxy for clinical safety. Instead, it serves as a controlled diagnostic tool to probe knowledge usage, reasoning, and response validity for mental-health applications of LLMs.

title: TopoEvo: A Topology-Aware Self-Evolving Multi-Agent Framework for Root Cause Analysis in Microservices
summary: Root cause analysis (RCA) in microservices is challenging due to (i) noisy and heterogeneous multimodal observability (metrics, logs, traces), (ii) cascading failure propagation that amplifies downstream symptoms, and (iii) non-stationary topology drift induced by autoscaling and rolling updates. Recent LLM-based RCA agents can generate tool-grounded explanations, yet they often remain topology-agnostic and suffer from \emph{symptom-amplification bias}, misattributing the root cause to salient downstream victims. We propose \textbf{TopoEvo}, a topology-aware self-evolving multi-agent framework that couples graph representation learning with structured, topology-constrained reasoning. TopoEvo first introduces \emph{Metric-orthogonal Multimodal Alignment} (MOMA), which decomposes metric embeddings into complementary subspaces and contrastively aligns logs and traces to reduce modality redundancy and sparsity, yielding stable node representations for graph encoding. It then applies \emph{Vector Quantization} (VQ) to discretize topology-enhanced states into auditable \emph{symptom tokens} with a symptom lexicon, enabling reliable retrieval and token-level evidence grounding. On top of these discrete topology cues, TopoEvo performs a multi-agent \emph{Hypothesis--Evidence--Test} (HET) workflow to explicitly verify propagation-consistent explanations and separate initiating anomalies from amplified downstream symptoms. Finally, a \emph{Self-Evolving Mechanism} refreshes hierarchical incident memory and performs conservative test-time adaptation with high-confidence pseudo-labels to maintain robustness under drift.

AI.tldr: TopoEvo is a topology-aware, self-evolving multi-agent system for root cause analysis in microservices that fuses metrics, logs, and traces, encodes them as graph-based symptom tokens, and uses structured reasoning plus continual adaptation to distinguish true root causes from propagated symptoms.
AI.motivation: Existing root cause analysis in microservices is hard because observability data are noisy and heterogeneous, failures propagate across services and obscure the true origin, and the system topology changes over time due to autoscaling and deployments. Recent LLM-based RCA agents can use tools but tend to ignore topology and are biased toward blaming highly affected downstream services instead of the real initiator. The paper aims to build an RCA framework that is explicitly topology-aware, robust to drift, and able to counter this symptom-amplification bias.
AI.method: The authors propose TopoEvo, which: (1) builds stable node representations by Metric-orthogonal Multimodal Alignment (MOMA), decomposing metric embeddings into complementary subspaces and contrastively aligning log and trace representations to reduce redundancy and sparsity; (2) augments these with graph/topology information and applies Vector Quantization to discretize states into a finite set of interpretable "symptom tokens" stored in a symptom lexicon for retrieval and evidence grounding; (3) runs a multi-agent Hypothesis–Evidence–Test (HET) reasoning workflow that uses these topology-aware tokens to generate, support, and test explanations while enforcing propagation consistency to separate initiators from downstream victims; and (4) introduces a Self-Evolving Mechanism that updates hierarchical incident memory and performs conservative test-time adaptation using high-confidence pseudo-labels to stay robust under topology and behavior drift.
AI.result: TopoEvo can generate topology-consistent, tool-grounded RCA explanations that better distinguish initiating anomalies from propagated symptoms, mitigating symptom-amplification bias. The discrete symptom tokens and lexicon provide interpretable, auditable evidence for decisions, while the self-evolving adaptation maintains performance under topology drift. (Concrete quantitative results are not provided in the abstract.)
AI.conclusion: Coupling graph representation learning and multimodal alignment with a topology-constrained, multi-agent reasoning process and conservative online adaptation yields a more robust and interpretable RCA framework for microservices. By encoding incidents as discrete, topology-aware symptom tokens and explicitly verifying failure propagation paths, TopoEvo addresses limitations of prior LLM-based RCA agents that ignore topology and overfit to prominent downstream symptoms.

title: Syntax Without Semantics: Teaching Large Language Models to Code in an Unseen Language
summary: Large language models (LLMs) achieve high pass rates on code generation benchmarks, yet whether they can transfer this ability to languages absent from pretraining remains poorly understood. We introduce PyLang, a minimal imperative language absent from all pretraining corpora, and evaluate frontier models zero-shot and fine-tuned Qwen3 (4B, 8B, 32B) on 352 problems. We find that fine-tuning quickly teaches syntax but fails to transfer semantic competence: Python outperforms PyLang by up to 19% across all configurations, and no intervention (multi-task learning, preference tuning, code infilling, or latent-space objectives) closes the gap. An LLM judge reveals that frontier models select an identical algorithm to Python 80% of the time, yet cannot translate it into a working PyLang implementation., and CKA analysis confirms that fine-tuned models converge to nearly identical internal representations across languages (CKA > 0.97) while diverging at the output stage. We term this the implementation fidelity gap: models possess language-agnostic algorithmic understanding but cannot express it in an unfamiliar language. Our findings highlight the need for training methods that decouple reasoning from language-specific realization.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that large language models can understand algorithms in a language-agnostic way but struggle to correctly implement them in a programming language they have never seen before, even after fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs perform well on standard code benchmarks, it is unclear whether this reflects genuine, transferable algorithmic understanding or simply memorization and pattern-matching in familiar languages. The authors want to test if models can transfer coding ability to a truly novel language absent from pretraining, thereby isolating language-agnostic reasoning from language-specific surface forms.
AI.method: The authors design PyLang, a minimal imperative programming language guaranteed to be absent from pretraining data. They construct a benchmark of 352 problems and evaluate Qwen3 models (4B, 8B, 32B) on code generation in both Python and PyLang, using zero-shot and various fine-tuning strategies. They attempt multiple interventions—multi-task learning, preference tuning, code infilling, and latent-space objectives. They further use an LLM-based judge to inspect whether models choose the same underlying algorithms across languages and apply CKA (Centered Kernel Alignment) to compare internal representations between Python and PyLang generations.
AI.result: Fine-tuning enables models to quickly learn PyLang syntax but not fully its semantics: across all configurations, performance in Python remains up to 19% higher than in PyLang, and none of the training interventions closes this gap. The LLM judge finds that models pick the same algorithm as in Python about 80% of the time but fail to render it into correct PyLang code. CKA analysis shows that internal representations for Python and PyLang are almost identical (CKA > 0.97), yet outputs differ substantially, indicating a breakdown at the final expression stage.
AI.conclusion: The paper identifies an "implementation fidelity gap": LLMs hold language-agnostic algorithmic understanding but cannot reliably express it in an unseen programming language, even after targeted training.
