This suggests that current training paradigms entangle reasoning with language-specific expression and that new methods are needed to explicitly separate abstract reasoning from the mechanics of implementing solutions in a given language.

title: ColPackAgent: Agent-Skill-Guided Hard-Particle Monte Carlo Workflows for Colloidal Packing
summary: We introduce ColPackAgent, an agent framework that autonomously runs Monte Carlo simulations of colloidal packing through a Model Context Protocol (MCP) tool server and an agent skill, whether as a standalone agent or inside an existing agent system. By harnessing the MCP server and agent skill, ColPackAgent executes a structured workflow for colloidal packing simulations, which are central to studies of phase behavior, self-assembly, and materials design. Without dedicated simulation tools and workflow instructions, general-purpose Large Language Model (LLM) agents tend to describe such workflows rather than execute them reliably. The MCP server exposes a custom-built colpack Python package that wraps HOOMD-blue hard-particle Monte Carlo, and the skill encodes a four-stage workflow contract. ColPackAgent can carry out the workflow interactively with human feedback, autonomously from an end-to-end prompt, or as autoresearch following a provided program file. We demonstrate the system in different modes with several colloidal packing simulation examples such as cube particles in 3D, a binary system of disks and capsules in 2D, and the 2D hard-disk freezing transition using autoresearch. We also compare model performance on this workflow across a panel of LLMs with 17 stage-specific prompts. This benchmark provides a stage-level check of how reliably different models follow the setup, planning, and analysis workflow. Together, these results show that pairing a domain Python package with MCP tools and a portable agent skill provides a practical route for turning a simulation toolkit into an agent-assisted research workflow.

AI.tldr: ColPackAgent is an agent framework that connects a colloidal packing Monte Carlo simulation toolkit to LLM-based agents via MCP tools and a standardized workflow, enabling agents to reliably run, not just describe, complex simulation studies.
AI.motivation: General-purpose LLM agents, without specialized tools and workflows, typically can only describe how to run colloidal packing simulations rather than execute them in a reliable, reproducible way. Colloidal packing simulations are crucial for studying phase behavior, self-assembly, and materials design, but existing simulation packages are not directly integrated with agent frameworks. There is a need for a systematic, portable way to turn a domain-specific simulation code into an agent-usable research workflow that supports human-in-the-loop, fully autonomous, and programmable autoresearch modes, while also enabling benchmarking of different LLMs on their ability to follow complex multi-stage workflows.
AI.method: The authors build ColPackAgent, which uses a Model Context Protocol (MCP) tool server that exposes a custom colpack Python package wrapping HOOMD-blue hard-particle Monte Carlo simulations. On top of this, they define an agent "skill" that encodes a four-stage workflow contract for colloidal packing studies. The system can be run as a standalone agent or embedded in existing agent systems. They design workflows where the agent interacts with the MCP tools to set up, run, and analyze simulations, either interactively with human feedback, end-to-end from a single prompt, or via an autoresearch mode that follows a program file. They then construct a benchmark using 17 stage-specific prompts to test multiple LLMs on how well they execute each stage of the workflow.
AI.result: ColPackAgent successfully runs colloidal packing Monte Carlo simulations across several representative systems, including 3D cube particles, 2D binary mixtures of disks and capsules, and the 2D hard-disk freezing transition. The framework operates effectively in interactive, fully autonomous, and autoresearch modes. The benchmarking experiments comparing different LLMs with 17 stage-specific prompts provide a granular, stage-level assessment of how reliably each model follows the workflow for setup, planning, execution, and analysis. The results show that integrating the colpack package via MCP tools plus the agent skill substantially improves the ability of LLM agents to execute, not merely describe, the simulation workflow.
AI.conclusion: Pairing a domain-specific Python simulation package with MCP tools and a standardized, portable agent skill enables practical, agent-assisted research workflows for colloidal packing simulations. This design turns an existing simulation toolkit into an executable workflow that LLM agents can reliably drive in different operation modes. The stage-based benchmark further establishes a method for evaluating LLMs on complex scientific workflows. Overall, ColPackAgent demonstrates that structured tool exposure and workflow contracts are an effective pattern for integrating scientific simulation codes into autonomous or semi-autonomous LLM-driven research pipelines.

title: PSD: Pushing the Pareto Frontier of Diffusion LLMs via Parallel Speculative Decoding
summary: Diffusion large language models (dLLMs) generate text by iteratively denoising masked token sequences. Although dLLMs can predict all masked positions in parallel within each step, the large number of denoising iterations still makes inference expensive. This cost can be reduced spatially by unmasking multiple tokens per step, or temporally by collapsing multiple denoising steps into one verification call. We propose Parallel Speculative Decoding (PSD), a training-free framework that jointly improves inference along both axes. Using the confidence scores from a single forward pass, PSD selects positions to unmask via a configurable, adaptive unmasking policy and constructs multi-depth speculative drafts without extra model calls. A final batched verification pass then applies hierarchical acceptance, keeping the deepest draft that remains consistent with the updated predictions. Experiments on three dLLMs across reasoning and code generation tasks show that PSD achieves favorable trade-offs between inference efficiency and generation quality, reaching up to $5.5\times$ tokens per forward pass with accuracy comparable to greedy decoding.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Parallel Speculative Decoding (PSD), a training-free framework that accelerates diffusion large language models by parallel unmasking and speculative multi-step drafting, maintaining similar accuracy to greedy decoding while achieving up to 5.5× more tokens per forward pass.
AI.motivation: Diffusion large language models require many denoising iterations to generate text, making inference expensive despite being able to predict all masked positions in parallel within a single step. Existing acceleration approaches typically improve either spatial efficiency (unmasking more tokens per step) or temporal efficiency (reducing the number of denoising steps), but not both simultaneously. There is a need for a method that jointly improves both dimensions of efficiency without retraining the model or significantly degrading generation quality.
AI.method: The authors propose Parallel Speculative Decoding (PSD), a training-free decoding framework. PSD uses the confidence scores from a single forward pass of a diffusion LLM to: (1) select which masked positions to unmask according to a configurable, adaptive unmasking policy (spatial acceleration), and (2) construct multi-depth speculative drafts that represent multiple future denoising steps without requiring additional model calls (temporal acceleration). After constructing these speculative drafts, the model performs a single batched verification forward pass. Using hierarchical acceptance, it selects the deepest draft whose tokens remain consistent with the updated model predictions; earlier drafts are considered fallbacks if inconsistencies are found.
AI.result: In experiments on three diffusion large language models across reasoning and code generation benchmarks, PSD provides improved inference efficiency with limited quality loss. The framework attains up to 5.5× more generated tokens per forward pass compared with standard greedy decoding, while maintaining comparable accuracy levels. The results highlight favorable trade-offs between decoding speed and output quality versus baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: Parallel Speculative Decoding effectively accelerates diffusion large language models by combining spatial and temporal inference optimizations within a single, training-free framework. By leveraging per-token confidence scores to guide adaptive unmasking and multi-depth speculative drafts, PSD significantly increases tokens-per-forward-pass while preserving accuracy close to greedy decoding. This shows that speculative, parallel decoding tailored to diffusion-style LLMs can deliver substantial practical speedups without retraining or heavily modifying existing models.

title: Toward LLMs Beyond English-Centric Development
summary: Through an analysis of sequences generated by open-weight large language models (LLMs), we demonstrate that LLMs are heavily biased toward English. While continual pre-training is commonly used to adapt LLMs to a target language, we show that it does not offer a cost advantage over training from scratch, even for improving cultural understanding in the target language. These findings suggest that dedicated per-language investment may become increasingly important for future LLM development, rather than relying primarily on the expansion of English-centric resources.

AI.tldr: The paper finds that current open-weight large language models are strongly biased toward English, and that continually pre-training them on another language is not more cost-effective than training a new model from scratch for that language.
AI.motivation: To understand how well open-weight LLMs support non-English languages, and to evaluate whether the common strategy of continual pre-training on a target language is an efficient way to improve language and cultural competence compared to training dedicated models from scratch.
AI.method: Analyze sequences generated by open-weight LLMs to quantify their bias toward English and then compare the effectiveness and cost of continual pre-training on a target language versus training new models from scratch in that language, including evaluation of cultural understanding in the target language.
AI.result: The analysis shows that open-weight LLMs exhibit a strong bias toward English and that continual pre-training on a target language does not provide a cost advantage over training a model from scratch, even when the goal is to improve cultural understanding in that target language.
AI.conclusion: Relying mainly on scaling English-centric resources and then adapting via continual pre-training may not be optimal for building strong non-English LLMs; instead, dedicated investment in separate per-language models or training regimes is likely to become increasingly important for future LLM development.

title: Nudging Beyond the Comfort Zone: Efficient Strategy-Guided Exploration for RLVR
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a scalable paradigm for improving the reasoning capabilities of large language models. However, its effectiveness is fundamentally limited by exploration: the policy can only improve on trajectories it has already sampled. While increasing the number of rollouts alleviates this issue, such brute-force scaling is computationally expensive, and existing approaches that modify the optimization objective provide limited control over what is explored. In this work, we propose NudgeRL, a framework for structured and diversity-driven exploration in RLVR. Our approach introduces Strategy Nudging, which conditions each rollout on lightweight, strategy-level contexts to induce diverse reasoning trajectories without relying on expensive oracle supervision. To effectively learn from such structured exploration, we further propose a unified objective, which decomposes the reward signal into inter- and intra-context components and incorporates a distillation objective to transfer discovered behaviors back to the base policy. Empirically, NudgeRL outperforms standard GRPO with up to 8 times larger rollout budgets, while outperforming oracle-guided RL baseline on average across five challenging math benchmarks. These results demonstrate that structured, context-driven exploration can serve as an efficient and scalable alternative to both brute-force rollout scaling and feasibility-oriented methods based on privileged information. Our code is available at https://github.com/tally0818/NudgeRL.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces NudgeRL, a reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) framework that improves exploration efficiency and reasoning performance in large language models through structured, strategy-conditioned rollouts and a unified learning objective.
AI.motivation: Existing RLVR methods for improving LLM reasoning are limited by inefficient exploration, because the policy can only improve on already-sampled trajectories. Simply increasing rollout counts is computationally expensive, and current objective tweaks do not provide fine-grained control over what is explored. There is a need for a scalable, controllable way to induce diverse and useful reasoning behaviors without expensive oracle guidance.
AI.method: The authors propose NudgeRL, which uses Strategy Nudging: each rollout is conditioned on lightweight, high-level strategy contexts that steer the model toward diverse reasoning trajectories. They then design a unified learning objective that (1) decomposes rewards into inter-context and intra-context components to separately capture cross-strategy and within-strategy performance, and (2) includes a distillation term to transfer successful behaviors discovered under nudged contexts back into the base, unconditioned policy.
AI.result: On five challenging math benchmarks, NudgeRL achieves better performance than standard GRPO even when GRPO is given up to 8× more rollouts, and it also outperforms an oracle-guided RL baseline on average. This indicates that structured, context-based exploration can yield stronger reasoning gains at lower computational cost than brute-force rollout scaling or methods that require privileged oracle information.
AI.conclusion: Structured, context-driven exploration via Strategy Nudging and a unified inter-/intra-context objective provides an efficient and scalable alternative to naive rollout scaling and oracle-driven feasibility methods in RLVR. NudgeRL improves LLM reasoning by better exploring and then distilling diverse strategies back into the core policy, suggesting a promising direction for scalable RL-based alignment and reasoning enhancement.

title: Evaluating Chinese Ambiguity Understanding in Large Language Models
summary: Linguistic ambiguity is critical to the robustness of Large Language Models (LLMs), yet existing research focuses mostly on English, with limited attention devoted to Chinese. Existing Chinese ambiguity datasets (e.g., CHAmbi) suffer from poor scalability. Guided by Potential Ambiguity (PA) Theory, we design a semi-automatic pipeline to construct CHA-Gen. It is the first PA Theory-grounded Chinese ambiguity dataset, which comprises 5,712 sentences (2,414 ambiguous, 3,298 unambiguous) across 18 potential ambiguous structures. Evaluating LLMs (e.g. Gemma 3, Qwen 2.5/3 series) via direct querying and machine translation, we find that LLMs struggle with ambiguity detection (improved by CoT prompting). Analysis of Qwen3-32B's CoT rationales reveals three common failure modes: ambiguity blindness, misattribution, and premature resolution. Uncertainty quantification with semantic entropy metric shows higher uncertainty for ambiguous sentences. Moreover, instruction tuning induces overconfidence, whereas Base models better capture semantic diversity. We further observe that models exhibit a bias toward dominant interpretations. Our work provides a scalable approach for Chinese ambiguity corpus and insights into LLMs' ambiguity handling, laying a foundation for enhancing Chinese ambiguity research in LLMs.

AI.tldr: They build a scalable, theory-grounded Chinese linguistic ambiguity dataset and use it to analyze how LLMs detect and handle ambiguity, revealing weaknesses, overconfidence, and interpretation biases.
AI.motivation: Most ambiguity research and datasets are in English, and existing Chinese ambiguity datasets are small and hard to scale. There is a need for a larger, systematically constructed Chinese ambiguity corpus, grounded in linguistic theory, to rigorously study how LLMs deal with ambiguity in Chinese.
AI.method: Using Potential Ambiguity (PA) Theory, they design a semi-automatic pipeline to construct CHA-Gen, a Chinese ambiguity dataset with both ambiguous and unambiguous sentences spanning 18 ambiguity-related structures. They evaluate several LLMs (Gemma 3, Qwen 2.5/3 series) via direct questioning and via machine-translation-based evaluation, test chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting, analyze CoT rationales of Qwen3-32B to categorize error modes, and apply a semantic-entropy-based uncertainty metric to study model uncertainty and the effects of instruction tuning.
AI.result: They obtain CHA-Gen with 5,712 Chinese sentences, of which 2,414 are ambiguous and 3,298 unambiguous, covering 18 types of potentially ambiguous structures. LLMs perform poorly on ambiguity detection, though CoT prompting improves performance. Analysis of Qwen3-32B's CoT explanations reveals three main failure modes: failing to see ambiguity at all, attributing ambiguity incorrectly, and committing too early to one reading. Semantic entropy is higher for ambiguous sentences, instruction-tuned models are more overconfident and less reflective of semantic diversity, base models better preserve diversity, and models overall prefer dominant interpretations.
AI.conclusion: CHA-Gen offers a scalable, PA-theory-based Chinese ambiguity dataset and a framework for evaluating LLMs' handling of Chinese ambiguity. The findings show that current LLMs struggle with recognizing and representing ambiguity, tend to be overconfident and biased toward dominant interpretations, and that CoT and base (non-instruction-tuned) models can better reflect underlying semantic uncertainty. This work establishes groundwork and tools for improving Chinese ambiguity modeling in LLMs.

title: Can We Trust AI-Inferred User States. A Psychometric Framework for Validating the Reliability of Users States Classification by LLMs in Operational Environments
comment: Full survey article with data tables for futher possible replicabilty and comparison
summary:
The use of large language models to assess user states in conversational and adaptive systems is based on the assumption that the metrics used for such assessment are stable and interpretable at the level of individual scores. This paper empirically tests this assumption, focusing on the psychometric reliability of artificial intelligence (AI) measures of user states.
  This study employed replication evaluation procedures to assess the repeatability of a broad set of metrics across three different bimodal large language models (GPT-4o audio, Gemini 2.0 Flash, Gemini 2.5 Flash). Analyses include both individual score reliability and aggregated reliability, allowing us to distinguish metrics potentially useful for real-time adaptation from those that retain their value only in aggregated analyses.
  The results demonstrate that metric reliability cannot be considered a default property in interpretive domains. The lack of stability at the level of individual scores precludes the interpretation of such scores as indicators of user state in real-time adaptive systems, even if these metrics demonstrate stability after aggregation. At the same time, the study indicates that individually unstable metrics can retain analytical utility in post-hoc studies, identifying rules governing interactions and their relationships with user experience parameters such as satisfaction, trust, and engagement.
  The main contribution of this work, besides quantifying the severity of the problem (only 31 of 213 metrics met the criteria), is the proposal of a replicable evaluation framework, enabling measurable evaluations of metric applicability. This approach supports more responsible AI design of adaptive systems, in which the interpretation of results requires explicit validation of reliability and monitoring for violations over time.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether AI-generated metrics of user states in conversational systems are reliable at the individual-score level and proposes an evaluation framework after finding that most metrics are not reliably repeatable.
AI.motivation: Many adaptive and conversational systems now use large language models to infer user states (e.g., satisfaction, trust, engagement) from interactions, assuming that the metrics produced are stable and interpretable for individuals. However, this assumption has not been thoroughly tested, especially regarding psychometric reliability and repeatability of these AI-based measures at both individual and aggregate levels.
AI.method: The authors use replication evaluation procedures to test the repeatability of a wide range of AI-generated user-state metrics across three bimodal LLMs (GPT-4o audio, Gemini 2.0 Flash, Gemini 2.5 Flash). They analyze reliability at the level of individual scores and in aggregated forms, classifying metrics into those potentially suitable for real-time adaptation versus those only suitable for post-hoc or aggregated analyses.
AI.result: They find that metric reliability is not guaranteed in interpretive domains: most metrics are unstable at the individual-score level, which undermines their use as real-time indicators of user state. Only 31 out of 213 examined metrics meet their reliability criteria. However, some metrics that are unreliable individually still show stability and usefulness when aggregated, maintaining value for post-hoc analyses of interaction patterns and their relationship to user experience variables like satisfaction, trust, and engagement.
AI.conclusion: AI-based user-state metrics cannot be assumed to be reliable for individual-level, real-time adaptive decisions. Reliability must be explicitly validated and monitored over time. The paper contributes both an empirical quantification of the reliability problem (demonstrating that the majority of metrics fail) and a replicable evaluation framework that allows practitioners to assess which metrics are sufficiently stable for different uses, supporting more responsible design of adaptive AI systems.

title: Dynamic Chunking for Diffusion Language Models
summary: Block discrete diffusion language models factorize a sequence autoregressively over fixed-size positional blocks, decoupling within-block parallel denoising from across-block conditioning. We argue that this rigid partition wastes structure already present in the sequence: blocks defined by position rather than by content separate semantically coherent tokens and group unrelated ones together. We introduce the \textbf{D}ynamic \textbf{C}hunking \textbf{D}iffusion \textbf{M}odel (DCDM), which replaces positional blocks with content-defined semantic chunks. At its core is Chunking Attention, a differentiable layer that routes tokens into $K$ clusters parameterized by learnable subspaces and shaped end-to-end by the diffusion objective. The resulting cluster assignments induce a chunk-causal attention mask under which a discrete diffusion denoiser factorizes the sequence likelihood autoregressively over semantic chunks, strictly generalizing block discrete diffusion. On downstream benchmarks at parameter scales up to 1.5B, DCDM consistently improves over both unstructured and positional-block diffusion baselines, with the advantage stable across scales and visible early in training.

AI.tldr: They propose Dynamic Chunking Diffusion Models (DCDM), which replace fixed positional blocks in block discrete diffusion language models with learned, content-based semantic chunks, improving modeling and downstream performance.
AI.motivation: Existing block discrete diffusion language models partition sequences into fixed-size positional blocks. This rigid partitioning ignores semantic structure in the sequence, often splitting coherent phrases across blocks and grouping unrelated tokens together, which can waste modeling capacity and limit performance.
AI.method: They introduce DCDM, which uses content-defined semantic chunks instead of positional blocks. The key component is Chunking Attention, a differentiable mechanism that routes tokens into K clusters (chunks) defined by learnable subspaces. These clusters are learned end-to-end via the diffusion training objective. The learned cluster assignments then define a chunk-causal attention mask, under which a discrete diffusion denoiser factorizes the sequence likelihood autoregressively over semantic chunks. This framework generalizes standard block discrete diffusion by allowing structure to emerge from content rather than fixed positions.
AI.result: On multiple downstream benchmarks and for models up to 1.5B parameters, DCDM outperforms both unstructured diffusion models and positional-block-based discrete diffusion baselines. The performance gains are consistent across model scales and appear early in training, indicating improved sample efficiency and robustness of the approach.
AI.conclusion: Replacing rigid positional blocks with learned, content-based semantic chunks leads to better use of sequence structure in discrete diffusion language models. DCDM’s Chunking Attention provides a flexible, end-to-end-learned chunking mechanism that strictly generalizes block diffusion and yields consistent empirical improvements on language modeling benchmarks.

title: ALSO: Adversarial Online Strategy Optimization for Social Agents
summary: Social simulation provides a compelling testbed for studying social intelligence, where agents interact through multi-turn dialogues under evolving contexts and strategically adapting opponents. Such environments are inherently non-stationary, requiring agents to dynamically adjust their strategies over time. However, most Large Language Model (LLM) based social agents rely on static personas, while existing approaches for enhancing social intelligence, such as offline reinforcement learning or external planners, are ill-suited to these settings, typically assuming stationarity and incurring substantial training overhead. To bridge this gap, we propose \textbf{ALSO} (\textbf{A}dversarial on\textbf{L}ine \textbf{S}trategy \textbf{O}ptimization), the first framework for online strategy optimization in multi-agent social simulation. ALSO advances social adaptation through two key contributions. (1) ALSO formulates multi-turn interaction as an adversarial bandit problem, where combinations of static personas and dynamic strategy instructions are treated as arms, providing a principled solution to non-stationarity without relying on environmental stability assumptions. (2) To predict rewards and generalize sparse feedback in multi-turn dialogues, ALSO introduces a lightweight neural surrogate to predict rewards from interaction histories, enabling sample-efficient exploration and continuous online adaptation. Experiments on the Sotopia benchmark demonstrate that ALSO consistently outperforms static baselines and existing optimization methods in dynamic environments, validating the effectiveness of adversarial online strategy optimization for building robust social agents.

AI.tldr: Introduces ALSO, a framework for online strategy optimization of LLM-based agents in non-stationary multi-agent social simulations, modeling interactions as an adversarial bandit and using a neural surrogate for reward prediction to enable adaptive, sample-efficient social behavior.
AI.motivation: Most LLM-based social agents use static personas and prior methods for improving social intelligence (offline RL, external planners) assume stationarity and require heavy training, making them unsuitable for dynamic, non-stationary multi-agent social simulations. There is a need for agents that can adapt their social strategies online as opponents and contexts evolve.
AI.method: Formulate multi-turn social interaction as an adversarial bandit problem where each arm is a combination of a static persona and dynamic strategy instructions. Use an online optimization algorithm over these arms. To address sparse and delayed rewards in dialogues, introduce a lightweight neural surrogate model that predicts rewards based on interaction histories, which guides exploration and enables continuous online adaptation with fewer samples.
AI.result: On the Sotopia social simulation benchmark, ALSO yields consistently better performance than static persona baselines and existing optimization approaches in dynamic, non-stationary environments, demonstrating superior adaptability and robustness of strategies learned online.
AI.conclusion: Adversarial online strategy optimization, instantiated by ALSO, is an effective way to build robust, socially adaptive LLM agents for dynamic multi-agent simulations. Modeling strategy selection as an adversarial bandit and using a neural surrogate for reward prediction together provide a principled, sample-efficient approach to handle non-stationarity in social interactions.

title: VCG-Bench: Towards A Unified Visual-Centric Benchmark for Structured Generation and Editing
summary: Despite the rapid advancements in Vision-Language Models (VLMs), a critical gap remains in their ability to handle structured, controllable diagrammatic tasks essential for professional workflows. Existing methods predominantly rely on pixel-based synthesis, which operates in probabilistic pixel spaces and is inherently limited in editability and fidelity. Instead, we propose a new Diagram-as-Code paradigm with symbolic logic that leverages mxGraph Extensible Markup Language (XML) for precise diagram generation and editing. We present VCG-Bench, a unified benchmark for visual-centric \texttt{mxGraph} tasks. VCG-Bench comprises: (1) a taxonomized dataset of 1,449 diverse diagrams spanning 6 domains and 15 sub-domains, (2) a paradigm definition that integrates Generation (Vision-to-Code) and Editability (Code-to-Code), (3) a Tailored Evaluation Protocol employing multi-dimensional metrics such as \texttt{mxGraph} Execution Success Rate, Style Consistency Score (SCS), etc. Experimental results highlight the challenges faced by current State-of-the-Art (SOTA) VLMs in structured fidelity and instruction compliance, reflecting their vision and reasoning capabilities.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a Diagram-as-Code paradigm using mxGraph XML and a new benchmark (VCG-Bench) to evaluate how well vision-language models can generate and edit structured diagrams symbolically rather than as pixels.
AI.motivation: Vision-Language Models have progressed rapidly but are still poor at professional, structured diagram tasks because current approaches generate diagrams as images (pixels), making them hard to edit, control, and guarantee structural fidelity. There is a need for symbolic, code-level representations and a unified benchmark to rigorously test these capabilities.
AI.method: The authors propose representing diagrams as symbolic code using mxGraph XML instead of pixel images. They build VCG-Bench, a unified benchmark that includes: (1) a curated, taxonomized dataset of 1,449 diagrams across 6 domains and 15 sub-domains; (2) a task paradigm that covers both diagram Generation (Vision-to-Code) and Editability (Code-to-Code); and (3) a tailored evaluation protocol with multi-dimensional metrics such as mxGraph Execution Success Rate and Style Consistency Score to assess structural correctness, style, and instruction following. They then test state-of-the-art VLMs on these tasks.
AI.result: Experiments on VCG-Bench show that current state-of-the-art VLMs struggle with the proposed diagram-as-code tasks, performing poorly in structured fidelity, style consistency, and instruction following according to the new metrics. This reveals substantial limitations in their vision understanding and reasoning when precise symbolic structure is required.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that existing VLMs are not yet capable of high-fidelity, controllable diagram generation and editing at the symbolic level. The proposed Diagram-as-Code paradigm using mxGraph XML, together with VCG-Bench and its evaluation protocol, provides a more realistic and rigorous way to assess and drive progress in visual-centric, structure-aware diagram reasoning and generation.

title: SaaS-Bench: Can Computer-Use Agents Leverage Real-World SaaS to Solve Professional Workflows?
summary: Computer-Using Agents (CUAs) are rapidly extending large language models (LLMs) beyond text-based reasoning toward action execution in more complex environments, such as web browsers and graphical user interfaces (GUIs). However, existing web and GUI agent benchmarks often rely on simplified settings, isolated tasks, or short-horizon interactions, making it difficult to assess capabilities of agents in realistic professional workflows. Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) environments are a natural choice for CUA evaluation, as they host a large share of modern digital work and naturally involve dynamic system states, cross-application coordination, domain-specific knowledge, and long-horizon dependencies. To this end, we introduce SaaS-Bench, a benchmark built on 23 deployable SaaS systems across six professional domains, containing 106 tasks grounded in realistic work scenarios. These tasks require long-horizon execution, cover both text-only and multimodal settings, and are evaluated with weighted verification checkpoints that measure strict task completion and partial progress. Experiments show that representative LLM-based agents struggle on SaaS-Bench, with even the strongest model completing fewer than 4% of tasks end-to-end, exposing limitations in planning, state tracking, cross-application context maintenance, and error recovery. Code are available at https://github.com/UniPat-AI/SaaS-Bench for reproduction.

AI.tldr: SaaS-Bench is a benchmark evaluating computer-using agents in realistic SaaS-based professional workflows, revealing current agents complete under 4% of tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing web and GUI agent benchmarks are too simplified, with isolated, short-horizon tasks that fail to capture the complexity of real professional workflows. There is a need to evaluate computer-using agents in realistic, dynamic environments that reflect how modern digital work is actually done, particularly within SaaS platforms that require long-term planning, domain knowledge, and cross-application coordination.
AI.method: The authors build SaaS-Bench, a benchmark based on 23 deployable SaaS systems across six professional domains. They design 106 tasks grounded in realistic work scenarios that require long-horizon execution and include both text-only and multimodal settings. Performance is measured using weighted verification checkpoints that capture both strict task completion and partial progress. They then evaluate several representative LLM-based computer-using agents on this benchmark.
AI.result: On SaaS-Bench, current LLM-based agents perform poorly: even the strongest model successfully completes fewer than 4% of tasks end-to-end. This low success rate highlights concrete weaknesses, particularly in long-horizon planning, state tracking over dynamic interfaces, maintaining context across multiple SaaS applications, and recovering from errors during execution.
AI.conclusion: SaaS-Bench exposes a large gap between current capabilities of LLM-based computer-using agents and the demands of realistic SaaS-based professional workflows. The benchmark provides a more challenging and representative testbed than prior simplified web/GUI benchmarks, and the authors argue it can guide future research on planning, memory, robustness, and cross-application reasoning in agent systems.

title: Few-Shot Large Language Models for Actionable Triage Categorization of Online Patient Inquiries
comment: 4 figures, 19 tables, 23 pages (including appendix and reference)
summary: Online patient inquiries are often informal, incomplete, and written before professional assessment, yet they must still be routed to an appropriate level of clinical follow-up. We study this as a four-class actionable triage task -- self-care, schedule-visit, urgent-clinician-review, or emergency-referral, and ask whether prompted large language models (LLMs) can support such routing under low-resource labeling conditions. Using the public HealthCareMagic-100K corpus, we construct a 300-example human calibrated gold evaluation set, a 700-example auto-labeled silver training set, and a 40-example few-shot pool. We compare Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency (TF-IDF) and Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers for Biomedical Text Mining (BioBERT) baselines train on silver labels against six prompted LLMs under 0-shot, 4-shot, and 12-shot conditions respectively. Accordingly, we evaluate with macro-$F_1$ alongside safety-aware metrics, including emergency-recall, under-triage rate, and severe under-triage rate. The strongest LLM (Claude Haiku 4.5, 12-shot) reaches macro-$F_1$ 0.475, exceeding the best supervised baseline (BioBERT, 0.378) on point estimate, with overlapping confidence intervals. Few-shot prompting and two-model agreement help in label-dependent ways: self-care agreement is reliable, urgent-clinician-review is not. We conclude that LLMs can support triage prioritization and selective human review, but not autonomous deployment.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates whether prompted large language models (LLMs) can triage informal online patient questions into four urgency levels using very limited labeled data, comparing them to traditional supervised text classifiers.
AI.motivation: Online patient questions are messy, incomplete, and written before any professional assessment, but they still need to be routed correctly to avoid missed emergencies and unnecessary clinician workload. Creating large, high-quality labeled datasets for medical triage is expensive and difficult. The authors want to know if modern LLMs, prompted with only a few examples, can safely and effectively support triage decisions under low-resource labeling conditions.
AI.method: The authors frame triage as a four-class classification task: self-care, schedule-visit, urgent-clinician-review, and emergency-referral. Using the HealthCareMagic-100K dataset, they create: (1) a 300-example human-validated gold evaluation set, (2) a 700-example automatically labeled silver training set, and (3) a 40-example pool for few-shot prompts. They train TF-IDF and BioBERT models on the silver labels as supervised baselines. They then test six different LLMs using prompt-based 0-shot, 4-shot, and 12-shot settings, including experiments with two-model agreement. Performance is assessed with macro-F1 and safety-focused metrics such as recall for emergency cases, overall under-triage, and severe under-triage.
AI.result: The best LLM configuration (Claude Haiku 4.5 with 12-shot prompting) achieves a macro-F1 score of 0.475, which is higher in point estimate than the best supervised baseline (BioBERT at 0.378), though confidence intervals overlap, so the improvement is not definitively conclusive. Analyses show that few-shot prompting improves performance and that using agreement between two models can help for some labels (e.g., self-care) but is unreliable for others (e.g., urgent-clinician-review). Safety metrics reveal that LLMs still make under-triage errors, especially on more urgent classes.
AI.conclusion: Prompted LLMs, even with limited labeled data, can outperform traditional supervised models on macro-F1 and can be useful for prioritizing which cases need quicker attention and where humans should focus their review. However, due to remaining safety issues and unreliable performance on higher-urgency classes, the models are not yet suitable for fully autonomous triage and should instead be used as decision-support tools with human oversight.

title: Agentic Discovery of Neural Architectures: AIRA-Compose and AIRA-Design
summary: Toward recursive self-improvement, we investigate LLM agents autonomously designing foundation models beyond standard Transformers. We introduce a dual-framework approach: AIRA-Compose for high-level architecture search, and AIRA-Design for low-level mechanistic implementation. AIRA-Compose uses 11 agents to explore fundamental computational primitives under a 24-hour budget. Agents evaluate million-parameter candidates, extrapolating top designs to 350M, 1B, and 3B scales. This yields 14 architectures across two families: AIRAformers (Transformer-based) and AIRAhybrids (Transformer-Mamba). Pre-trained at 1B scale, these consistently outperform Llama 3.2 and Composer-found baselines. On downstream tasks, AIRAformer-D and AIRAhybrid-D improve accuracy by 2.4% and 3.8% over Llama 3.2. Furthermore, AIRA-Compose finds models with highly efficient scaling frontiers: AIRAformer-C scales 54% and 71% faster than Llama 3.2 and Composer's best Transformer, while AIRAhybrid-C outscales Nemotron-2 by 23% and Composer's best hybrid by 37%. AIRA-Design tasks 20 agents with writing novel attention mechanisms for long-range dependencies and high-performing training scripts. On the Long Range Arena benchmark, agent-designed architectures reach within 2.3% and 2.6% of human state-of-the-art on document matching and text classification. On the Autoresearch benchmark, Greedy Opus 4.5 achieves 0.968 validation bits-per-byte under a fixed time budget, surpassing the published minimum. Together, these frameworks show AI agents can autonomously discover architectures and algorithmic optimizations matching or surpassing hand-designed baselines. This establishes a powerful paradigm for discovering next-generation foundation models, marking a clear step toward recursive self-improvement.

AI.tldr: The paper presents AI-driven frameworks (AIRA-Compose and AIRA-Design) that allow LLM agents to autonomously design and implement new foundation model architectures that rival or surpass human-designed Transformer-based models, advancing recursive self-improvement.
AI.motivation: To move toward recursive self-improvement where AI systems can autonomously improve their own architectures, and to go beyond standard Transformer designs by using LLM agents as automated neural architecture and algorithm designers that can discover more efficient and higher-performing foundation models.
AI.method: They propose two complementary agent frameworks. AIRA-Compose performs high-level architecture search with 11 agents that explore computational primitives under a 24-hour budget, evaluating million-parameter candidates and extrapolating the best to larger 350M–3B scales, yielding two architecture families (AIRAformers and AIRAhybrids). AIRA-Design uses 20 agents focused on low-level mechanistic design, including novel attention mechanisms for long-range dependencies and optimized training scripts. The discovered architectures are pre-trained at 1B scale and evaluated against strong baselines on multiple benchmarks and scaling properties.
AI.result: AIRA-Compose discovers 14 architectures (AIRAformers and AIRAhybrids) whose 1B-scale models consistently outperform Llama 3.2 and Composer-found baselines, with AIRAformer-D and AIRAhybrid-D improving downstream accuracy by 2.4% and 3.8% over Llama 3.2. Architectures like AIRAformer-C and AIRAhybrid-C exhibit significantly better scaling efficiency: 54–71% faster than Llama 3.2 and Composer’s best Transformer, and 23–37% better than Nemotron-2 and Composer’s best hybrid. AIRA-Design’s agent-generated mechanisms achieve within 2.3–2.6% of human SOTA on Long Range Arena tasks and beat the published minimum bits-per-byte on Autoresearch (0.968 bpb).
AI.conclusion: Autonomous LLM agents can effectively discover both high-level architectures and low-level algorithmic optimizations that match or surpass human-designed foundation models in accuracy and scaling efficiency. This dual-framework paradigm demonstrates a concrete step toward recursive self-improvement and offers a promising route to next-generation foundation models beyond conventional Transformer designs.

title: ASRU: Activation Steering Meets Reinforcement Unlearning for Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) may memorize sensitive cross-modal information during pretraining, making machine unlearning (MU) crucial. Existing methods typically evaluate unlearning effectiveness based on output deviations, while overlooking the generation quality after unlearning. This can easily lead to hallucinated or rigid responses, thereby affecting the usability and safety of the unlearned model. To address this issue, we propose ASRU, a controllable multimodal unlearning framework that incorporates generation quality as a core evaluation objective. ASRU first induces initial refusal behavior through activation redirection, and then optimizes fine-grained refusal boundaries using a customized reward function, thereby achieving a better trade-off between target knowledge unlearning and model utility. Experiments on Qwen3-VL show that ASRU significantly improves unlearning effectiveness (+24.6%) on average and generation quality (5.8x) on average while effectively preserving model utility, using only a small amount of retained supervision data.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ASRU, a controllable multimodal unlearning framework for MLLMs that jointly optimizes forgetting of sensitive knowledge and preservation of generation quality and utility.
AI.motivation: Multimodal large language models can memorize sensitive cross-modal information during pretraining. Existing machine unlearning methods mostly judge success by how much outputs change on the target data, but they neglect whether the post-unlearning model still generates coherent, non-hallucinatory, and useful responses. This gap can lead to unlearned models that are formally different from the original but practically unusable or unsafe. The paper is motivated by the need for unlearning approaches that explicitly factor in generation quality and utility for MLLMs.
AI.method: The authors propose ASRU, a two-stage controllable unlearning framework for multimodal LLMs. First, ASRU induces refusal behavior for sensitive targets via activation redirection, steering internal representations so that the model learns to decline answering on the unlearning targets. Second, it refines this behavior with a customized reward-based optimization that shapes fine-grained refusal boundaries. The reward is designed to explicitly balance three aspects: effective forgetting of target knowledge, high-quality generation on non-target inputs, and preservation of overall model utility. The approach uses only a relatively small supervised dataset of retained (non-forgotten) examples to guide this optimization.
AI.result: On the Qwen3-VL multimodal model, ASRU yields large gains in both unlearning and quality metrics. It improves unlearning effectiveness by an average of +24.6% compared to baselines, and boosts generation quality by a factor of 5.8x on average, while maintaining general utility close to the original model. These results indicate that the framework more cleanly removes sensitive knowledge without degrading, and even improving, the overall response quality.
AI.conclusion: ASRU demonstrates that machine unlearning in multimodal LLMs can be made both more effective and more practical by treating generation quality as a primary objective, not a side effect. By combining activation redirection with reward-based fine-tuning of refusal boundaries, the framework achieves a better trade-off between forgetting targeted information and preserving (or enhancing) model usability. The work suggests a path toward safer deployment of MLLMs in settings where certain cross-modal information must be reliably removed while keeping the model broadly functional.

title: Imperfect World Models are Exploitable
summary: We propose a novel definition of model exploitation in reinforcement learning. Informally, a world model is exploitable if it implies that one policy should be strictly preferred over another while the environment's true transition model implies the reverse. We analogize our definition with a prior characterization of reward hacking but show that the associated proof of inevitability does not transfer to exploitation. To overcome this obstruction, we develop a general theory of reward hacking and model exploitation that proves that exploitation is essentially unavoidable on large policy sets and yields the corresponding claim for hacking as a special case. Unfortunately, we also find that the conditions that guarantee unhackability in finite policy sets have no counterpart that precludes exploitation. Consequently, we introduce a relaxed notion of exploitation and derive a safe horizon within which it can be avoided. Taken together, our results establish a formal bridge between reward hacking and model exploitation and elucidate the limits of safe planning in world models.

AI.tldr: The paper formalizes when a learned world model in reinforcement learning can be “exploited” (i.e., recommends the wrong policy ordering), shows that such failures are essentially unavoidable for large policy classes, relates this to reward hacking, and provides conditions and a finite planning horizon under which a relaxed form of exploitation can be avoided.
AI.motivation: Existing work has characterized and proved the inevitability of reward hacking in reinforcement learning, but analogous formal understanding for failures that arise from inaccuracies in the learned transition model (“model exploitation”) is lacking. The authors want a rigorous definition of model exploitation, a theoretical relationship between exploitation and reward hacking, and conditions under which safe planning with world models is or isn’t possible.
AI.method: They first propose a formal definition of model exploitation, based on mismatches between policy preferences induced by the learned world model and the true environment dynamics. They compare this to an existing formalization of reward hacking and analyze why earlier inevitability proofs for hacking do not directly apply to model exploitation. They then develop a unified, general theory covering both reward hacking and model exploitation, derive conditions under which these phenomena are essentially unavoidable on large policy sets, and study the special case that recovers the earlier hacking result. For finite policy sets, they analyze conditions that avoid reward hacking and show why analogous conditions cannot prevent exploitation. Finally, they introduce a relaxed (weaker) notion of exploitation and mathematically derive a finite “safe horizon” within which such relaxed exploitation can be ruled out.
AI.result: 1) A precise definition of model exploitation in RL that captures when a learned world model reverses the true preference ordering between two policies. 2) A general theoretical framework that proves exploitation is effectively inevitable when optimizing over large policy sets, and that includes reward hacking inevitability as a corollary. 3) A negative result: structural conditions that ensure no reward hacking on finite policy sets do not extend to rule out model exploitation. 4) A relaxed definition of exploitation and a provable safe planning horizon during which this weaker form of exploitation is guaranteed not to occur.
AI.conclusion: Model exploitation—wrong policy recommendations induced by an imperfect world model—is formally distinct from, but closely related to, reward hacking. For sufficiently rich policy classes, both phenomena are essentially unavoidable, limiting the guarantees we can provide for safe planning with learned models. While reward hacking can sometimes be prevented on finite policy sets, analogous guarantees are not available for strict model exploitation; at best, one can adopt a relaxed notion of exploitation and restrict planning to a provably safe time horizon during which even this relaxed exploitation will not arise.

title: H-Mem: A Novel Memory Mechanism for Evolving and Retrieving Agent Memory via a Hybrid Structure
summary: Memory data are ubiquitous in Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents (e.g., OpenClaw and Manus). A few recent works have attempted to exploit agents'memory for improving their performance on the question-answering (QA) task, but they lack a principled mechanism for effectively modeling how memory data evolves over time and retrieving memory data effectively, leading to poor performance in memory utilization. To fill this gap, we present H-Mem, a novel memory mechanism via a hybrid structure that can not only effectively model the evolution of agent memory over a long period of time, but also provide an efficient memory retrieval approach. Particularly, H-Mem builds a temporal and semantic tree structure that allows the short-term memory data to evolve progressively into long-term memory data, where the latter provides summarized information about the former, while simultaneously constructing a knowledge graph to capture the relationships between entities in memory. Moreover, it offers an effective memory retrieval approach by exploiting the hybrid structure of the tree and graph structures. Extensive experiments on three agent memory benchmarks show that H-Mem achieves state-of-the-art performance on the QA task.

AI.tldr: Introduces H-Mem, a hybrid temporal-semantic tree plus knowledge-graph memory mechanism for LLM agents that improves QA performance.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based agents store and use memory but lack a principled way to model how memory evolves over time and to retrieve it effectively. Current methods underutilize memory, leading to suboptimal QA results on tasks requiring long-term, structured recall and reasoning.
AI.method: Proposes H-Mem, which constructs (1) a temporal and semantic tree where short-term memories are progressively abstracted into long-term summarized nodes, and (2) a knowledge graph capturing entity relationships across memories. The system defines algorithms for updating these structures as new memories arrive and for retrieving relevant memories by jointly exploiting both the hierarchical tree and the relational graph during QA.
AI.result: On three agent-memory QA benchmarks, H-Mem outperforms previous approaches, achieving state-of-the-art results, indicating more effective memory modeling and retrieval in LLM-based agents.
AI.conclusion: A hybrid temporal-semantic tree plus knowledge-graph memory architecture enables more effective long-term memory evolution and retrieval for LLM agents, yielding improved QA performance and suggesting a promising direction for structured memory mechanisms in agent systems.

title: PAGER: Bridging the Semantic-Execution Gap in Point-Precise Geometric GUI Control
summary: Large vision-language models have significantly advanced GUI agents, enabling executable interaction across web, mobile, and desktop interfaces. Yet these gains largely rely on a forgiving region-tolerant paradigm, where many nearby pixels inside the same component remain valid. Precise geometric construction breaks this assumption: actions must land on points in continuous canvas space rather than tolerant regions. Because geometric primitives carry ontological dependencies, a local coordinate error can induce cascading topological failures that distort downstream objects and invalidate the final construction. We identify this regime as precision-sensitive GUI tasks, requiring point-level accuracy, geometry-aware verification, and robustness to dependency-driven error propagation. To benchmark it, we introduce PAGE Bench, with 4,906 problems and over 224K process-supervised, pixel-level GUI actions. We further propose PAGER, a topology-aware agent that decomposes construction into dependency-structured planning and pixel-level execution. Pixel-grounded supervised tuning establishes executable action grammar, while precision-aligned reinforcement learning mitigates rollout-induced exposure bias through state-conditioned geometric feedback. Experiments reveal a pronounced Semantic-Execution Gap: general multimodal models can exceed 88% action type accuracy yet remain below 6% task success. PAGER closes this gap, delivering 4.1x higher task success than the strongest evaluated general baseline and raising step success rate from below 9% for GUI-specialized agents to over 62%, establishing a new state of the art for point-precise GUI control.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces PAGE Bench, a benchmark for precision-sensitive GUI tasks requiring point-level accuracy, and PAGER, a topology-aware agent that significantly improves success in point-precise GUI control over existing multimodal and GUI-specialized models.
AI.motivation: Existing GUI agents powered by large vision-language models work well in region-tolerant settings, where many nearby pixels count as a valid click. However, they fail when strict point-level geometric precision is needed, such as drawing in a continuous canvas, where small coordinate errors can propagate and break the entire construction. There was no dedicated benchmark or method focusing on this precision-sensitive GUI regime.
AI.method: The authors formulate precision-sensitive GUI tasks characterized by point-level accuracy needs, geometry-aware verification, and robustness to dependency-driven error propagation. They build PAGE Bench with 4,906 problems and over 224K pixel-level, process-supervised actions. They then propose PAGER, a topology-aware agent that decomposes construction into dependency-structured planning and pixel-level execution. PAGER is trained via pixel-grounded supervised tuning to learn an executable action grammar, and then refined with precision-aligned reinforcement learning that uses state-conditioned geometric feedback to reduce exposure bias during rollouts.
AI.result: Experiments show a large Semantic-Execution Gap: existing multimodal models achieve high action-type prediction accuracy (over 88%) but very low end-to-end task success (below 6%) on precision-sensitive GUI tasks. PAGER substantially narrows this gap, achieving 4.1x higher task success than the strongest general baseline and boosting step success rate from below 9% for prior GUI-specialized agents to over 62%, setting a new state of the art in point-precise GUI control.
AI.conclusion: Precision-sensitive GUI tasks expose limitations of current vision-language GUI agents that are hidden in region-tolerant settings, creating a gap between understanding what to do and executing it precisely. PAGE Bench provides a standardized benchmark for this regime, and PAGER demonstrates that topology-aware planning plus pixel-level execution, trained with both supervised learning and precision-aligned RL, can dramatically improve performance, establishing a strong new baseline for point-precise GUI control.

title: Deterministic Event-Graph Substrates as World Models for Counterfactual Reasoning
summary: We study event-graph substrates: a class of world models that represent agent state as an append-only log of typed RDF triples and answer counterfactual queries by forking the log under a structured intervention vocabulary. Substrates are inspectable at the triple level, support exact counterfactuals, and transfer across domains without learned components. We formalize the class, prove a duality between explanatory and counterfactual queries that reduces both to the same causal-ancestor traversal, and evaluate a 1,400-line CLEVRER-DSL interpreter atop a domain-agnostic substrate runtime at full CLEVRER validation scale (n=75,618). The substrate exceeds the NS-DR symbolic oracle on all four per-question categories (by 9.89, 20.26, 17.65, and 0.80 percentage points), and exceeds the parametric ALOE baseline on descriptive and explanatory while lagging on predictive and counterfactual. We also introduce twin-EventLog, a 500-specification Park-canonical Smallville counterfactual benchmark on which the substrate exceeds Llama-3.1-8B with full context by 18.80 points joint accuracy.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces event-graph substrates: inspectable, log-based world models for exact counterfactual reasoning, formalizes their properties, proves a duality between explanation and counterfactual queries, and shows strong performance on CLEVRER and a new Smallville-style benchmark.
AI.motivation: Current causal and counterfactual reasoning systems often rely on opaque, learned components, struggle with exact counterfactuals, and are hard to transfer across domains. The authors want a transparent, symbolic world model that can support precise counterfactual reasoning, share a unified mechanism for explanation and counterfactuals, and generalize across tasks and environments without retraining.
AI.method: They define event-graph substrates as append-only logs of typed RDF triples with a structured intervention vocabulary that enables forking of the event log to answer counterfactuals. They formalize the substrate class, prove that both explanatory and counterfactual queries reduce to the same causal-ancestor traversal on the event graph, and implement a ~1,400-line interpreter for CLEVRER-DSL running on a domain-agnostic substrate runtime. They then evaluate on CLEVRER at full validation scale and introduce a new benchmark, twin-EventLog, based on Park-canonical Smallville-style counterfactual specifications.
AI.result: On CLEVRER, their substrate-based system outperforms the NS-DR symbolic oracle across all four question types by 9.89, 20.26, 17.65, and 0.80 percentage points, respectively, and beats the parametric ALOE baseline on descriptive and explanatory questions while trailing it on predictive and counterfactual types. On the new twin-EventLog benchmark of 500 counterfactual specifications, the substrate surpasses Llama-3.1-8B with full context by 18.80 points in joint accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Event-graph substrates provide a transparent, domain-agnostic symbolic world model that supports exact counterfactual reasoning through log forking and causal-ancestor traversal. The formal duality between explanations and counterfactuals simplifies query handling, and empirical results on CLEVRER and twin-EventLog show that such non-parametric substrates can match or exceed strong symbolic and neural baselines, especially on descriptive and explanatory tasks, while remaining competitive on predictive and counterfactual queries.

title: Contexting as Recommendation: Evolutionary Collaborative Filtering for Context Engineering
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are highly sensitive to their input contexts, motivating the development of automated context engineering. However, existing methods predominantly treat this as a global search problem, seeking a single context strategy that maximizes average performance across a dataset. This restrictive assumption overlooks the fact that different inputs often require distinct guidance, leaving substantial instance-level performance gains untapped. In this paper, we propose a paradigm shift by formulating context engineering as a recommendation problem. We introduce \textbf{Neural Collaborative Context Engineering (NCCE)}, a framework that transitions optimization from a static global search to dynamic, instance-wise routing. NCCE first bootstraps a diverse catalog of anchor contexts and then employs a novel \textbf{Context-CF Co-Evolution} mechanism. This stage establishes a synergistic feedback loop: a lightweight Neural Collaborative Filtering (NCF) model learns instance-context preferences to guide the generation of specialized context variants, while the newly evaluated contexts continuously refine the NCF model's understanding of latent preferences. At inference time, the trained NCF model acts as a context router, dynamically assigning the most suitable context strategy to each unseen instance. Theoretical Proofs and comprehensive experiments demonstrate that by matching individual inputs with their optimal contexts, NCCE significantly improves task accuracy, highlighting the critical importance of personalization in LLM context engineering.

AI.tldr: The paper reframes LLM prompt/context design as a per-instance recommendation problem instead of a single best prompt search, using a neural collaborative filtering framework to route each input to its most suitable context and showing notable accuracy gains.
AI.motivation: LLMs are very sensitive to prompts/contexts, and automated methods (prompt search, instruction optimization, etc.) usually look for one global best context for an entire dataset. This ignores that different inputs may need different guidance or prompt styles, leaving potential performance improvements unrealized. The authors want a method that can personalize context selection to each input in a scalable, principled way.
AI.method: They propose Neural Collaborative Context Engineering (NCCE). First, they build a diverse library of anchor contexts. They then introduce Context-CF Co-Evolution: a lightweight neural collaborative filtering (NCF) model learns a latent preference structure between instances and contexts based on observed performance, and this model is used to guide the generation of specialized context variants. New variants are evaluated with the LLM; their performance is fed back to further train/refine the NCF model. After training, the NCF model serves as a context router at inference, assigning the most suitable context from the catalog to each new input. They also provide theoretical analysis of the framework.
AI.result: The method improves task accuracy across benchmarks compared to global, single-context baselines and other context-optimization strategies. The experiments, along with theoretical proofs, support that instance-wise context routing can better exploit LLM capabilities than using a one-size-fits-all context.
AI.conclusion: Personalized, instance-level context selection is crucial for maximizing LLM performance. By modeling context engineering as a recommendation problem and using an NCF-based router that co-evolves with a catalog of prompts, NCCE can dynamically match each input to a tailored context, delivering significant accuracy gains over static global prompt search methods.

title: Learning Bilevel Policies over Symbolic World Models for Long-Horizon Planning
summary: We tackle the challenge of building embodied AI agents that can reliably solve long-horizon planning problems. Imitation learning from demonstrations has shown itself to be effective in training robots to solve a diversity of complex tasks requiring fine motor control and manipulation over low-level (LL), continuous environments. Yet, it remains a difficult endeavour to generate long-horizon plans from imitation learning alone. In contrast, high-level (HL), symbolic abstractions facilitate efficient and interpretable long-horizon planning. We propose to combine the strengths of LL imitation learning for manipulation and control, and HL symbolic abstractions for long-horizon planning. We realise this idea via \emph{bilevel policies} of the form $(π^{\mathrm{hl}}, π^{\mathrm{ll}})$, consisting of a neural policy $π^{\mathrm{ll}}$ learned from LL demonstrations, and an HL symbolic policy $π^{\mathrm{hl}}$ that is constructed from symbolic abstractions of the LL demonstrations combined with inductive generalisation. We implement these ideas in the BISON system. Experiments on extended MetaWorld benchmarks demonstrate that BISON generalises to long horizons and problems with greater numbers of objects than those solved by VLA and end-to-end methods, and is more time and memory efficient in training and inference. Notably, when ignoring LL execution, BISON's HL policies can solve HL problems with 10,000 relevant objects in under a minute. Project page: https://dillonzchen.github.io/bison

AI.tldr: Paper introduces BISON, a bilevel policy framework that combines high-level symbolic planning with low-level imitation learning to solve long-horizon, complex embodied AI tasks more efficiently and at larger scales than prior methods.
AI.motivation: Imitation learning excels at low-level, continuous control for complex manipulation, but struggles with long-horizon planning. Conversely, high-level symbolic planning handles long horizons and many objects but lacks fine-grained control. There is a need for an approach that unifies these strengths to enable embodied agents to solve long, complex tasks efficiently and with good generalisation.
AI.method: The paper proposes bilevel policies (π^hl, π^ll). The low-level policy π^ll is a neural network trained via imitation learning from continuous demonstrations to handle manipulation and control. The high-level policy π^hl is a symbolic planner constructed from abstractions derived from those same low-level demonstrations, combined with inductive generalisation rules. This architecture is instantiated in the BISON system and evaluated on extended MetaWorld benchmarks, comparing against vision-language-action (VLA) and end-to-end methods in terms of generalisation, runtime, and memory efficiency.
AI.result: On extended MetaWorld tasks, BISON generalises better to longer horizons and to environments with more objects than those seen during training, outperforming VLA and end-to-end baselines. It is also more time- and memory-efficient in both training and inference. The high-level policy alone can plan for problems involving up to 10,000 relevant objects in under a minute when low-level execution is omitted.
AI.conclusion: Combining symbolic high-level planning with low-level imitation-learned control via bilevel policies yields embodied agents that can efficiently solve long-horizon tasks and scale to large numbers of objects, outperforming existing VLA and end-to-end methods. This demonstrates the value of explicit symbolic abstractions built from demonstrations for generalisation and scalability in robotic planning and control.

title: DimMem: Dimensional Structuring for Efficient Long-Term Agent Memory
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents require long-term memory to leverage information from past interactions. However, existing memory systems often face a fidelity--efficiency trade-off: raw dialogue histories are expensive, while flat facts or summaries may discard the structure needed for precise recall. We propose \textbf{DimMem}, a lightweight dimensional memory framework that represents each memory as an atomic, typed, and self-contained unit with explicit fields such as time, location, reason, purpose, and keywords. This representation exposes the structure needed for dimension-aware retrieval, memory update, and selective assistant-context recall without storing full histories in the model context. Across LoCoMo-10 and LongMemEval-S, DimMem achieves \textbf{81.43\%} and \textbf{78.20\%} overall accuracy, respectively, outperforming existing lightweight memory systems while reducing LoCoMo per-query token cost by \textbf{24\%}. We further show that dimensional memory extraction is learnable by compact models: after fine-tuning on the DimMem schema, a Qwen3-4B extractor surpasses LightMem with GPT-4.1-mini on both benchmarks and reaches performance comparable to, or better than, much larger extractors in key settings. These results suggest that explicit dimensional structuring is an effective and efficient foundation for long-term memory in LLM agents. Code is available at https://github.com/ChowRunFa/DimMem.

AI.tldr: DimMem is a structured, lightweight long-term memory framework for LLM agents that improves retrieval accuracy and efficiency by representing memories as typed, dimensional units instead of raw histories or flat summaries.
AI.motivation: LLM agents need long-term memory to use information from past interactions, but storing full dialogue histories is expensive and simple fact lists or summaries lose important structure needed for precise recall. Existing lightweight memory systems thus face a fidelity–efficiency trade-off. The paper aims to design a memory representation that is both compact and structurally rich enough to support accurate retrieval, updating, and context construction.
AI.method: The authors introduce DimMem, which encodes each memory as an atomic, typed, self-contained unit with explicit fields (dimensions) like time, location, reason, purpose, and keywords. This structured schema supports dimension-aware retrieval, memory updating, and selective recall without keeping full histories in the LLM context. They build extraction models that convert dialogues into DimMem entries and evaluate retrieval and agent performance on long-term memory benchmarks (LoCoMo-10 and LongMemEval-S). They also fine-tune a compact Qwen3-4B model on the DimMem schema for automatic extraction and compare it against larger extractors and existing lightweight memory systems such as LightMem with GPT-4.1-mini.
AI.result: On LoCoMo-10 and LongMemEval-S, DimMem attains 81.43% and 78.20% overall accuracy, respectively, outperforming prior lightweight memory systems, while cutting per-query token cost on LoCoMo by 24%. The fine-tuned Qwen3-4B DimMem extractor surpasses LightMem with GPT-4.1-mini on both benchmarks and reaches performance comparable to or better than much larger extraction models in several evaluation settings.
AI.conclusion: Explicit dimensional structuring of memories—encoding them as typed units with fields like time, location, and purpose—provides an effective and efficient basis for long-term memory in LLM agents. DimMem improves accuracy and reduces token costs compared to existing lightweight memory approaches, and its schema can be learned by relatively small models, suggesting it is practical for scalable agent deployments.

title: Petri Net Induced Heuristic Search for Resource Constrained Scheduling
comment: Accepted at the International Symposium on Combinatorial Search (SoCS 2026)
summary: We formulate the Resource-Constrained Project Scheduling Problem (RCPSP) as optimal search over the reachability graph of a Timed Transition Petri Net with Resources, using relative-delay tokens so that scheduling decisions correspond to transition firings in the induced state space. We solve the resulting problem with $A^*$ guided by a heuristic that combines Critical Path and resource-based lower bounds, and prove that it is consistent under our token-based time semantics. Experiments on the PSPLIB benchmarks show that the approach outperforms strong exact Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MIP) baselines (SCIP, CBC) in both success rate and solve time. Per-instance analysis shows that heuristic search and MIP degrade along independent axes, resource tightness for $A^*$ and formulation size for MIP, with resource strength mediating which solver benefits from scale.

AI.tldr: They solve the Resource-Constrained Project Scheduling Problem (RCPSP) by modeling it as optimal search over a Timed Transition Petri Net with resources, and use an A* algorithm with a new consistent heuristic that outperforms strong MIP solvers on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Classical exact approaches to the RCPSP, particularly MIP formulations, can struggle with scalability and performance depending on instance characteristics like resource tightness and formulation size. The authors are motivated to provide an alternative exact method with different performance trade-offs, potentially outperforming MIP on many instances, and to better understand how instance structure affects solver behavior.
AI.method: They model RCPSP as a Timed Transition Petri Net with resources, where scheduling decisions map to transition firings in a reachability graph constructed using relative-delay tokens to represent time. They then apply A* search on this state space, guided by a heuristic that merges Critical Path (precedence-based) and resource-based lower bounds. They formally prove that this heuristic is consistent under their time semantics. They then run experiments on standard PSPLIB benchmark instances and compare against strong exact MIP solvers (SCIP, CBC).
AI.result: Empirically, their A*-based approach achieves higher success rates and faster solve times than strong MIP baselines on PSPLIB benchmarks. The per-instance analysis reveals that A* and MIP struggle for largely different reasons: A* performance deteriorates mainly with increasing resource tightness, whereas MIP performance deteriorates with increasing formulation size. Resource strength is identified as a key factor that determines which solver scales better on a given instance.
AI.conclusion: Modeling RCPSP as optimal search on a timed Petri net and solving it with A* plus a carefully designed, consistent heuristic is a competitive exact alternative to MIP. The approach not only outperforms state-of-the-art MIP solvers on many benchmarks, but also offers complementary performance characteristics, suggesting that choice of solver should depend on resource tightness and formulation size, with resource strength influencing which approach is preferable at scale.

title: CompactQE: Interpretable Translation Quality Estimation via Small Open-Weight LLMs
summary: Current state-of-the-art Quality Estimation (QE) in machine translation relies on massive, proprietary LLMs, raising data privacy concerns. We demonstrate that smaller, open-source LLMs (<30B parameters) are a viable, cost-effective and privacy-preserving alternative. Using a single-pass prompting strategy, our models simultaneously generate quality scores, MQM error annotations, suggested error corrections, and full post-editions. Our analysis shows these models achieve highly competitive system-level correlations with human judgments that outperform traditional neural metrics, fine-tuned models, and human inter-annotator agreement, effectively approximating the capabilities of much larger proprietary LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that small, open-source language models can do high-quality machine translation quality estimation, rivaling large proprietary models, while being cheaper and better for privacy.
AI.motivation: State-of-the-art MT quality estimation depends on large, closed, proprietary LLMs, which are expensive and pose data privacy risks when user data must be sent to external providers. There is a need for cost-effective, privacy-preserving alternatives that remain competitive in quality estimation performance.
AI.method: The authors use smaller (<30B parameter) open-source LLMs and design a single-pass prompting strategy that asks the model to output several pieces of information at once: overall quality scores, MQM-style error annotations, suggested corrections for each error, and complete post-edited translations. They then compare the system-level correlations of these models’ scores with human judgments against traditional neural metrics, fine-tuned models, and human inter-annotator agreement.
