AI.result: The small open-source LLMs achieve strong system-level correlation with human judgments, outperforming traditional neural QE metrics, specialized fine-tuned QE models, and even human inter-annotator agreement. Their multi-output prompting shows that these models can simultaneously handle scoring, error labeling, and post-editing effectively.
AI.conclusion: Smaller, open-source LLMs are a viable and attractive alternative to large proprietary LLMs for machine translation quality estimation. With a simple single-pass prompting design, they can approximate or match the capabilities of much larger systems while being cheaper to run and more compatible with data privacy requirements.

title: ScreenSearch: Uncertainty-Aware OS Exploration
summary: Desktop GUI agents operate under partial observability: visually similar screens can correspond to different underlying workflow states, so locally plausible actions can lead to sharply different outcomes. We frame this as a problem of computer/OS state exploration, where effective behavior requires both expanding the reachable frontier and reducing ambiguity before committing. We present ScreenSearch, a system that combines structural screen retrieval and deduplication with an ambiguity-aware PUCT graph-bandit for large-scale desktop exploration. The retrieval layer converts UIA trees into location-aware structural features, indexes related screens through sparse token search and metadata filters, and maintains a shared deduplicated state graph across VM workers. On top of this graph, we define a scalable ambiguity signal based on matched-action outcome dispersion. If similar screens produce different next states under the same action signature, the state should be probed further rather than treated as resolved. We use this signal together with frontier rewards to drive large-scale exploration and replay-start policy evaluation over the shared graph. Across 11 desktop applications, ScreenSearch collects over 1M screenshots and over 30K deduplicated states, yielding large exploration corpora with substantial cross-application and within-application diversity. On a fixed replay-start slice, we observe a clear novelty--ambiguity trade-off: some policies reduce ambiguity quickly while discovering little frontier. Ambiguity reduction alone is therefore not a sufficient exploration objective. Appendix ablations show that stronger proposal priors can materially improve unique-state discovery during corpus building. These results suggest that state identity, proposal quality, and ambiguity-aware search all matter when deciding when to probe and when to commit.

AI.tldr: ScreenSearch is a system for large-scale exploration of desktop GUI state spaces under partial observability, using structural screen retrieval and an ambiguity-aware search policy to decide when to further probe a screen versus move on.
AI.motivation: Desktop GUI agents see only the screen, but visually similar interfaces can hide different underlying states. Naively choosing locally sensible actions can therefore cause divergent and unpredictable outcomes. Existing exploration approaches often ignore this ambiguity or treat each screenshot independently, limiting their ability to build reliable state representations or to evaluate policies. The paper is motivated by the need for scalable, ambiguity-aware exploration that builds a shared, deduplicated state graph across applications and virtual machines, enabling better training and evaluation of GUI agents.
AI.method: The authors propose ScreenSearch, which treats GUI interaction as exploration of an underlying OS/state graph. First, a retrieval and deduplication layer transforms UI Automation (UIA) trees into location-aware structural features, indexes them using sparse token search plus metadata filters, and merges similar screens into a shared deduplicated state graph across multiple VM workers. On top of this graph, they define an ambiguity signal based on outcome dispersion: for a given action signature on structurally similar screens, they measure how often it leads to different next states. They then use an ambiguity-aware PUCT-style graph bandit algorithm that trades off between (1) expanding the frontier of unexplored states and (2) revisiting ambiguous states whose outcomes are inconsistent, driving large-scale exploration and replay-start policy evaluation.
AI.result: Applying ScreenSearch to 11 desktop applications, the system collects over 1 million screenshots and condenses them into more than 30,000 deduplicated states, demonstrating scalable corpus building. The resulting graph exhibits substantial diversity both across different applications and within individual applications. When evaluating policies from replay-start states, they find a strong novelty–ambiguity trade-off: policies that quickly reduce ambiguity tend to discover fewer new frontier states. Ablation studies in the appendix show that using stronger proposal priors—i.e., better heuristics for suggesting actions—significantly increases the number of unique states discovered during corpus construction.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that resolving screen ambiguity alone is not an adequate exploration objective, because it can come at the cost of discovering new parts of the state space. Effective GUI exploration must balance novelty seeking with ambiguity reduction. The results highlight three key factors for desktop state exploration: (1) accurate state identity via structural retrieval and deduplication, (2) high-quality proposal priors to suggest promising actions, and (3) ambiguity-aware search that uses outcome dispersion to decide when to probe a state further versus commit and move on. Together, these ingredients enable more reliable large-scale exploration and evaluation for desktop GUI agents under partial observability.

title: ForMaT: Dataset for Visually-Grounded Multilingual PDF Translation
summary: We present ForMaT (Format-Preserving Multilingual Translation), a parallel corpus of 3,956 PDFs across 15 language pairs that preserves original layout metadata proposed for multimodal machine translation. To ensure structural diversity in the dataset, we employ K-Medoids sampling over 45 geometric features, capturing complex elements like nested tables and formulas to focus only on visually diverse PDF documents. Our evaluation reveals that current MT systems struggle with spatial grounding and geometric synchronization, often losing the link between text and its visual context. ForMaT provides a benchmark for developing layout-aware translation models that integrate visual and textual context for high-fidelity document reconstruction.

AI.tldr: Introduces ForMaT, a format-preserving multilingual PDF translation corpus and benchmark focused on layout-aware, multimodal machine translation.
AI.motivation: Standard MT datasets ignore rich document layouts, making it hard to train or evaluate systems that must preserve visual structure (e.g., tables, formulas, complex layouts) during translation. Existing MT systems lose alignment between text and visual context in PDFs, which is critical for accurate document reconstruction. A dedicated, layout-preserving, multilingual benchmark is needed.
AI.method: Construct a parallel corpus of 3,956 PDFs across 15 language pairs where original layout metadata is preserved. Use K-Medoids clustering/sampling over 45 geometric layout features (capturing structures like nested tables, formulas, and complex layouts) to select structurally diverse documents. Evaluate existing MT systems on this corpus, focusing on spatial grounding and geometric synchronization between text and layout.
AI.result: The collected corpus, ForMaT, provides a diverse set of PDFs with complex visual structures and preserved layout metadata. Experiments show that current MT systems perform poorly on maintaining spatial grounding and geometric synchronization, frequently breaking the association between translated text and its original visual context.
AI.conclusion: ForMaT exposes significant weaknesses of current MT models in layout- and vision-aware translation. It offers a benchmark resource to foster research on multimodal, layout-aware translation models that jointly leverage visual and textual signals to achieve high-fidelity, format-preserving document translation and reconstruction.

title: Reasoners or Translators? Contamination-aware Evaluation and Neuro-Symbolic Robustness in Tax Law
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have significantly enhanced automated legal reasoning. Yet, it remains unclear whether their performance reflects genuine legal reasoning ability or artifacts of data contamination. We present a comprehensive empirical study of tax law reasoning approaches and implement a contamination detection protocol to rigorously assess LLM reliability. We show that performance can be inflated by contamination. Building on this analysis, we conduct a systematic evaluation, comparing monolithic LLMs with hybrid systems that translate statutory text into formal representations and delegate inference to symbolic solvers. We build a novel test suite designed to probe generalization to unseen documents via case and rule variations. Our findings indicate that legal reasoning is inherently compositional and that neuro-symbolic frameworks offer a more reliable and robust foundation for legal AI, as well as improved generalization to unobserved situations.

AI.tldr: The paper empirically evaluates whether large language models truly perform legal reasoning in tax law or just benefit from training data contamination, and finds that neuro-symbolic hybrid systems generalize more reliably.
AI.motivation: Automated legal reasoning with LLMs is rapidly improving, but it is unclear if high benchmark scores reflect real reasoning or if models have effectively seen the answers during training (data contamination). In tax law, where rules are complex and compositional, there is a need to rigorously test reliability, robustness, and generalization to new statutes and cases.
AI.method: The authors design a contamination detection protocol to check whether LLM performance is inflated by prior exposure to benchmark data. They then construct a new test suite for tax law that stresses generalization via variations in cases and rules and via unseen documents. They compare two main approaches: (1) monolithic LLMs doing end-to-end legal reasoning directly from statutory text, and (2) hybrid neuro-symbolic systems that first translate statutes into formal logical or symbolic representations and then use dedicated symbolic solvers for inference.
AI.result: The study finds that standard LLM performance on tax law tasks can indeed be inflated due to data contamination. In controlled, contamination-resistant tests, monolithic LLMs struggle more with systematic generalization, especially when presented with variations in cases and rules. Hybrid neuro-symbolic systems, while sometimes less fluent, demonstrate more consistent and robust reasoning, particularly on unseen documents and novel combinations of legal rules.
AI.conclusion: Legal reasoning in tax law is strongly compositional and benefits from representations that make this structure explicit. Neuro-symbolic frameworks, which separate language understanding from formal inference, provide a more reliable and robust basis for legal AI than purely monolithic LLMs, especially when aiming for trustworthy generalization to new legal situations. Benchmarks and evaluations must actively address data contamination to avoid overestimating LLM legal capabilities.

title: Linked Multi-Model Data on Russian Domestic and Foreign Policy Speeches
summary: This paper introduces a dataset of interlinked multimodal political communications from the Russian government, addressing persistent deficiencies in the availability of social text- and image-based data for authoritarian politics contexts. The dataset comprises two large corpora of official speeches delivered by senior actors within the Kremlin and the Russian Ministry of Foreign Affairs over multiple decades. For each speech, we provide Russian- and English-language texts, associated images and captions where available, and harmonized metadata including (e.g.) dates, speakers, (geo)locations, and official government content tags. Unique identifiers link images to speeches and align Russian and English versions of the same communication texts. We further augment these linked datasets with validated topical annotations for both speech texts and speech images, which are generated via transformer-based multimodal topic modeling and refined by a Russian politics expert. The resulting data resources support multimodal, multilingual, temporal, and/or spatial analyses of (authoritarian) political communication and offer a valuable testbed for social science research and large language model (LLM) applications in political domains.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a new multimodal, multilingual dataset of Russian government political communications, linking speeches, images, and rich metadata for research on authoritarian politics and LLM applications.
AI.motivation: There is a lack of accessible, large-scale, text- and image-based datasets suitable for studying political communication—especially in authoritarian regimes such as Russia. Existing resources are often incomplete, unimodal (only text), not interlinked across languages, or lack standardized metadata and expert-validated annotations. This hinders systematic, longitudinal, and multimodal analysis of how authoritarian governments communicate domestically and internationally.
AI.method: The authors compile two large corpora of official speeches from senior figures in the Kremlin and the Russian Ministry of Foreign Affairs over multiple decades. For each speech, they collect Russian and English versions where available, link associated images and captions, and create harmonized metadata (dates, speakers, locations, official tags, etc.). They assign unique identifiers to connect images with speeches and align language versions of the same communication. They then apply transformer-based multimodal topic modeling to both texts and images to generate topical annotations, which are subsequently reviewed and refined by an expert in Russian politics, producing validated topic labels.
AI.result: The outcome is a structured dataset consisting of: (1) parallel Russian- and English-language speech texts, (2) linked images and captions, (3) standardized temporal, spatial, and actor metadata, and (4) expert-validated topical labels for both text and image content obtained via multimodal topic modeling. All items are interlinked via unique IDs, enabling seamless navigation between modalities and languages and supporting complex queries and analyses.
AI.conclusion: The resulting dataset provides a valuable resource for multimodal, multilingual, temporal, and spatial analysis of authoritarian political communication. It facilitates social science research on Russian politics and propaganda, and serves as a useful benchmark and testbed for large language models and other AI systems operating in political communication domains, especially under authoritarian contexts.

title: Sign-Separated Finite-Time Error Analysis of Q-Learning
summary: This paper develops a sign-separated finite-time error analysis for constant step-size Q-learning. Starting from the switching-system representation, the error is decomposed into its componentwise negative and positive parts. The negative part is dominated by a lower comparison linear time-invariant (LTI) system associated with a fixed optimal policy, whereas the positive part is controlled by a linear switching system. The resulting bounds show that the negative-side LTI certificate is no slower than the positive-side switching certificate and may produce a faster exponential envelope. The analysis identifies a max-induced asymmetry in Q-learning error dynamics. This asymmetry is connected to overestimation: positive action-wise errors can be selected and propagated by the Bellman maximum, whereas negative errors admit an optimal-policy lower comparison. Finite-time bounds are provided for both deterministic and stochastic constant-step-size recursions.

AI.tldr: The paper provides a finite-time, sign-separated error analysis for constant step-size Q-learning, revealing an inherent asymmetry between positive and negative errors and its link to overestimation.
AI.motivation: To move beyond asymptotic analyses of Q-learning and understand how errors evolve in finite time under constant step-size updates, particularly how the maximization in the Bellman operator induces overestimation and asymmetric error dynamics.
AI.method: Model Q-learning as a switching system and decompose its error into componentwise positive and negative parts. Bound the negative error using a lower comparison linear time-invariant system tied to a fixed optimal policy, and bound the positive error via a linear switching system. Derive exponential-type finite-time bounds for deterministic and stochastic recursions.
AI.result: The authors obtain separate finite-time bounds for positive and negative components of the Q-learning error. They show that the LTI-based bound for the negative part is never slower—and can be faster—than the switching-system bound for the positive part, demonstrating a rigorously quantified asymmetry in error decay and behavior.
AI.conclusion: Q-learning’s error dynamics are inherently asymmetric due to the max operator: positive errors can be preferentially propagated (causing overestimation), while negative errors are constrained by comparison to an optimal policy. This leads to different finite-time convergence envelopes for positive and negative errors and clarifies the mechanism behind overestimation in constant step-size Q-learning.

title: Towards Generalization of Block Attention via Automatic Segmentation and Block Distillation
summary: Block attention, which processes the input as separate blocks that cannot attend to one another, offers significant potential to improve KV cache reuse in long-context scenarios such as Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). However, its broader application is hindered by two key challenges: the difficulty of segmenting input text into meaningful, self-contained blocks, and the inefficiency of existing block fine-tuning methods that risk degrading performance. To address these, we first construct SemanticSeg, a large and diverse semantic segmentation dataset containing over 30k instances across 16 categories-including books, code, web text, and conversations with text lengths ranging from 2k to 32k. Using this dataset, we train a lightweight segmenter to automatically partition text into human-instinct-aligned blocks with controllable granularity. Second, we propose block distillation, a training framework that is more efficient than block fine-tuning, which uses a frozen full-attention teacher model to guide the block-attention student. This framework integrates three novel components: block sink tokens to mitigate information loss at block boundaries, block dropout to leverage training signals from all blocks, and token-level loss weighting to focus learning on block-attention-sensitive tokens. Experiments across multiple models and benchmarks demonstrate that our segmenter outperforms heuristic and statistical baselines, and block distillation achieves near-full-attention performance under block attention, establishing a practical and scalable pathway for deploying block attention.

AI.tldr: They introduce a practical way to use block attention for long-context models by learning how to segment text into good blocks and distilling a full-attention model into a block-attention model so that performance stays close to standard attention while enabling better KV cache reuse.
AI.motivation: Block attention improves KV cache reuse and efficiency in long-context tasks like RAG, but it is hard to decide where to split text into independent blocks, and existing ways to adapt models to block attention are inefficient and often hurt accuracy. The paper aims to remove these barriers so block attention can be widely and reliably used in practice.
AI.method: 1) Build SemanticSeg, a 30k+ example dataset across 16 domains (books, code, web, dialog, etc., up to 32k tokens) annotated with human-intuitive semantic blocks; 2) Train a lightweight segmenter on SemanticSeg to automatically cut text into meaningful blocks with controllable block size; 3) Propose a block distillation framework where a frozen full-attention teacher guides a block-attention student via distillation losses, augmented with (a) block sink tokens at boundaries to reduce cross-block information loss, (b) block dropout so the student learns from all blocks rather than overfitting to specific patterns, and (c) token-level loss weighting that emphasizes tokens most affected by block attention.
AI.result: The learned segmenter produces better block partitions than heuristic and statistical methods across domains. The block-distilled block-attention models reach performance close to their full-attention teachers on several models and benchmarks, while benefiting from block attention’s efficiency and KV cache reuse advantages in long-context settings.
AI.conclusion: With good automatic segmentation and an efficient distillation-based training scheme incorporating block sink tokens, block dropout, and token-weighted losses, block attention can match nearly full-attention quality while bringing substantial efficiency gains. This makes block attention a practical, scalable choice for deploying long-context language models, especially in scenarios like RAG where KV cache reuse is crucial.

title: ShopGym: An Integrated Framework for Realistic Simulation and Scalable Benchmarking of E-Commerce Web Agents
summary: Developing and evaluating e-commerce web agents requires environments that preserve meaningful task structure while enabling controllable, reproducible, and scalable scientific comparison. Existing methodologies force a tradeoff: live storefronts provide realism but are non-stationary, difficult to inspect, and irreproducible, while hand-built sandbox benchmarks provide control but cover only a narrow range of layouts, catalogs, policies, and interaction patterns. We argue that the core bottleneck is methodological: the field lacks a scalable way to construct evaluation settings that are simultaneously realistic, diverse, controllable, inspectable, and reproducible. We introduce ShopGym, an integrated framework for realistic simulation and scalable benchmarking of e-commerce web agents. ShopGym is a framework for constructing e-commerce simulation environments and grounded benchmark tasks. Its simulation layer, ShopArena, converts live seed storefronts into self-contained sandbox shops through anonymized shop specifications and a staged, validated generation process. On top of these simulated storefronts, ShopGuru synthesizes benchmark tasks across seven skill categories, grounding each task in the shop's catalog, navigation structure, policies, and interaction affordances. Together, ShopArena and ShopGuru produce self-contained, resettable, inspectable, and stable evaluation artifacts that preserve structural properties and agent-evaluation signals relevant to shopping tasks. We validate the framework through graph-based structural analysis and agent-based behavioral evaluation with 224 generated tasks across six sandbox shops: three constructed with synthetic data and three with real data. Our results show that the synthetic shops preserve key structural properties of live storefronts, with agent performance on synthetic shops positively correlated with performance on live storefronts.

AI.tldr: Introduces ShopGym, a framework to realistically and reproducibly simulate e-commerce websites for evaluating web agents.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluation methods for e-commerce web agents face a tradeoff between realism and control: live storefronts are realistic but non-stationary, hard to inspect, and irreproducible, while hand-crafted sandboxes are controllable but narrow and unrepresentative. The field lacks a scalable methodology to create environments that are simultaneously realistic, diverse, controllable, inspectable, and reproducible.
AI.method: Propose ShopGym, which consists of two main components. ShopArena takes live seed storefronts and converts them into anonymized, self-contained sandbox shops via a staged and validated generation pipeline, preserving structural properties like catalog organization, navigation, policies, and interaction patterns. ShopGuru sits on top of these simulated shops and automatically synthesizes benchmark tasks across seven skill categories, grounding each task in the store’s actual catalog, navigation, policies, and UI affordances. The authors then evaluate the framework via graph-based structural analysis of the generated shops and behavioral experiments with web agents on 224 tasks across six sandbox shops (three synthetic, three based on real data).
AI.result: The synthetic sandbox shops produced by ShopArena retain key structural characteristics of real-world storefronts according to graph-based analysis. Agent performance on synthetic shops shows positive correlation with performance on live storefronts, indicating that the synthetic environments are predictive of real-world behavior. The framework successfully generates 224 tasks across six sandbox shops spanning synthetic and real data.
AI.conclusion: ShopGym provides a scalable, reproducible, and controllable way to evaluate e-commerce web agents while preserving realism and diversity. The combination of ShopArena and ShopGuru yields stable, inspectable benchmarks whose structural and behavioral properties align with those of live storefronts, mitigating the tradeoff between realism and experimental control in web agent evaluation.

title: Reference-Free Reinforcement Learning Fine-Tuning for MT: A Seq2Seq Perspective
summary: Production machine translation relies overwhelmingly on encoder-decoder Seq2Seq models, yet reinforcement learning approaches to MT fine-tuning have largely targeted decoder-only LLMs at $\geq$7B parameters, with limited systematic study of encoder-decoder architectures. We apply Group Relative Policy Optimization to NLLB-200 (600M and 1.3B) using a hybrid reference-free reward (LaBSE and COMET-Kiwi) that requires no parallel data at fine-tuning time, evaluating across 13 typologically diverse languages. GRPO yields consistent improvements on all 13 languages, up to $+$5.03 chrF++ for Traditional Chinese, and, without any target-language data, competes with 3-epoch supervised fine-tuning on morphologically complex languages . We identify a consistent empirical pattern in which gains are largest where baseline performance is weakest and reward discriminability is highest, making this approach most effective precisely where parallel data is scarcest, and replicate this pattern across English and Spanish source languages.

AI.tldr: The paper fine-tunes encoder-decoder MT models with reinforcement learning (GRPO) using a reference-free reward, achieving consistent gains across 13 languages, especially low-resource ones.
AI.motivation: Production MT mostly uses encoder-decoder Seq2Seq models, but recent RL-based MT fine-tuning has focused on large decoder-only LLMs (≥7B parameters) and lacks systematic study on encoder-decoder architectures. There is also a need for methods that do not require parallel data for fine-tuning, particularly for low-resource languages.
AI.method: Apply Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), a reinforcement learning algorithm, to NLLB-200 models (600M and 1.3B parameters). Use a hybrid, reference-free reward combining LaBSE (semantic similarity) and COMET-Kiwi (quality estimation) so that no parallel data is needed at fine-tuning time. Evaluate effects across 13 typologically diverse languages, with experiments involving both English and Spanish as source languages.
AI.result: GRPO fine-tuning yields consistent translation quality improvements across all 13 languages, with gains up to +5.03 chrF++ for Traditional Chinese. In morphologically complex languages, this approach, despite using no target-language parallel data during fine-tuning, achieves performance comparable to 3-epoch supervised fine-tuning. The largest improvements occur for languages where the baseline model is weakest and the reward signal is most discriminative.
AI.conclusion: Reinforcement learning with GRPO can effectively fine-tune encoder-decoder MT models using purely reference-free rewards, providing substantial benefits especially for low-resource or difficult languages where parallel data is scarce. The relationship between baseline performance, reward discriminability, and gains appears systematic and reproducible across different source languages, suggesting a robust pattern that can guide where and when to apply this method.

title: Property-Guided LLM Program Synthesis for Planning
summary: LLMs have shown impressive success in program synthesis, discovering programs that surpass prior solutions. However, these approaches rely on simple numeric scores to signal program quality, such as the value of the solution or the number of passed tests. Because a score offers no guidance on why a program failed, the system must generate and evaluate many candidates hoping some succeed, increasing LLM inference and evaluation costs. We study a different approach: property-guided LLM program synthesis. Instead of scoring programs after evaluation, we check whether a candidate satisfies a formally defined property. When the property is violated, we stop the evaluation early and provide the LLM with a concrete counterexample showing exactly how the program failed. This feedback drastically reduces both the number of program generations and the evaluation cost, and can guide the LLM to generate stronger programs. We evaluate this approach on PDDL planning domains, asking the LLM to synthesize direct heuristic functions: every state reachable by strictly improving transitions has a strictly improving successor. A heuristic with this property leads hill-climbing algorithm directly to a goal state. A counterexample-guided repair loop generates one candidate program, checks the property over a training set, and returns the first case that violates the property. We evaluate our approach on ten planning domains with an out-of-distribution test set. The synthesized heuristics are effectively direct on virtually all test tasks, and compared to the best prior generation method our approach generates seven times fewer programs per domain on average, solves more tasks without using search, and requires several orders of magnitude less computation to evaluate candidates. Whenever a problem admits a verifiable property, property-guided LLM synthesis can reduce cost and improve program quality.

AI.tldr: They propose property-guided LLM program synthesis, where candidate programs are checked against formal properties and counterexamples are fed back to the LLM, reducing generations and evaluation cost while improving quality.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based program synthesis typically optimizes simple scalar scores (e.g., number of passed tests), which do not explain *why* a program failed. This forces the system to generate and evaluate many candidates, making the process computationally expensive and sample-inefficient. The authors aim to make LLM-based synthesis more efficient and produce higher-quality programs by leveraging richer, formally defined feedback instead of opaque numeric scores.
AI.method: They introduce property-guided LLM program synthesis: instead of scoring programs post hoc, they verify candidate programs against a formal property. If the property is violated, evaluation halts early and a concrete counterexample is returned to the LLM, indicating precisely how the program failed. They instantiate this on PDDL planning domains, asking the LLM to synthesize direct heuristic functions with a specific property: every state reachable by strictly improving transitions has a strictly improving successor, which ensures that hill-climbing leads directly to a goal. A counterexample-guided repair loop iteratively generates one candidate heuristic, checks it on a training set, and, upon violation, returns the first failing case to guide the next generation.
AI.result: On ten PDDL planning domains with out-of-distribution test sets, the synthesized heuristics are effectively direct on almost all test tasks. Compared to the best prior generation method, their approach generates roughly seven times fewer candidate programs per domain, solves more tasks without any search, and uses several orders of magnitude less computation for candidate evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Whenever problems admit formally verifiable properties, property-guided feedback can significantly reduce the cost and improve the effectiveness of LLM-based program synthesis. Counterexample-guided repair using such properties leads to more efficient search in program space and stronger synthesized programs than approaches that rely solely on numeric scores.

title: Ontology for Policing: Conceptual Knowledge Learning for Semantic Understanding and Reasoning in Law Enforcement Reports
summary: Law enforcement reports contain structured fields and written narratives. However, many incident facts that are needed for review, police training, and investigations are in natural language and require manual reading. We propose a framework using symbolic methods for converting narratives into evidence-linked facts. Our objective is to measure the value of narratives to recover incident details only from the unstructured text and build temporal graphs with time cues and domain axioms. We achieve this by redacting personal identifiers, semantic parsing, predicate mapping to ontology, and reasoning. We evaluate the symbolic approach on 450 property crime reports and a short human review. Of the extracted events from the system, 54.1% had a confidence score of at least 0.80 and 93.7% were mapped through the PropBank--VerbNet--WordNet semantic path. 100% agreement was reached on incident initiation, stolen items, and temporal cues and lower agreement for forced entry interpretation.

AI.tldr: They turn free-text police narratives into structured, time-linked incident facts using symbolic NLP and show these narratives contain reliably recoverable details.
AI.motivation: Police reports contain crucial incident details only in unstructured narratives, which are hard to search, review, and use for training or investigation without manual reading. There is a need to automatically extract precise, explainable incident facts and timelines from these narratives so they can be systematically analyzed and reused.
AI.method: They design a symbolic NLP pipeline that: (1) redacts personal identifiers; (2) semantically parses narratives; (3) maps predicates to an ontology via resources like PropBank, VerbNet, and WordNet; and (4) applies logical reasoning and domain axioms to build temporal graphs of incident events and facts. They then run this system on 450 property-crime reports and conduct a brief human review of results.
AI.result: From the narratives, the system extracted events where 54.1% had high confidence (≥0.80). 93.7% of events could be semantically mapped through the PropBank–VerbNet–WordNet chain into the ontology. Human reviewers fully agreed (100% agreement) on system outputs concerning incident initiation, stolen items, and temporal cues, while agreement was lower for interpreting forced entry events.
AI.conclusion: Symbolic, ontology-based methods can convert unstructured police report narratives into structured, temporally organized incident facts with reasonably high confidence and strong human agreement on many key aspects, though some complex concepts like forced entry remain challenging and need further work.

title: Look Before You Leap: Autonomous Exploration for LLM Agents
summary: Large language model based agents often fail in unfamiliar environments due to premature exploitation: a tendency to act on prior knowledge before acquiring sufficient environment-specific information. We identify autonomous exploration as a critical yet underexplored capability for building adaptive agents. To formalize and quantify this capability, we introduce Exploration Checkpoint Coverage, a verifiable metric that measures how broadly an agent discovers key states, objects, and affordances. Our systematic evaluation reveals that agents trained with standard task-oriented reinforcement learning consistently exhibit narrow and repetitive behaviors that impede downstream performance. To address this limitation, we develop a training strategy that interleaves task-execution rollouts and exploration rollouts, with each type of rollout optimized by its corresponding verifiable reward. Building on this training strategy, we propose the Explore-then-Act paradigm, which decouples information-gathering from task execution: agents first utilize an interaction budget to acquire grounded environmental knowledge, then leverage it for task resolution. Our results demonstrate that learning to systematically explore is imperative for building generalizable and real-world-ready agents.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and addresses the tendency of LLM-based agents to exploit prior knowledge too early in unfamiliar environments, proposing a formal exploration metric and a new Explore-then-Act training paradigm that explicitly trains and separates exploration from task execution, leading to more generalizable agents.
AI.motivation: LLM-based agents often fail in new or complex environments because they act too quickly based on prior knowledge instead of gathering sufficient environment-specific information. Existing reinforcement learning approaches tend to produce narrow, repetitive behaviors that limit generalization and downstream performance. There is a need for a formal way to measure exploration quality and for training methods that promote robust, autonomous exploration in order to make agents adaptive and real-world ready.
AI.method: 1) Define a verifiable metric called Exploration Checkpoint Coverage to quantify how broadly an agent discovers key states, objects, and affordances. 2) Conduct systematic evaluations of standard task-oriented RL-trained agents to analyze their exploration behaviors using this metric. 3) Propose a training strategy that interleaves two types of rollouts: task-execution rollouts and exploration rollouts, each optimized by its own verifiable reward signal. 4) Build on this strategy to introduce the Explore-then-Act paradigm, where agents first spend an interaction budget purely on exploration and knowledge acquisition, and only then perform task execution using the gathered information.
AI.result: The evaluation shows that conventional task-oriented RL agents have constrained, repetitive exploration that harms downstream performance. In contrast, agents trained with the proposed interleaved rollout strategy and Explore-then-Act paradigm achieve broader exploration coverage (higher Exploration Checkpoint Coverage) and consequently perform better on downstream tasks, especially in unfamiliar environments.
AI.conclusion: Autonomous, systematic exploration is a crucial and underdeveloped capability for LLM-based agents. By formally measuring exploration via Exploration Checkpoint Coverage and by separating exploration from task execution through interleaved training and the Explore-then-Act paradigm, agents become more generalizable and better suited for real-world deployment. Learning to explore effectively is not optional but essential for robust agent performance across environments.

title: Defining Cultural Capabilities for AI Evaluation: A Taxonomy Grounded in Intercultural Communication Theory
summary: Tremendous efforts have been put into evaluating the inclusivity and effectiveness of AI systems across cultures. However, the cultural capabilities considered in much of the literature remain vaguely defined, are referred to using interchangeable terminology, and are typically limited to recalling accurate information about various demographics, regions, and nationalities. To address this construct ambiguity, we draw from Intercultural Communication scholarship and propose a three-level taxonomy of AI-relevant cultural capabilities: Cultural Awareness answers "Does the model know?", Cultural Sensitivity answers "How does it frame its knowledge?", and Cultural Competence answers "Can it adapt as the interaction evolves?". Beyond conceptual clarification, we position this taxonomy as a practical tool for improving the validity and interpretability of AI evaluation in real-world, multicultural settings. Without such construct clarity, evaluation results risk overstating model capabilities and may lead to inappropriate deployment decisions in culturally sensitive contexts.

AI.tldr: The paper clarifies and structures what it means for AI systems to have “cultural capabilities,” proposing a three-level taxonomy—awareness, sensitivity, and competence—to improve how we evaluate AI in multicultural settings.
AI.motivation: Existing work on culturally inclusive and effective AI uses vague, overlapping terms, and usually checks only whether models can recall factual information about different cultures. This ambiguity makes it hard to know what exactly is being measured and risks overstating model abilities, which can be harmful when AI is deployed in culturally sensitive contexts. The authors want to provide conceptual clarity to support more rigorous, meaningful evaluation.
AI.method: The authors draw on theories and constructs from Intercultural Communication scholarship to define and organize AI-relevant cultural capabilities. They synthesize this literature to propose a three-level taxonomy—Cultural Awareness, Cultural Sensitivity, and Cultural Competence—each tied to a specific question about model behavior. They then argue how this taxonomy can be used as a framework to design and interpret evaluations of AI systems in multicultural, real-world scenarios.
AI.result: The outcome is a conceptual taxonomy specifying three distinct types of cultural capabilities: (1) Cultural Awareness: whether the model has relevant cultural knowledge; (2) Cultural Sensitivity: whether the model frames and expresses that knowledge in appropriate, respectful ways; and (3) Cultural Competence: whether the model can adapt its responses as an intercultural interaction unfolds. This framework highlights that factual accuracy alone is insufficient to claim cultural capability in AI.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that clear, theoretically grounded constructs are essential for evaluating AI in multicultural contexts. Their three-level taxonomy offers a practical tool to design more valid, interpretable evaluations and to prevent misinterpretation or overclaiming of model cultural capabilities. Without such clarity, evaluation results can mislead stakeholders and contribute to problematic or harmful deployments in culturally sensitive settings.

title: An Algebraic Exposition of the Theory of Dyadic Morality
summary: This paper provides an algebraic exposition of the theory of dyadic morality (TDM), a psychological model of moral judgment grounded in a simple two-node template: an intentional agent causing harm to a vulnerable patient. We formalize TDM using structural causal modeling (SCM) notation and identify three psychological operators (typecasting operator, completion operator, and valence-dependent inference mechanism) that extend standard SCM to capture how people compute moral judgments under constraints. We address scalability challenges arising from TDM's dyadic limitation, showing how moral cognition compresses multi-node scenarios through node collapse and sequential processing. Drawing on this algebraic framework, we demonstrate concrete applications to AI policy design: detecting conflicting obligations, structuring helpfulness policies to preserve user agency, and designing post-failure communication as causal interventions. Finally, we recommend scoped, contextual measurement of mind perception over universal averaging to operationalize the theory empirically. This algebraic formalization enables neurosymbolic AI systems to compute morality in a way that is both mathematically rigorous and faithful to human moral cognition.

AI.tldr: Algebraic formalization of the Theory of Dyadic Morality (TDM) using structural causal models to capture how humans make moral judgments, with applications to AI policy design and measurement.
AI.motivation: TDM is a prominent psychological model of moral judgment centered on an agent harming a patient, but it lacks a rigorous algebraic and causal formalization and clear pathways to implementation in AI systems. The paper aims to provide this formal scaffolding so that human-like moral cognition can be represented, analyzed, scaled beyond simple dyads, and practically applied in AI design and evaluation.
AI.method: The authors recast TDM within structural causal modeling (SCM) notation, identifying three key psychological operators—typecasting, completion, and valence-dependent inference—that extend standard SCM to better reflect human moral reasoning. They analyze how complex, multi-agent moral scenarios can be reduced via node collapse and sequential processing within this formalism. They then map this framework onto concrete AI policy problems (e.g., conflict detection, preserving user agency, post-failure communication) to show how the algebra can guide design choices. They also propose a measurement strategy for mind perception that is contextual and scoped rather than based on global averages.
AI.result: (1) A formal SCM-based algebra for TDM that specifies how agents, patients, intentions, harm, and moral valence interact; (2) definitions of three operators that implement psychological processes of moral judgment under cognitive constraints; (3) an account of how dyadic reasoning can scale to multi-node cases through compression mechanisms; (4) illustrative AI policy applications showing how the framework can reveal conflicts, shape helpfulness policies, and structure post-failure interventions; and (5) a concrete recommendation for empirically operationalizing mind perception in a way consistent with the formal theory.
AI.conclusion: The algebraic SCM formalization of TDM offers a rigorous, implementable account of human moral cognition that can be embedded in neurosymbolic AI systems. By incorporating dyadic structure, specialized psychological operators, and scalable compression mechanisms, AI systems can compute moral judgments that better mirror human intuitions. Additionally, context-sensitive measurement of mind perception is essential for testing and refining the theory in practice, supporting its use in real-world AI policy and system design.

title: Can Vision Language Models Be Adaptive in Mathematics Education? A Learner Model-based Rubric Study
summary: Adaptive learning refers to educational technologies that track learners' learning progress and adapt the instructional process based on individual learners' learning performance. It is increasingly recognized as critical for developing an effective learning support tool. Vision language models (VLMs) have seen adoption in mathematics education, and students have been using them as learning aids for personalized instruction. However, it is unknown whether VLMs have the ability to adapt to different learner profiles when providing mathematical instructions. Current VLMs lack a systematic evaluation framework for this adaptivity to different learner profiles in mathematics tutoring tasks. To address this gap, we draw on the learner model from the adaptive learning framework (Shute and Towle, 2018) and propose a learner model-based rubric. Our rubric formalizes adaptivity assessment into three aspects: cognitive aspects, motivational aspects, and complexity. We also evaluate two additional dimensions of VLM responses: correctness (of answers and solutions) and quality (of the response itself). Our experimental results show measurable differences in adaptivity across models and also reveal that current VLMs struggle to consistently produce learner model-based instructional responses, especially when receiving limited learner information.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well current vision-language models (VLMs) can adapt their math tutoring to different learner profiles, using a rubric based on an established adaptive learning learner model.
AI.motivation: While VLMs are increasingly used in mathematics education as personalized learning aids, it is unclear whether they actually adapt instruction to different types of learners. There is also no systematic framework to evaluate such adaptivity in the context of math tutoring. The paper aims to fill this evaluation gap to understand the true adaptive capabilities and limitations of VLM-based tutors.
AI.method: The authors adopt the learner model from Shute and Towle’s adaptive learning framework and translate it into a concrete rubric for assessing VLM responses. This rubric operationalizes adaptivity into three aspects—cognitive aspects, motivational aspects, and complexity—and adds two further evaluation dimensions: correctness of answers/solutions and overall response quality. Using this rubric, they experimentally evaluate multiple VLMs on math tutoring tasks conditioned on different learner profiles and amounts of learner information.
AI.result: The experiments reveal measurable differences in adaptivity performance across different VLMs. However, they also show that none of the current models can reliably produce responses that are well-aligned with the learner model—particularly when the provided learner information is sparse or limited.
AI.conclusion: The proposed learner model-based rubric provides a structured way to assess adaptive behavior in VLMs for math tutoring. Current VLMs display some adaptive capabilities but struggle to generate consistently learner-tailored instructional responses, especially under limited learner data. This indicates that substantial work remains to build truly adaptive VLM-based educational tools and that future model and system design should explicitly target the dimensions captured by the rubric.

title: Formal Methods Meet LLMs: Auditing, Monitoring, and Intervention for Compliance of Advanced AI Systems
summary: We examine one particular dimension of AI governance: how to monitor and audit AI-enabled products and services throughout the AI development lifecycle, from pre-deployment testing to post-deployment auditing. Combining principles from formal methods with SoTA machine learning, we propose techniques that enable AI-enabled product and service developers, as well as third party AI developers and evaluators, to perform offline auditing and online (runtime) monitoring of product-specific (temporally extended) behavioral constraints such as safety constraints, norms, rules and regulations with respect to black-box advanced AI systems, notably LLMs. We further provide practical techniques for predictive monitoring, such as sampling-based methods, and we introduce intervening monitors that act at runtime to preempt and potentially mitigate predicted violations. Experimental results show that by exploiting the formal syntax and semantics of Linear Temporal Logic (LTL), our proposed auditing and monitoring techniques are superior to LLM baseline methods in detecting violations of temporally extended behavioral constraints; with our approach, even small-model labelers match or exceed frontier LLM judges. Our predictive and intervening monitors significantly reduce the violation rates of LLM-based agents while largely preserving task performance. We further show through controlled experiments that LLMs' temporal reasoning shows a pronounced degradation in accuracy with increasing event distance, number of constraints, and number of propositions.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a formal-logic-based framework for monitoring, auditing, and intervening on AI systems (especially LLM agents) across their lifecycle, to detect and reduce violations of temporal behavioral constraints.
AI.motivation: As AI systems, particularly LLMs, are increasingly embedded in products and services, developers and regulators need reliable ways to ensure that these systems comply with safety constraints, norms, rules, and regulations over time. Existing LLM-based judges and ad hoc evaluation methods struggle with temporally extended behaviors and black-box models, especially as scenarios become longer and more complex. There is a gap in practical, rigorous, and scalable tools for lifecycle monitoring and auditing of AI behavior, both pre- and post-deployment.
AI.method: The authors combine formal methods—especially Linear Temporal Logic (LTL)—with state-of-the-art machine learning. They encode product-specific, temporally extended behavioral constraints as LTL formulas and develop techniques for (1) offline auditing of logs and test runs and (2) online runtime monitoring of AI-enabled products and services, treating the underlying advanced AI system (e.g., an LLM) as a black box. They design predictive monitors using sampling-based methods to forecast future violations before they occur, and they introduce intervening monitors that can act at runtime to preempt or mitigate predicted violations. They compare these formal-methods-driven monitors against LLM-based baseline judges and labelers, including small models and frontier models, under varied temporal reasoning conditions (event distance, number of constraints, number of propositions).
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that LTL-based auditing and monitoring techniques outperform LLM baseline methods at detecting violations of temporally extended behavioral constraints. Notably, small-model labelers, when guided by the formal structure of LTL, match or exceed the performance of frontier LLM judges. Predictive and intervening monitors substantially lower the violation rates of LLM-based agents while mostly preserving task performance. Additional controlled studies reveal that LLM temporal reasoning accuracy degrades significantly as sequences become longer, constraints grow in number, and propositions increase in complexity.
AI.conclusion: Formal, LTL-driven monitoring and auditing offer a powerful and practical approach to AI governance for black-box advanced AI systems. By leveraging explicit temporal specifications, developers and third-party evaluators can more reliably detect and even preempt behavioral violations across the AI lifecycle, without sacrificing much task performance. The work also highlights a systematic weakness in current LLMs’ temporal reasoning, underscoring the need for complementary formal methods to ensure compliance with safety constraints, norms, rules, and regulations in AI-enabled products and services.

title: Judge Circuits
summary: LLM-as-a-judge has become the dominant paradigm for grading model outputs at scale, yet the same model assigns systematically different scores when its output format changes (e.g., a 1-5 rating vs. a True/False label). Existing diagnoses of these format-induced inconsistencies stop at the input-output level. Using Position-aware Edge Attribution Patching (PEAP), we causally investigate the internal mechanism in Gemma-3, Qwen2.5, and Llama-3. We find that judgments across structured understanding and open-ended preference tasks share a sparse, generalized Latent Evaluator sub-graph in the mid-to-late multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs); zero-ablating it collapses judgment while preserving world knowledge in architecturally modular models. By structurally decoupling abstract judging from output formatting, we provide a mechanistic account of format-induced inconsistency on the open-weight models we study: a continuous judgment signal computed in the shared trunk is mapped through fragile, format-specific terminal branches, enabling format-independent preference to be isolated downstream of the requested output format. Our findings imply that benchmark-level reliability comparisons across formats are partially measuring formatter geometry rather than evaluation quality.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that large language models use a shared internal ‘Latent Evaluator’ circuit to form judgments, and that inconsistencies across rating formats come from fragile, format-specific output mappings rather than the evaluation itself.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used as automatic judges for model outputs, but the same judge model can give inconsistent scores just because the requested output format changes (e.g., 1–5 scale vs. True/False). Existing work mainly documents this behavior at the input-output level and lacks a causal, mechanistic understanding of why it happens inside the network. This paper aims to uncover the internal structures responsible for judgment and how they relate to output formats, to better understand and potentially fix format-induced inconsistencies.
AI.method: The authors apply Position-aware Edge Attribution Patching (PEAP), a mechanistic interpretability technique, to trace and causally test which internal connections are responsible for judgment behavior in several open-weight LLMs (Gemma-3, Qwen2.5, Llama-3). They search for sparse subgraphs in the mid-to-late MLP layers that carry evaluation signals across both structured understanding and open-ended preference tasks. They then ablate these identified subgraphs (e.g., zeroing their contributions) to test whether judgment ability and world knowledge can be selectively affected, and analyze how the continuous internal judgment signal is routed to different output formats via format-specific “terminal branches.”
AI.result: They identify a sparse, generalized “Latent Evaluator” subgraph in mid-to-late MLP layers that is consistently involved in judgment across diverse tasks and formats. Causal ablations of this subgraph largely destroy the model’s ability to judge while leaving its world knowledge mostly intact, especially in models with more modular architectures. They further show that a continuous, format-agnostic judgment signal from this shared trunk is funneled through fragile, format-specific branches near the output, so that small differences in output format prompt can lead to different surface scores despite relying on the same underlying evaluation signal.
AI.conclusion: Judging and formatting are structurally decoupled in the studied LLMs: a shared latent evaluator computes a continuous preference signal, and separate, format-specific output branches translate that signal into discrete formats. Format-induced inconsistencies thus arise mainly from the geometry and brittleness of these terminal branches rather than from differences in the underlying evaluation quality. As a result, comparisons of benchmark reliability across formats are partially confounded by properties of the formatter, and more reliable LLM-as-a-judge systems should explicitly account for or redesign these format-mapping components.

title: Context, Reasoning, and Hierarchy: A Cost-Performance Study of Compound LLM Agent Design in an Adversarial POMDP
summary: Deploying compound LLM agents in adversarial, partially observable sequential environments requires navigating several design dimensions: (1) what the agent sees, (2) how it reasons, and (3) how tasks are decomposed across components. Yet practitioners lack guidance on which design choices improve performance versus merely increase inference costs. We present a controlled study of compound LLM agent design in CybORG CAGE-2, a cyber defense environment modeled as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP). Reward is non-positive, so all configurations operate in a failure-mitigation mode. Our evaluation spans five model families, six models, and twelve configurations (3,475 episodes) with token-level cost accounting. We vary context representation (raw observations vs. a deterministic state-tracking layer with compressed history), deliberation (self-questioning, self-critique, and self-improvement tools, with optional chain-of-thought prompting), and hierarchical decomposition (monolithic ReAct vs. delegation to specialized sub-agents). We find that: (1) Programmatic state abstraction delivers the largest returns per token spent (RPTS), improving mean return by up to 76% over raw observations. (2) Distributing deliberation tools across a hierarchy degrades performance relative to hierarchy alone for all five model families, reaching up to 3.4$\times$ worse mean return while using 1.8-2.7$\times$ more tokens. We call this destructive pattern a deliberation cascade. (3) Hierarchical decomposition without deliberation achieves the best absolute performance for most models, and context engineering is generally more cost-effective than deliberation. These findings suggest a design principle for structured adversarial POMDPs: invest in programmatic infrastructure and clean task decomposition rather than deeper per-agent reasoning, as these strategies can interfere when combined.

AI.tldr: The paper systematically evaluates how different compound LLM agent designs perform and spend tokens in an adversarial, partially observable cyber-defense environment, and derives practical design principles.
AI.motivation: There is growing interest in using compound LLM agents (with tools, memory, and sub-agents) for complex sequential decision-making in adversarial, partially observable environments. However, practitioners lack empirical guidance on which architectural choices—observation representation, deliberation mechanisms, or task decomposition—actually improve performance versus simply inflating inference cost. The paper aims to provide controlled, quantitative evidence to separate effective design patterns from expensive but unhelpful complexity.
AI.method: The authors use CybORG CAGE-2, a cyber-defense benchmark modeled as a POMDP with non-positive rewards, meaning agents are always mitigating failure rather than accumulating positive reward. They evaluate five LLM families (six models) across twelve agent configurations, running 3,475 episodes with detailed token-level cost tracking. The study orthogonally varies: (1) context representation (direct/raw observations vs. a deterministic state-tracking layer that abstracts and compresses history), (2) deliberation strategies (self-questioning, self-critique, self-improvement tools, and optional chain-of-thought prompting), and (3) hierarchical decomposition (single monolithic ReAct-style agent vs. a hierarchy with specialized sub-agents). They then compare returns and returns-per-token-spent (RPTS) across these design choices.
AI.result: Programmatic state abstraction (via a deterministic state-tracking layer with compressed history) substantially improves efficiency, yielding up to 76% higher mean return compared to feeding raw observations, and achieving the best returns per token spent. Introducing rich deliberation tools (self-questioning, critique, improvement, CoT) and distributing them across a hierarchy generally harms performance: adding such deliberation to hierarchical agents consistently decreases mean return for all five model families, with up to 3.4× worse returns while consuming 1.8–2.7× more tokens. This negative interaction between hierarchy and distributed deliberation is termed a "deliberation cascade." Hierarchical decomposition without explicit deliberation is typically the strongest performer in absolute terms, and context engineering (state abstraction) is more cost-effective than deeper per-agent reasoning across models.
AI.conclusion: For structured adversarial POMDP settings like cyber defense, the most effective and cost-efficient strategy is to invest in programmatic state abstraction and clean hierarchical task decomposition rather than layering on heavy internal deliberation mechanisms for each agent. Overly complex deliberation—especially when cascaded through a hierarchy—can interfere with coordination, inflate token costs, and reduce performance. A practical design principle emerges: build strong programmatic infrastructure and clear role decomposition first, and only then, if at all, add lightweight reasoning aids, as aggressive per-agent deliberation is not just wasteful but can be actively harmful in compound LLM agents.

title: From Flat Language Labels to Typological Priors: Structured Language Conditioning for Multilingual Speech-to-Speech Translation
comment: Submitted to IEEE/ACM TASLP. This work extends S2ST-Omni, accepted to Findings of ACL 2026
summary: Compositional speech-to-speech translation (S2ST) systems built upon speech large language models (SpeechLLMs) have recently shown promising performance. However, existing S2ST systems often either neglect source-language information or encode it through a language-as-label paradigm, representing each source language as an independent flat embedding. Such a design overlooks systematic linguistic structure shared across languages, which may limit data-efficient multilingual adaptation when supervised S2ST data are scarce. To address this issue, we propose S2ST-Omni 2, a many-to-one compositional S2ST framework that systematically reformulates multilingual language conditioning from flat language labels to structured typological priors. Specifically, S2ST-Omni 2 revisits language conditioning at three levels: typology-informed hierarchical language encoding for structured source-language representation, dynamically-gated language-aware Dual-CTC for content-adaptive acoustic modulation, and typology-aware LLM prompting for decoder-side linguistic guidance. Experiments on CVSS-C show that S2ST-Omni 2 achieves superior average performance among representative S2ST approaches across BLEU, COMET, ASR-BLEU, and BLASER 2.0 under the adopted evaluation protocol. Ablation studies indicate that the proposed representation-level, acoustic-level, and decoding-level strategies provide complementary benefits. Moreover, controlled data-budget analyses and a Japanese-to-English evaluation using only approximately 3 hours of supervised training data suggest that explicit typological priors provide useful inductive biases for data-efficient multilingual S2ST.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces S2ST-Omni 2, a speech-to-speech translation framework that replaces flat language labels with structured typological priors to improve multilingual, data-efficient translation.
AI.motivation: Existing SpeechLLM-based S2ST systems either ignore the source language or model it as independent flat language embeddings, failing to exploit shared linguistic structure across languages. This limits the ability to adapt efficiently in multilingual, low-resource settings where supervised S2ST data are scarce. The authors want to incorporate linguistic typology to better guide the model and enhance data efficiency and translation quality.
AI.method: They design S2ST-Omni 2, a many-to-one compositional S2ST framework that rethinks multilingual language conditioning at three levels: (1) Representation level: a typology-informed hierarchical language encoding that embeds languages according to structured typological properties rather than flat IDs. (2) Acoustic level: a dynamically-gated, language-aware Dual-CTC module that performs content-adaptive acoustic modulation conditioned on language information. (3) Decoding level: typology-aware prompting for the LLM decoder to provide language-specific linguistic guidance. The model is evaluated on the multilingual CVSS-C benchmark and compared to representative S2ST approaches using BLEU, COMET, ASR-BLEU, and BLASER 2.0 metrics. Ablation experiments and data-budget analyses are conducted to isolate contributions of each component and to quantify data efficiency, including a low-resource Japanese–English scenario with only ~3 hours of supervised data.
AI.result: On CVSS-C, S2ST-Omni 2 outperforms other representative S2ST methods on average across BLEU, COMET, ASR-BLEU, and BLASER 2.0 under a shared evaluation protocol. Ablations show that each of the three proposed strategies (hierarchical typology-based language encoding, language-aware Dual-CTC, and typology-aware prompting) yields gains and that they are complementary when combined. Data-budget experiments and a Japanese-to-English setting with around 3 hours of supervised S2ST data demonstrate that injecting explicit typological priors improves data efficiency and generalization in multilingual S2ST.
AI.conclusion: Structured typological priors provide effective inductive biases for SpeechLLM-based S2ST. By replacing flat language labels with hierarchical typology-informed encodings and incorporating language-aware mechanisms at the acoustic and decoding stages, S2ST-Omni 2 achieves better multilingual S2ST performance and stronger data efficiency, particularly in low-resource scenarios. The three-level conditioning design is complementary and offers a principled path toward more scalable, typology-informed multilingual S2ST systems.

title: Confirming Correct, Missing the Rest: LLM Tutoring Agents Struggle Where Feedback Matters Most
summary: Effective tutoring requires distinguishing optimal, valid but suboptimal, and incorrect student solutions, a distinction central to intelligent tutoring systems (ITS) but untested for LLM-based tutors. As LLMs are increasingly explored as conversational complements to ITS, evaluating their diagnostic precision is essential. We present a benchmark of seven LLM feedback agents in propositional logic using knowledge-graph-derived ground truth across 10,836 solution--feedback pairs and three feedback conditions. Models achieved near-ceiling performance on optimal steps but systematically over-rejected valid but suboptimal reasoning and over-validated incorrect solutions, precisely where adaptive tutoring matters most. These failures persisted across models regardless of solution context, suggesting architectural rather than informational limits. Moreover, accurate diagnosis did not reliably produce pedagogically actionable feedback, revealing a gap between diagnostic judgment and instructional effectiveness. Our findings suggest that LLMs are better suited for hybrid architectures where KG-grounded models handle diagnosis while LLMs support open-ended scaffolding and dialogue.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how accurately large language models diagnose student solutions in propositional logic and finds they fail most where nuanced tutoring is needed, suggesting a hybrid system with knowledge-graph-based diagnosis and LLM-based dialogue.
AI.motivation: Intelligent tutoring systems need to distinguish between optimal, suboptimal-but-valid, and incorrect student solutions to provide effective, adaptive feedback. As LLMs are being integrated as conversational components in such systems, it is important to understand whether they can perform this fine-grained diagnostic role reliably, especially in domains like propositional logic where reasoning steps are structured and evaluable.
AI.method: The authors construct a benchmark in propositional logic using a knowledge graph to generate ground-truth labels for student solution steps as optimal, valid but suboptimal, or incorrect. They then test seven different LLM feedback agents across 10,836 solution–feedback pairs under three different feedback conditions, comparing model judgments against the knowledge-graph-derived ground truth and analyzing performance across step types and contexts.
AI.result: All tested LLMs performed very well at recognizing optimal solution steps but consistently misclassified the more nuanced cases: they frequently rejected valid but suboptimal reasoning and often accepted incorrect solutions. These systematic errors were robust across models and independent of the surrounding solution context, indicating they likely stem from model architecture limits rather than insufficient information. Additionally, even when models diagnosed steps correctly, their written feedback did not consistently translate into pedagogically useful guidance for learners.
AI.conclusion: LLMs currently lack the diagnostic precision needed to fully replace the core diagnosis engine in intelligent tutoring systems, especially for nuanced reasoning steps where adaptive guidance matters most. The authors argue that LLMs are better used within hybrid architectures: structured, knowledge-graph-based components should handle fine-grained diagnostic evaluation, while LLMs focus on open-ended explanation, scaffolding, and dialogue to support learning once reliable diagnoses are available.

title: RecMem: Recurrence-based Memory Consolidation for Efficient and Effective Long-Running LLM Agents
summary: Memory systems often organize user-agent interactions as retrievable external memory and are crucial for long-running agents by overcoming the limited context windows of LLMs. However, existing memory systems invoke LLMs to process every incoming interaction for memory extraction, and such an eager memory consolidation scheme leads to substantial token consumption. To tackle this problem, we propose RecMem by rethinking when memory consolidation should be conducted. RecMem stores incoming interactions in a subconscious memory layer and encode them using lightweight embedding models for retrieval. LLMs are only invoked to extract episodic and semantic memory when sustained recurrence are observed for semantically similar interactions. Such recurrence-based consolidation works because these interactions correspond to a semantic cluster with rich information and thus are worth extraction and summarization. To improve accuracy, RecMem also incorporates a semantic refinement mechanism that recovers the fine-grained facts omitted by memory extraction. Experiments show that RecMem reduces the memory construction token cost of three SOTA memory systems by up to 87% while exceeding their accuracy.

AI.tldr: RecMem is a memory system for long‑running LLM agents that defers expensive LLM-based memory extraction until it detects repeated, semantically similar interactions, cutting token costs while improving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM memory systems perform eager memory consolidation: they call an LLM to extract and summarize memory for every user-agent interaction as it arrives. This approach is very costly in tokens and computation, especially for long-running agents, yet much of the processed content may be trivial, non-recurring, or unimportant. The authors aim to reduce this token overhead while preserving or even improving the usefulness and accuracy of the stored memories, enabling more efficient and scalable long-term memory for agents.
AI.method: The authors introduce RecMem, which separates memory into a subconscious layer and consolidated memory. All incoming interactions are first stored in the subconscious layer and indexed with lightweight embedding models to support retrieval without invoking a full LLM. RecMem monitors for sustained recurrence of semantically similar interactions; when a cluster of similar interactions recurs over time, RecMem invokes the LLM to extract episodic and semantic memories from that cluster and stores them as consolidated memory. This recurrence-based consolidation assumes that frequently recurring semantic clusters correspond to information worth summarizing. To address information loss from summarization, RecMem adds a semantic refinement mechanism: when retrieving and using consolidated memories, it can go back to the original subconscious interactions to recover fine-grained details omitted during extraction.
AI.result: In experiments comparing with three state-of-the-art LLM memory systems, RecMem achieves up to 87% reduction in token consumption for memory construction (i.e., calls to the LLM for extracting and summarizing memory). Despite this large reduction in token cost, RecMem matches or exceeds the accuracy of the baselines on evaluated tasks, demonstrating that recurrence-based consolidation and semantic refinement can maintain high-quality memory while being significantly more efficient.
AI.conclusion: RecMem shows that deferring LLM-based memory consolidation until semantically similar interactions recur is an effective strategy for long-term agent memory. By combining a lightweight subconscious memory with recurrence-triggered consolidation and semantic refinement, it drastically reduces token costs while improving or maintaining accuracy relative to existing systems. This suggests that when-to-consolidate is as important as what-to-store in designing scalable, high-quality memory for LLM agents.

title: Fully Open Meditron: An Auditable Pipeline for Clinical LLMs
comment: Preprint. 31 pages, 10 figures. Code, models, and data: https://github.com/EPFLiGHT/FullyOpenMeditron
summary: Clinical decision support systems (CDSS) require scrutable, auditable pipelines that enable rigorous, reproducible validation. Yet current LLM-based CDSS remain largely opaque. Most "open" models are open-weight only, releasing parameters while withholding the data provenance, curation procedures, and generation pipelines that determine model behavior. Fully Open (FO) models, which expose the complete training stack end-to-end, do not currently exist in medicine. We introduce Fully Open Meditron, the first fully open pipeline for building LLM-CDSS, comprising a clinician-audited training corpus, a reproducible data construction and training framework, and a use-aligned evaluation protocol. The corpus unifies eight public medical QA datasets into a normalized conversational format and expands coverage with three clinician-vetted synthetic extensions: exam-style QA, guideline-grounded QA derived from 46,469 clinical practice guidelines, and clinical vignettes. The pipeline enforces system-wide decontamination, gold-label resampling of teacher generations, and end-to-end validation by a four-physician panel. We evaluate using an LLM-as-a-judge protocol over expert-written clinical vignettes, calibrated against 204 human raters. We apply the recipe to five FO base models (Apertus-70B/8B-Instruct, OLMo-2-32B-SFT, EuroLLM-22B/9B-Instruct). All MeditronFO variants are preferred over their bases. Apertus-70B-MeditronFO improves +6.6 points over its base (47.2% to 53.8%) on aggregate medical benchmarks, establishing a new FO SoTA. Gemma-3-27B-MeditronFO is preferred over MedGemma in 58.6% of LLM-as-a-judge comparisons and outperforms it on HealthBench (58% vs 55.9%). These results show that fully open pipelines can achieve state-of-the-art domain-specific performance without sacrificing auditability or reproducibility.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Fully Open Meditron, the first fully open, end-to-end pipeline for training and evaluating large language model-based clinical decision support systems, achieving state-of-the-art performance while remaining fully auditable and reproducible.
AI.motivation: Most existing LLM-based clinical decision support systems are opaque: they release model weights but not the full details of training data, curation, and evaluation pipelines, making rigorous auditing, validation, and reproducibility difficult.
