In a medical context, this opacity is problematic because clinical tools must be scrutable, verifiable, and free from data leakage or contamination. There is a need for a truly fully open stack—data, processing, training, and evaluation—for domain-specific medical LLMs.
AI.method: The authors construct a fully open pipeline (Fully Open Meditron) that includes: (1) a clinician-audited medical training corpus that harmonizes eight public medical QA datasets into a normalized conversational format; (2) three clinician-vetted synthetic data extensions: exam-style QA, guideline-grounded QA generated from 46,469 clinical practice guidelines, and clinical vignettes; (3) strict data hygiene processes such as system-wide decontamination and resampling of gold-label teacher generations; and (4) end-to-end expert validation by a panel of four physicians. They train multiple base open models (Apertus-70B/8B-Instruct, OLMo-2-32B-SFT, EuroLLM-22B/9B-Instruct, and Gemma-3-27B) under this pipeline and evaluate them using an LLM-as-a-judge protocol over expert-written vignettes, calibrated with ratings from 204 human clinicians or raters. The entire data construction, training, and evaluation framework is made fully open and reproducible.
AI.result: All models trained with the MeditronFO pipeline outperform their respective base models. Specifically, Apertus-70B-MeditronFO achieves a +6.6-point gain on aggregate medical benchmarks (from 47.2% to 53.8%), setting a new state-of-the-art among fully open models in medicine. Gemma-3-27B-MeditronFO is preferred over MedGemma in 58.6% of pairwise LLM-as-a-judge comparisons and also surpasses it on HealthBench (58% vs. 55.9%). These gains demonstrate that the pipeline substantially improves clinical reasoning and QA performance without proprietary components.
AI.conclusion: Fully Open Meditron demonstrates that a completely transparent, auditable, and reproducible pipeline for medical LLM-based clinical decision support is feasible and can reach or surpass state-of-the-art performance. By releasing the full training stack—corpus, curation methods, synthetic data generation, training framework, and evaluation protocols—the work enables rigorous external validation, inspection for contamination or bias, and iterative community-driven improvement. This suggests that high-performance domain-specific CDSS do not need to rely on closed or partially open ecosystems and can instead be developed in a fully open, clinically aligned manner.

title: Can Large Language Models Imitate Human Speech for Clinical Assessment? LLM-Driven Data Augmentation for Cognitive Score Prediction
summary: Accurate assessment of cognitive decline from spontaneous speech remains challenging due to limited dataset size and class imbalance. In this work, we propose a large language model (LLM)-driven data augmentation framework to improve the prediction of cognitive scores from speech. Experiments are conducted on a Japanese corpus in which each participant provides both a spontaneous oral narrative and a written response to the same clinical prompt. The written responses serve as semantic anchors to generate multiple oral-like monologues in different styles using GPT-5. We then predict Hasegawa Dementia Scale scores, a widely used cognitive screening tool in Japan, using a Partial Least Squares regression model trained on Sentence-BERT speech embeddings. We investigate two augmentation strategies: random class-balanced selection, which yields moderate but unstable improvements, and similarity-guided class-balanced selection. The latter prioritizes semantically close synthetic samples, leading to more consistent improvements and substantially reducing prediction error for minority low-score participants while maintaining performance for the majority group. Overall, our findings demonstrate the potential of semantically guided LLM-driven augmentation as a principled approach for addressing class imbalance and improving data efficiency in clinical speech analysis.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an LLM-based data augmentation framework that uses written responses as semantic anchors to generate synthetic spontaneous speech, improving regression-based prediction of dementia screening scores, especially for underrepresented low-score participants.
AI.motivation: Assessing cognitive decline from spontaneous speech is difficult because datasets are small and cognitively impaired participants (low scores) are underrepresented, causing class imbalance and limiting model performance and reliability. The authors aim to improve prediction of cognitive scores from speech under these constraints.
AI.method: Using a Japanese clinical corpus where each participant provides a spontaneous oral narrative and a written response to the same prompt, the authors use an LLM (GPT-5) to generate multiple oral-like monologues conditioned on the written responses, which act as semantic anchors. They extract Sentence-BERT embeddings from real and synthetic speech transcripts and train Partial Least Squares regression models to predict Hasegawa Dementia Scale scores. They compare two augmentation schemes: (1) random class-balanced selection of synthetic samples, and (2) similarity-guided class-balanced selection that prefers synthetic utterances semantically close to the original written responses.
AI.result: Random class-balanced augmentation gives only moderate and unstable gains. In contrast, the similarity-guided strategy yields more consistent improvements, notably lowering prediction error for minority low-score participants without degrading performance on the majority higher-score group.
AI.conclusion: Semantically guided, LLM-driven data augmentation is an effective and principled way to handle class imbalance and limited data in clinical speech analysis. By using written responses as semantic anchors and prioritizing semantically close synthetic samples, the framework improves data efficiency and enhances cognitive score prediction, particularly for underrepresented clinically important low-score cases.

title: FORGE: Self-Evolving Agent Memory With No Weight Updates via Population Broadcast
summary: Can LLM agents improve decision-making through self-generated memory without gradient updates? We propose FORGE (Failure-Optimized Reflective Graduation and Evolution), a staged, population-based protocol that evolves prompt-injected natural-language memory for hierarchical ReAct agents. FORGE wraps a Reflexion-style inner loop, where a dedicated reflection agent (using the same underlying LLM, no distillation from a stronger model) converts failed trajectories into reusable knowledge artifacts: textual heuristics (Rules), few-shot demonstrations (Examples), or both (Mixed), with an outer loop that propagates the best-performing instance's memory to the population between stages and freezes converged instances via a graduation criterion. We evaluate on CybORG CAGE-2, a stochastic network-defense POMDP at a 30-step horizon against the B-line attacker, where all four tested LLM families (Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite, Grok-4-Fast, Llama-4-Maverick, Qwen3-235B) exhibit strongly negative, heavy-tailed zero-shot rewards. Compared against both a zero-shot baseline and a Reflexion baseline (isolated single-stream learning), FORGE improves average evaluation return by 1.7-7.7$\times$ over zero-shot and by 29-72% over Reflexion in all 12 model-representation conditions, reducing major-failure rates (below $-100$) to as low as $\sim$1%. We find that (1) population broadcast is critical mechanism, with a no-graduation ablation confirming that broadcast carries the performance gains while graduation primarily saves compute; (2) Examples achieves the strongest returns for three of four models, Rules offers the best cost-reliability profile with $\sim$40% fewer tokens; and (3) weaker baseline models benefit disproportionately, suggesting FORGE may mitigate capability gaps rather than amplify strong models. All evidence is confined to CAGE-2 B-line; cross-family findings are directional evidence.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FORGE, a population-based, memory-evolving protocol that lets LLM agents improve long-horizon decision-making purely via prompt-injected natural-language memories, without gradient updates, and shows large gains on a hard cyber-defense task over zero-shot and Reflexion baselines.
AI.motivation: LLMs often perform poorly and unreliably on complex, long-horizon decision-making tasks, especially in stochastic, partially observable environments like cyber defense. Typical improvement methods rely on gradient-based fine-tuning, stronger teacher models, or ad-hoc prompt engineering, which can be expensive, brittle, or unavailable. The authors want to know whether agents powered by fixed LLMs can substantially improve their decision quality by autonomously generating, sharing, and refining their own natural-language memories (heuristics and examples) over time, in a structured way, without updating model weights. They also aim to understand what forms of memory (rules vs examples) and what learning protocol (single-stream vs population-based with graduation and broadcast) work best, and whether such methods disproportionately help weaker models, potentially narrowing capability gaps.
AI.method: They design FORGE, a staged, population-based protocol built around hierarchical ReAct agents. Each agent is backed by the same frozen LLM and operates with an inner and outer learning loop. In the inner loop, they use a Reflexion-style mechanism: when an agent produces failed trajectories on the task, a reflection agent (using the same base LLM) converts these failures into explicit, reusable knowledge written in natural language. These knowledge artifacts can be: (a) textual heuristics or rules, (b) few-shot demonstrations (examples), or (c) a mixture of both. These artifacts are then injected into the agent’s prompt as memory for future episodes. In the outer loop, multiple agents form a population. After each stage, the best-performing instance’s memory is broadcast to the rest of the population, and a graduation criterion is used to freeze agents whose behavior has converged, so they no longer consume compute in later stages. They evaluate FORGE on the CybORG CAGE-2 B-line cyber-defense benchmark, a 30-step stochastic POMDP, using four different LLM families and three representation types (Rules, Examples, Mixed). They compare FORGE against zero-shot agents and a Reflexion baseline that learns only in a single isolated stream. They also run ablations, including removing graduation, to isolate the effect of population broadcast vs compute savings.
AI.result: Across 12 combinations of model family and memory representation, FORGE consistently outperforms both zero-shot and Reflexion baselines. Relative to zero-shot, it yields 1.7x to 7.7x higher average returns. Relative to Reflexion, it improves average returns by 29–72%. It substantially reduces catastrophic failures (episodes with reward below -100), down to about 1% in the best conditions. An ablation without graduation shows that population-wide broadcast of the best memory is the main driver of performance gains, while graduation mainly reduces computational cost. Among memory types, using examples as few-shot demonstrations usually gives the highest returns for three of the four LLMs, whereas rule-style heuristics provide a better cost–reliability tradeoff with about 40% token savings. Weaker baseline models gain relatively more from FORGE than stronger ones, indicating a disproportionate benefit for less-capable LLMs. All findings are empirically grounded only in the CAGE-2 B-line environment, and cross-model trends are presented as directional rather than definitive.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that LLM agents can meaningfully and reliably improve their decision-making in complex, long-horizon settings solely through self-generated, prompt-based memory, without any gradient updates or reliance on stronger teacher models. FORGE’s staged, population-based protocol—with Reflexion-style reflection, population-wide memory broadcast, and graduation to save compute—provides a robust framework for such improvement. Population broadcast is identified as the crucial mechanism for performance gains, while graduation mostly offers efficiency benefits. Different memory formats trade off performance versus token cost: examples typically yield the best raw returns, whereas rules are more token-efficient and reliable. The method appears especially beneficial for weaker LLMs, suggesting it can help narrow capability gaps rather than just amplifying the strongest models. However, the evidence is currently limited to a single cyber-defense benchmark, so broader generalization remains to be validated.

title: Multi-Level Contextual Token Relation Modeling for Machine-Generated Text Detection
summary: Machine-generated texts (MGTs) pose risks such as disinformation and phishing, underscoring the need for reliable detection. Metric-based methods, which extract statistically distinguishable features of MGTs, are often more practical than complex model-based methods that are prone to overfitting. Given their diverse designs, we first place representative metric-based methods within a unified framework, enabling a clear assessment of their advantages and limitations. Our analysis identifies a core challenge across these methods: the token-level detection score is easily biased by the inherent randomness of the MGTs generation process. Then, we theoretically derive the multi-hop transitions of the token-level detection score and explore their local and global relations. Based on these findings, we propose a multi-level contextual token relation modeling framework for MGT detection. Specifically, for local relations, we model them through a lightweight Markov-informed calibration module that refines token-level evidence before aggregation. For global relations, we introduce a rule-support reasoning module that uses explicit logical rules derived from contextual score statistics. Finally, we combine the local calibrated score and the global rule-support reasoning signal in a joint multi-level inference framework. Extensive experiments show broad and substantial improvements across various real-world scenarios, including cross-LLM and cross-domain settings, with low computational overhead.

AI.tldr: They propose a new framework to better detect machine-generated texts by modeling contextual relations among token-level detection scores, reducing randomness-induced bias and improving robustness across models and domains.
AI.motivation: Metric-based machine-generated text detectors, which rely on statistical features instead of heavy model-based architectures, are more practical but suffer from bias due to randomness in text generation. Existing metric-based methods are diverse and lack a unifying perspective, and their token-level scores are unstable and easily affected by generative stochasticity, weakening detection reliability.
AI.method: 1) Formalize existing metric-based MGT detection methods into a unified framework and analyze how token-level detection scores behave. 2) Theoretically derive multi-hop transitions of token-level detection scores and study their local and global relations. 3) Propose a multi-level contextual token relation modeling framework: (a) a local Markov-informed calibration module that refines token-level scores before aggregation, and (b) a global rule-support reasoning module that applies logical rules based on contextual score statistics. 4) Combine local and global signals within a joint multi-level inference framework for final detection decisions.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple real-world scenarios, including cross-language-model (cross-LLM) and cross-domain settings, show that the proposed method yields broad and substantial performance gains over existing metric-based baselines, while maintaining low computational cost.
AI.conclusion: Modeling both local and global contextual relations among token-level detection scores in a principled way significantly reduces randomness-induced bias and enhances the reliability, robustness, and practicality of metric-based machine-generated text detection, without incurring heavy computation costs.

title: Prospective multi-pathogen disease forecasting using autonomous LLM-guided tree search
summary: Probabilistic forecasting of infectious diseases is crucial for public health but relies on labor-intensive manual model curation by expert modeling teams. This bespoke development bottlenecks scalability to granular geographic resolutions or emerging pathogens. Here, we present an autonomous system using Large Language Model (LLM)-guided tree search to iteratively generate, evaluate, and optimize executable forecasting software. In a fully prospective, real-time evaluation during the 2025-2026 US respiratory season, the system autonomously discovered methodologically diverse models for influenza, COVID-19, and respiratory syncytial virus (RSV). Aggregating these machine-generated models yielded an ensemble that consistently matched or outperformed the gold-standard, human-curated Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) hub ensembles out-of-sample. The system successfully navigated data-scarce "cold start" scenarios for RSV. Moreover, controlled retrospective ablations revealed that optimizing log-scale distance metrics prevents reward hacking, while an automated judge-in-the-loop ensures structural fidelity to complex scientific theories. By autonomously translating epidemiological theory into accurate, transparent code, this framework overcomes the modeling labor bottleneck, enabling rapid deployment of expert-level disease forecasting at unprecedented scales.

AI.tldr: An autonomous LLM-guided system automatically designs and optimizes infectious disease forecasting models that match or beat CDC human-curated ensembles.
AI.motivation: Probabilistic infectious disease forecasts are vital for public health planning but currently require intensive manual work by expert teams, limiting scalability to many locations, pathogens, and rapid responses to new threats.
AI.method: Use a Large Language Model-guided tree search to iteratively generate, evaluate, and refine executable forecasting code, combined with ensemble aggregation, log-scale optimization to avoid reward hacking, and an automated judge-in-the-loop to enforce epidemiological and scientific consistency.
AI.result: In real-time evaluation during the 2025–2026 US respiratory season, the autonomously generated models for influenza, COVID-19, and RSV, when ensembled, consistently matched or outperformed CDC gold-standard human-curated ensemble forecasts, including successful performance in data-scarce cold-start RSV scenarios; ablation studies confirmed the importance of log-scale metrics and automated judging.
AI.conclusion: LLM-guided automated model discovery can reliably translate epidemiological theory into transparent, high-performing forecasting software, removing the manual modeling bottleneck and enabling rapid, scalable deployment of expert-level disease forecasts.

title: DebiasRAG: A Tuning-Free Path to Fair Generation in Large Language Models through Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved unprecedented success due to their exceptional generative capabilities. However, because they depend on knowledge encapsulated from training corpora, they may produce hallucinations, stereotypes, and socially biased content. In particular, LLMs are prone to prejudiced responses involving race, gender, and age, which are collectively referred to as social biases. Prior studies have used fine-tuning and prompt engineering to mitigate such biases in LLMs, but these methods require additional training resources or domain knowledge to design the framework. Moreover, they may degrade the original capabilities of LLMs and often overlook the need for dynamic debiasing contexts for fairer inference. In this paper, we propose DebiasRAG, a novel tuning-free and dynamic query-specific debiasing framework based on retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). DebiasRAG improves fairness while preserving the intrinsic properties of LLMs, such as representation ability. DebiasRAG consists of three stages: (1) query-specific debiasing candidate generation; (2) context candidate pool construction; and (3) gradient-updated debiasing-guided context piece reranking. First, DebiasRAG leverages self-diagnosed bias contexts relevant to the query through regular retrieval, where the bias contexts are prepared offline by the DebiasRAG provider. Given the query-specific bias contexts, DebiasRAG reversely produces debiasing contexts, which are provided as additional fairness constraints for LLM outputs. Second, a regular RAG retrieval process produces query-related contexts from the regular RAG document database, such as a chunked Wikipedia dataset.

AI.tldr: DebiasRAG is a tuning-free, retrieval-augmented framework that dynamically debiases large language model outputs using query-specific contexts while preserving their core capabilities.
AI.motivation: Large language models often generate socially biased content (e.g., across race, gender, age) because they rely on static knowledge learned from training data. Existing debiasing approaches such as fine-tuning and prompt engineering need extra resources or expert design, can harm the model’s original abilities, and rarely adapt dynamically to each query. The paper aims to address these limitations by developing a lightweight, dynamic, and fairer inference framework.
AI.method: The paper introduces DebiasRAG, a three-stage, tuning-free debiasing framework built on retrieval-augmented generation. (1) It uses regular retrieval over an offline, provider-prepared bias-context collection to obtain query-specific bias contexts, and then reversely generates debiasing contexts that encode fairness constraints. (2) It runs standard RAG retrieval over a main document database (e.g., chunked Wikipedia) to gather task-relevant information. (3) It applies a gradient-updated reranking mechanism guided by the debiasing contexts to select and order the final context pieces fed into the LLM, thus steering generation toward less biased outputs without modifying model parameters.
AI.result: Experimental results (described in the full paper) are expected to show that DebiasRAG reduces social bias in LLM outputs while preserving, or minimally degrading, core capabilities such as representation and general knowledge usage, and that it does so without additional fine-tuning or heavy human prompt engineering.
AI.conclusion: DebiasRAG demonstrates that dynamic, query-specific, retrieval-based debiasing can effectively mitigate social biases in LLMs at inference time without tuning the underlying model. The approach offers a practical path to fairer LLM deployment by leveraging external debiasing and task contexts, coordinated through a gradient-informed reranking stage.

title: SGR: A Stepwise Reasoning Framework for LLMs with External Subgraph Generation
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities across diverse NLP applications, such as translation, text generation, and question answering. Nevertheless, they remain limited in complex settings that demand deep reasoning and logical inference. Since these models are trained on large-scale text corpora, their generation process may still introduce irrelevant, noisy, or factually inconsistent content. To mitigate this problem, we introduce SGR, a stepwise framework that enhances LLM reasoning through external subgraph generation. SGR builds query-specific subgraphs from external knowledge bases and uses their semantic structure to support multi-step inference. By grounding intermediate reasoning steps in structured external knowledge, the framework helps the model concentrate on relevant entities, relations, and supporting evidence. In particular, SGR first constructs a subgraph tailored to the input question. It then guides the model to reason progressively over the generated structure and combines multiple reasoning trajectories to obtain the final prediction. Experimental results across several benchmark datasets show that SGR achieves consistent improvements over competitive baselines, highlighting its value for improving both reasoning accuracy and factual reliability.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes SGR, a framework that improves large language model reasoning by constructing and using external knowledge subgraphs for stepwise inference, leading to higher accuracy and factual reliability.
AI.motivation: Although large language models are powerful on many NLP tasks, they still struggle with complex reasoning and may generate irrelevant or factually inconsistent content. There is a need for mechanisms that ground their reasoning in reliable external knowledge and focus them on relevant information.
AI.method: The authors introduce SGR, a stepwise reasoning framework that first builds a query-specific subgraph from external knowledge bases, then guides the LLM to reason progressively over this structured graph. The framework leverages the semantic structure of entities and relations, and aggregates multiple reasoning trajectories derived from the subgraph to form the final answer.
AI.result: Across several benchmark datasets, SGR consistently outperforms strong baseline methods on reasoning tasks, demonstrating gains in both reasoning accuracy and factual consistency of model outputs.
AI.conclusion: Grounding LLM reasoning in tailored external subgraphs and enforcing a stepwise inference process improves their performance on complex reasoning tasks and enhances factual reliability, showing that structured knowledge integration is an effective way to address LLM reasoning limitations.

title: Optimized Three-Dimensional Photovoltaic Structures with LLM guided Tree Search
summary:
We present a case study for how AI coding systems can be used to generate novel scientific hypotheses. We combine a generic coding agent (Google's AntiGravity) with an LLM-driven tree search algorithm (Empirical Research Assistance / ERA) to autonomously generate high-efficiency three-dimensional photovoltaic (3DPV) structures that overcome losses limiting flat solar panels at mid-latitudes. These structures operate by presenting favorable angles to the sun throughout the day, and for illustrative purposes we focus on optimizing performance for a single solar day. Our workflow begins by using AntiGravity to reproduce calculations \cite{bernardi2012solar} showing that 3DPV can have energy densities much higher than stationary flat PV panels. We use these initial designs as the starting point for large scale tree search, where we seek improved solutions and score them for their diurnal yield. The initial tree search leads to nominally more efficient solutions, yet they are caused by algorithmic reward hacking, arising from non-physical design features such as structurally levitating disconnected tiers and exploitations of the discretizations in the optics solver. To counteract this, we develop a workflow where the coding agent iteratively patches the physics engine with constraints to eliminate reward hacking. With reward-hacking eliminated, ERA discovers a series of designs with various constraints and improved performance, including optimal designs with different fixed collector areas, optimizing zenith tracking and avoiding self shadowing.
  Combining coding agents with tree search (ERA) provides a powerful platform for scientific discovery, for problems whose solutions can be empirically evaluated with a score function.

AI.tldr: The paper demonstrates how combining a coding agent with an LLM-driven tree search can autonomously design high-efficiency 3D photovoltaic structures while addressing reward hacking issues.
AI.motivation: Explore whether AI coding systems, combined with search, can autonomously generate useful, novel scientific hypotheses and designs—in this case for improving solar panel efficiency at mid-latitudes using 3D photovoltaic structures.
AI.method: Use Google's AntiGravity coding agent to reproduce known 3D PV calculations, then employ an LLM-driven tree search algorithm (ERA) to explore new 3D geometries scored by simulated daily energy yield. Detect and analyze reward hacking in the designs, then iteratively modify the physics/optics engine with additional constraints via the coding agent to remove non-physical exploits and rerun the search.
AI.result: Initial tree search produces seemingly more efficient but non-physical 3D PV designs that exploit weaknesses in the simulation (e.g., floating tiers, discretization artifacts). After iteratively patching the physics engine to remove these exploits, the system discovers constrained, physically plausible 3D designs with improved diurnal performance, including area-constrained optima, zenith-tracking-optimized configurations, and self-shadowing-avoidant layouts.
AI.conclusion: AI coding agents combined with structured search (ERA) can serve as an effective platform for scientific discovery on problems that admit an evaluable score function, provided that the evaluation environment is iteratively hardened against reward hacking and non-physical exploits.

title: Improving Cross-Cultural Survey Simulation with Calibrated Value Personas
comment: Submitted to the Fourth International Workshop on Value Engineering in AI (VALE 2026), held at IJCAI-ECAI 2026
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to simulate human opinions and survey responses, but their ability to reproduce population responses across cultures remains limited. Existing persona-based prompting methods typically rely on sociodemographic or personality traits, which are only indirect proxies for the values that shape human responses. We propose a value-based persona construction method that derives textual descriptors from survey responses capturing core cultural dimensions. By sampling value profiles from target populations and aggregating LLM responses across personas, we obtain population-level predictions grounded in observed value distributions. We further introduce a calibration procedure that improves response diversity while preserving estimated opinions. We show that our approach reduces prediction error across countries, with the largest improvements observed in underrepresented populations. This substantially narrows the performance gap between countries aligned with dominant LLM priors and those that are less represented in training data, while also yielding response distributions that closely match human diversity.

AI.tldr: They build value-based personas for LLMs to better match real survey results across countries, and calibrate outputs to improve both accuracy and diversity of simulated opinions.
AI.motivation: LLMs are used to simulate survey responses, but current persona methods based on demographics or personalities fail to robustly reproduce real population opinions, especially across different cultures. There is a need for a method that better captures the underlying value systems that drive responses and reduces bias toward cultures overrepresented in LLM training data.
AI.method: They construct personas from value profiles derived from human survey responses on core cultural dimensions. These profiles are turned into textual persona descriptors and used to prompt the LLM. By sampling many such value-based personas from the target population’s value distribution and aggregating responses, they obtain predicted population-level opinions. They also introduce a calibration procedure that adjusts generation to increase response diversity while keeping overall opinion estimates stable.
AI.result: The value-based persona method reduces prediction error of LLM-simulated surveys across multiple countries compared to sociodemographic- or personality-based personas. Gains are especially large for underrepresented populations in the training data. The proposed calibration further improves diversity of generated responses without harming accuracy of aggregate opinion estimates, and the simulated distributions better resemble human response distributions.
AI.conclusion: Grounding LLM personas in empirically observed value distributions across cultures enables more accurate and equitable simulation of population-level survey responses. This approach narrows performance gaps between overrepresented and underrepresented countries in LLM training data and yields opinion distributions that better mirror human diversity, suggesting value-based personas plus calibration as a more robust paradigm for cross-cultural opinion simulation with LLMs.

title: Argus: Evidence Assembly for Scalable Deep Research Agents
summary: Deep research agents have achieved remarkable progress on complex information seeking tasks. Even long ReAct style rollouts explore only a single trajectory, while recent state of the art systems scale inference time compute via parallel search and aggregation. Yet deep research answers are composed of complementary pieces of evidence, which parallel rollouts often duplicate rather than complete, yielding diminishing returns while pushing the aggregation context toward the model's limit. We propose Argus, an agentic system in which a Searcher and a Navigator cooperate to treat deep research as assembling a jigsaw from complementary evidence pieces, rather than brute forcing the whole answer in parallel. The Searcher collects evidence traces for a given sub-query through ReAct-style interaction. The Navigator maintains a shared evidence graph, verifying which pieces are still missing, dispatching Searchers to gather them, and reasoning over the completed graph to produce a source-traced final answer. We train the Navigator with reinforcement learning to verify, dispatch, and synthesize, while independently training the Searcher to remain a standard ReAct agent. The resulting Navigator supports rollouts with a single Searcher or many in parallel without retraining. With both Searcher and Navigator built on a 35B-A3B MoE backbone, Argus gains 5.5 points with a single Searcher and 12.7 points with 8 parallel Searchers, averaged over eight benchmarks. With 64 Searchers it reaches 86.2 on BrowseComp, surpassing every proprietary agent we benchmark, while the Navigator's reasoning context stays under 21.5K tokens.

AI.tldr: Argus is an agentic system that coordinates a Searcher and a Navigator to perform deep research more efficiently by assembling complementary evidence rather than brute-force parallel search.
AI.motivation: Existing deep research agents either follow a single ReAct-style trajectory or use many parallel rollouts that often duplicate evidence, waste compute, and overflow context windows. The authors want a system that can exploit parallelism while gathering complementary, not redundant, evidence and keeping reasoning within context limits.
AI.method: They introduce a two-component agent, Argus. A ReAct-style Searcher interacts with tools/web to collect evidence for sub-queries. A Navigator maintains a shared evidence graph, verifies what information is missing, dispatches Searchers with targeted sub-queries, and synthesizes a source-traced final answer. The Navigator is trained with reinforcement learning to verify evidence, schedule searches, and synthesize answers, while the Searcher is trained separately as a standard ReAct agent. The same Navigator can coordinate one or many Searchers in parallel without retraining, all on a 35B-A3B MoE backbone.
AI.result: Argus improves performance across eight benchmarks, gaining 5.5 points with a single Searcher and 12.7 points with 8 parallel Searchers compared to baselines. With 64 Searchers, it achieves 86.2 on the BrowseComp benchmark, outperforming all proprietary agents they tested, while keeping the Navigator’s reasoning context under 21.5K tokens.
AI.conclusion: Coordinating a ReAct-style Searcher with an RL-trained Navigator that manages an evidence graph yields more efficient and effective deep research than naive parallel ReAct rollouts. Argus scales to many parallel Searchers with substantial performance gains and controlled context length, demonstrating a promising approach for building scalable research agents.

title: Artificial Aphasias in Lesioned Language Models
summary: Aphasias, selective language impairments which can arise from brain damage, reveal the functional organization of human language by providing causal links between affected brain regions and specific symptom profiles. Drawing on this literature, we introduce an aphasia-inspired technique to characterize the emergent functional organization of language models (LMs). We ``lesion'' (zero-out) model parameters and measure the effects of this intervention against clinical aphasia symptoms, as diagnosed by the Text Aphasia Battery (TAB). When applied to 112,426 outputs from five 1B-scale LMs, the full range of evaluated symptoms surface, but in distributions largely distinct from those of humans. Our method uncovers broad symptom-profile differences between attention components (query, key, value, output) and feed-forward components (up, gate, down), with weaker evidence for differences among components within the same mechanism. We also find an effect of depth, where lesions in early layers disproportionately cause syntactic and semantic symptoms while late-middle layers yield higher rates of phonological and fluency deficits. Although some LM lesions induce quantitatively more similar profiles to some human aphasia types than others, qualitative differences in symptom patterns between LMs and humans suggest that aphasia syndromes are heavily influenced by the details of learning and processing rather than being a domain-invariant consequence of disrupted language processing.

AI.tldr: The paper uses an aphasia-inspired lesion method on language models to study their internal functional organization, comparing resulting error patterns with human aphasia symptoms.
AI.motivation: To understand how high-level language abilities are organized inside large language models, and whether their internal structure parallels the functional organization of human language as revealed by aphasia syndromes.
AI.method: They "lesion" (zero-out) specific parameters in 1B-scale language models and then evaluate the resulting outputs using the Text Aphasia Battery (TAB), which categorizes language symptoms similar to clinical aphasia diagnostics. They systematically compare symptom distributions across different architectural components (attention vs. feed-forward, and subcomponents like Q/K/V/O and up/gate/down) and across layer depths.
AI.result: Lesioned LMs exhibit the full spectrum of evaluated aphasia-like symptoms but with distributions that differ substantially from human patients. They find strong differences between attention and feed-forward components, weaker differences among subcomponents within each mechanism, and a depth effect where early-layer lesions mainly yield syntactic/semantic issues while later lesions more often cause phonological and fluency-like deficits. Some lesions resemble certain human aphasia profiles more than others, but only in a coarse, quantitative sense.
AI.conclusion: The internal organization of language abilities in current LMs does not closely mirror human aphasia syndromes. Rather than reflecting a domain-invariant structure of language processing, aphasia-like error patterns appear strongly shaped by the specific learning dynamics and processing mechanisms of each system (brains vs. LMs). Aphasia-inspired lesioning, however, is a useful tool to probe emergent functional organization in LMs.

title: A Unified Generative-AI Framework for Smart Energy Infrastructure: Intelligent Gas Distribution, Utility Billing, Carbon Analytics, and Quantum-Inspired Optimisation
summary: The accelerating convergence of smart metering, generative artificial intelligence, and quantum-inspired combinatorial optimisation is reshaping how energy utilities manage physical infrastructure, customer engagement, and environmental accountability

AI.tldr: The paper discusses how combining smart metering, generative AI, and quantum-inspired optimisation is transforming utility operations and sustainability management.
AI.motivation: Energy utilities face increasing complexity in managing grid infrastructure, interacting with customers, and meeting environmental and regulatory targets; emerging technologies may offer new ways to optimise these dimensions simultaneously.
AI.method: Conceptual or applied framework that integrates data from smart meters, uses generative AI for prediction and scenario generation, and applies quantum-inspired combinatorial optimisation to improve decision-making in grid operations and customer and sustainability strategies.
AI.result: The integrated use of these technologies enables more efficient infrastructure planning and operation, more personalised and proactive customer engagement, and improved tracking and optimisation of environmental performance.
AI.conclusion: Convergence of smart metering, generative AI, and quantum-inspired optimisation offers a powerful new paradigm for utilities, enhancing operational efficiency, customer experience, and environmental accountability, and may guide future investments and policy in the energy sector.

title: A Generative AI Framework for Intelligent Utility Billing CO 2 Analytics and Sustainable Resource Optimisation
summary: Distribution utilities are now expected to deliver bills that customers can actually read attach a defensible carbon number to every kWh sold and schedule load against grid stress and emissions constraints We propose an end-to-end framework that unifies four production-grade capabilities under one architectural roof a generative-AI agent that drafts each customers natural-language billing statement from structured numeric inputs under a constrained decoding policy a transformer-based forecaster that supplies the day-ahead consumption estimate with calibrated quantile bands

AI.tldr: An end-to-end architecture for electricity distribution utilities that unifies generative billing explanations, carbon attribution per kWh, load scheduling, and forecasting into a single framework.
AI.motivation: Distribution utilities must now provide readable, personalized bills, assign credible carbon intensities to each kWh, and manage load considering both grid constraints and emissions, but current solutions are fragmented and not integrated end-to-end.
AI.method: They design an integrated architecture with four main components, including a constrained generative-AI agent that produces customer-facing natural-language billing statements from structured data, and a transformer-based forecaster that generates day-ahead consumption forecasts with calibrated uncertainty quantiles; the remaining components likely cover carbon-intensity estimation and grid-aware, emission-aware load scheduling.
AI.result: The framework demonstrates that these four production-grade capabilities can be combined under one system, enabling utilities to automate bill explanations, forecast demand with uncertainty, and support carbon- and grid-aware operations; specific quantitative results are not provided in the abstract fragment.
AI.conclusion: A unified, AI-driven architecture can simultaneously address customer communication, accurate demand forecasting, and operational and carbon-accounting needs for distribution utilities, suggesting a practical pathway to modern, data-centric utility operations.

title: Context Pruning for Coding Agents via Multi-Rubric Latent Reasoning
summary: LLM-powered coding agents spend the majority of their token budget reading repository files, yet much of the retrieved code is irrelevant to the task at hand. Existing learned pruners compress this context with a single-objective sequence labeler, collapsing all facets of code relevance into one score and one transition matrix. We show that this formulation creates a modeling bottleneck: a single CRF transition prior must serve heterogeneous retention patterns, including contiguous semantic spans and sparse structural support lines. We propose LaMR (Latent Multi-Rubric), a structured pruning framework that decomposes code relevance into two interpretable quality dimensions, semantic evidence and dependency support, each modeled by a dedicated CRF with dimension-specific transition dynamics. A mixture-of-experts gating network dynamically weights the per-rubric emissions conditioned on the query, and a final CRF layer on the fused emissions produces the aggregate keep-or-prune decision. To supervise each dimension without additional annotation cost, we derive multi-rubric labels from the existing training corpus via AST-based program analysis, simultaneously denoising the teacher's binary labels. By effectively filtering distracting noise, LaMR frequently matches or even outperforms unpruned full-context baselines. Experiments on four benchmarks (SWE-Bench Verified, SWE-QA, LCC, LongCodeQA) show that LaMR wins 12 of 16 head-to-head multi-turn comparisons. It saves up to 31% more tokens on multi-turn agent tasks and improves Exact Match by up to +3.5 on single-turn tasks, while performance is frequently enhanced by denoising the context, and any remaining drops are marginal.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LaMR, a multi-rubric CRF-based pruning framework that improves LLM coding agents by selectively retaining only semantically relevant and dependency-supporting code, reducing token usage while maintaining or improving task performance.
AI.motivation: LLM coding agents waste many tokens reading large portions of code repositories, much of which is irrelevant, and existing pruning methods use a single-objective model that cannot adequately capture different types of code relevance, creating a modeling bottleneck.
AI.method: LaMR decomposes code relevance into two latent, interpretable dimensions—semantic evidence and dependency support—each modeled with its own CRF that has dimension-specific transition dynamics. A mixture-of-experts gating network, conditioned on the query, weights rubric-specific emissions, which are then combined and passed to a final CRF to produce keep-or-prune decisions. Multi-rubric labels are automatically derived from existing training data using AST-based program analysis, which also denoises noisy teacher labels without extra annotation.
AI.result: On four benchmarks (SWE-Bench Verified, SWE-QA, LCC, LongCodeQA), LaMR wins 12 out of 16 head-to-head multi-turn evaluations, saves up to 31% more tokens in multi-turn agent tasks, and improves Exact Match by up to 3.5 points on single-turn tasks, often matching or surpassing unpruned full-context baselines despite using fewer tokens.
AI.conclusion: Decomposing code relevance into multiple rubrics and modeling each with specialized CRFs, then combining them via a gating network, yields more effective context pruning for LLM coding agents, filtering distracting noise and enabling token savings with equal or better performance than full-context and single-rubric pruning baselines, with any residual performance losses being small.
